sources.summary: The paper uses SDSS J1253 + 6817 follow-up COS spectra and finds extended shallow transmission plus three contiguous low-optical-depth bins, indicating large sightline variance.

quotes.quote: In Cycle 18 Program 12249 (PI Zheng) follow-up HST /COS spectroscopy was obtained for four z_ em >3.4 quasars previously verified to show flux at He 2 Ly : SDSS J2346 0016 zheng04b , SDSS J1711 + 6052 zheng05 , SDSS J1319 + 5202 syphers09a and SDSS J1253 + 6817 syphers09b . The COS spectra of SDSS J2346 0016 and SDSS J1253 + 6817 have been presented in syphers11 , whereas we also discuss the two faint higher-redshift quasars taken in Program 12249. All quasars were observed with the COS G140L grating in the 1105 \ setup, while three (HS 0911 + 4809, SDSS J2346 0016 and SDSS J1253 + 6817) have additional coverage at <1100 , obtained in the 1280 \ setup.

Unexpectedly, two sightlines show longer ( z 0.1 ) shallow transmission regions: SDSS J1253 + 6817 at z 3.1 and SDSS J1711 + 6052 at z 3.15 and z 3.39 . Extensive tests with modified background subtraction windows, background smoothing lengths and gain sag corrections confirmed that these shallow transmission regions are not due to local systematic background subtraction errors.

At z=3.16 , 3/5 sightlines have _ eff,HeII 3 (SDSS J1253 + 6817, SDSS J1711 + 6052, HS 0911 + 4809), very similar to values obtained at lower redshifts. In SDSS J1253 + 6817 these low effective optical depths are measured in 3 contiguous redshift bins ( 3.06<z<3.18 ), indicating large-scale variance between the sightlines.

gmw_00012891.name: SDSS J2346 0016
gmw_00012891.overview: SDSS J2346 0016 is a quasar [arXiv:1405.7405]. Its sightline exhibits highly saturated helium II absorption at redshift z ~ 3.4, showing the highest helium II effective optical depths among compared sightlines [arXiv:1405.7405].

sources.summary: The paper finds SDSS J2346 0016 has the highest He II effective optical depths, remains highly saturated at z~3.4, and shows only weak positive flux in comparison windows.

quotes.quote: Again we see a strong sightline-to-sightline variance, with the highest effective optical depths measured toward SDSS J2346 0016, whereas the absorption in two other sightlines remains low (SDSS J1319 + 5202 and SDSS J1711 + 6052).

At z 3.4 the sightline toward SDSS J2346 0016 is highly saturated, implying x _ HeII 0.015 on 20 of the covered pathlength, whereas the other three contributing sightlines show _ eff,HeII 3 , implying x _ HeII 0.004 on 40 of the pathlength.

This is in sharp contrast with the SDSS J2346 0016 sightline that remains highly saturated ( _ eff,HeII 5 ) at all levels of H 1 absorption. In comparison, the same redshift ranges toward SDSS J2346 0016 show positive flux only at the 3.2 level, yielding significantly higher but finite He 2 effective optical depths ( _ eff,HeII =5.06^ +0.34 _ 0.26 and _ eff,HeII =4.96^ +0.35 _ 0.27 ). The somewhat stronger H 1 forest absorption toward SDSS J2346 0016 can only partly account for the difference.

gmw_00012892.name: SDSS J1711 + 6052
gmw_00012892.overview: SDSS J1711 + 6052 is a high-redshift quasar [arXiv:1405.7405]. Its sightline exhibits extended, shallow helium II transmission regions [arXiv:1405.7405] and robustly low effective optical depths that indicate unexpectedly high levels of ionization [arXiv:1405.7405].

sources.summary: The paper reports extended shallow transmission toward SDSS J1711 + 6052, robust low effective optical depths among the highest-redshift sightlines, and pulse-height-verified large-scale He II transmission.

quotes.quote: Unexpectedly, two sightlines show longer ( z 0.1 ) shallow transmission regions: SDSS J1253 + 6817 at z 3.1 and SDSS J1711 + 6052 at z 3.15 and z 3.39 . Extensive tests with modified background subtraction windows, background smoothing lengths and gain sag corrections confirmed that these shallow transmission regions are not due to local systematic background subtraction errors.

The majority (60 ) of the _ eff,HeII measurements at z>3.34 are from the two highest-redshift sightlines in our sample (SDSS J1711 + 6052 and SDSS J1319 + 5202), which also contribute the four lowest _ eff,HeII 3 values (Table tab:he2tau ). These low effective optical depths indicate surprisingly high levels of ionization, in contrast to current models. Improved background subtraction and accounting for possible remaining systematics indicates that these low _ eff,HeII values are robust.

The sightline to SDSS J1711 + 6052 shows large-scale ( 20 proper Mpc) low-level He 2 transmission that we have verified with pulse height statistics (Fig. fig:j1711pha ). The two redshift windows 3.110<z<3.246 and 3.340<z<3.440 show positive flux at 13 significance.

gmw_00012893.name: GRB host galaxy at z=1.1
gmw_00012893.overview: The GRB host galaxy at z=1.1 is a faint galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.1 [arXiv:1012.0328]. It has been detected in the g' and ' bands, though its stellar mass remains unconstrained by optical data [arXiv:1012.0328].

sources.summary: The paper treats the GRB 080413B host as a faint z=1.1 galaxy contributing constant late-time flux, detected in g' and ' bands, with stellar mass unconstrained by the GROND optical data.

quotes.quote: The combined optical/X-ray spectral energy distributions were fitted with power-law and broken power-law models and two absorbing columns: one Galactic foreground with a hydrogen column of N_H = 3.1 10^ 20  cm ^ 2 (Kalberla et al. ) and another one that is local to the GRB host galaxy at z=1.1 . Only the latter was allowed to vary in the fits.
The flattening at the end ( > T_0 + 1  Ms) of the light curve suggests a faint host galaxy.

Segments d and e are fully dominated by the second component with segments e and f showing the rising influence of the constant flux, which we interpret as the host galaxy. This host galaxy was detected in the g' and ' bands, but the stellar mass is not constrained by the optical identification obtained by GROND because observations probe the rest-frame wavelengths below the 4000 break, where the mass-to-light ratio can vary by a factor of more than 100.

gmw_00012894.name: northwest source
gmw_00012894.overview: The northwest source is a point-like source located far beyond the belt of its system [arXiv:2303.13584]. It is likely an unresolved background submillimetre galaxy that is only detected at long wavelengths [arXiv:2303.13584].

sources.summary: The paper says the northwest source is far beyond the belt, coincides with low-SNR AzTEC/SCUBA-2 peaks, and is likely an unresolved background galaxy.

quotes.quote: The point source to the northwest is far beyond the belt and so is unlikely to be related to the system. It appears at the same right ascension and declination as peaks in the AzTEC and SCUBA-2 data, although these are both detected at a SNR <3 .

We also conclude that both the northwest and southeast sources are most likely to be galaxies given their point like nature -- submillimetre galaxies are typically observed to have sizes <1 e.g. ikarashi15,fujimoto16,gomez22 and so will be unresolved in our observations. This conclusion is also consistent with the fact that both sources are only detected at long wavelengths.

gmw_00012895.name: southeast source
gmw_00012895.overview: The southeast source is an unresolved point source that is likely a background galaxy [arXiv:2303.13584]. It is coincident with a belt structure and is consistent with a bright point detected in submillimetre observations [arXiv:2303.13584].

sources.summary: The paper says the southeast source lies against the belt, matches prior submillimetre features within positional uncertainties, lacks common proper motion, and is likely an unresolved background galaxy.

quotes.quote: The point source to the southeast is coincident with the belt and so it is harder to distinguish from the belt in the prior observations due to the low resolution of those observations. The SPIRE and AzTEC data both show a brightness asymmetry that is brightest at this point. There is a bright ( 7 ) point in the SCUBA-2 data that is offset from the southeast source by 2.5 in both right ascension and declination.

This means that we expect the positional uncertainty of the SCUBA-2 bright point to be 2.4 and so it is consistent with the location of the southeast source. From this we conclude that the southeast source does not share a common proper motion with the star and so must also be a background source. We also conclude that both the northwest and southeast sources are most likely to be galaxies given their point like nature -- submillimetre galaxies are typically observed to have sizes <1 e.g. ikarashi15,fujimoto16,gomez22 and so will be unresolved in our observations.

gmw_00012896.name: FAQS 151113.7+490557
gmw_00012896.overview: FAQS 151113.7+490557 is a broad absorption line quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/9911284]. The object is classified as radio-loud, exhibiting a radio-to-optical flux ratio, log R, of approximately 1.1 [arXiv:astro-ph/9911284].

sources.summary: The paper identifies FAQS 151113.7+490557 as one of two broad absorption line QSOs found in the FAQS subsample and describes the pair as radio-loud, with this source having log R about 1.1.

quotes.quote: Amongst the 33 QSOs, we have found two broad absorption line (BAL) QSOs, namely, FAQS 151113.7+490557 and FAQS 161744.4+515054. These two BAL QSOs are radio-loud, and have radio-to-optical flux ratios ( log R ) with values of 1.1 and 2.5 respectively.

Among the 33 QSOs, we have found two broad absorption line (BAL) QSOs, namely, FAQS 151113.7+490557 and FAQS 161744.4+515054. These BAL QSOs are radio-loud, and have a radio-to-optical flux ratios, log R (Weymann et al. 1991) equal 1.1, and 2.5 respectively.

gmw_00012897.name: FAQS 161744.4+515054
gmw_00012897.overview: FAQS 161744.4+515054 is a radio-loud broad absorption line quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/9911284]. It has a radio-to-optical flux ratio of 2.5 and a 1.4 GHz flux density of 99.8 mJy [arXiv:astro-ph/9911284].

sources.summary: The paper identifies FAQS 161744.4+515054 as a radio-loud BAL QSO with log R about 2.5 and a 1.4 GHz flux density of 99.8 mJy, calling it the brightest known radio-loud BAL QSO.

quotes.quote: Amongst the 33 QSOs, we have found two broad absorption line (BAL) QSOs, namely, FAQS 151113.7+490557 and FAQS 161744.4+515054. These two BAL QSOs are radio-loud, and have radio-to-optical flux ratios ( log R ) with values of 1.1 and 2.5 respectively. The last object being the brightest radio-loud BAL QSO known, with a flux density of 99.8 mJy at 1.4 Ghz.

Among the 33 QSOs, we have found two broad absorption line (BAL) QSOs, namely, FAQS 151113.7+490557 and FAQS 161744.4+515054. These BAL QSOs are radio-loud, and have a radio-to-optical flux ratios, log R (Weymann et al. 1991) equal 1.1, and 2.5 respectively. The last object has a flux density of 99.8 mJy at 1.4 GHz, being the brightest radio-loud BAL QSO known so far.

gmw_00012898.name: host of GRB 070714B
gmw_00012898.overview: The host of GRB 070714B is a moderately star-forming galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.923 [arXiv:0802.1346]. Spectroscopy of this host galaxy reveals a single emission line at 7167 Angstroms, which is identified as the [O II] line [arXiv:0802.1346].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the GRB 070714B host as a moderately star-forming z=0.923 galaxy whose 7167 Angstrom line is best explained as redshifted [O II].

quotes.quote: Subsequent observations detected a host galaxy at the location of the optical transient. Here we report on photometry and spectroscopy of the host galaxy, which lead us to conclude that the host is a moderately star-forming galaxy at a redshift of z=0.923.

Spectroscopy of the host of GRB 070714B reveals a single line at 7167 . Our multiband optical and infrared photometry strongly implies that this can only be the 3727 [ O II ] line at a redshift of z=0.923.

gmw_00012899.name: ID=5
gmw_00012899.overview: ID=5 is a galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.38 [arXiv:1510.02506]. It features extended emission, a stellar mass of approximately 10^9.65 solar masses, and a high nebular-to-stellar color excess ratio [arXiv:1510.02506].

sources.summary: The paper compares ID=5 with ID=7: both have extended emission, high S/N, the same redshift and similar stellar mass, while ID=5 has a larger nebular-to-stellar color excess ratio.

quotes.quote: By examining ID=5 and ID=7 , two galaxies with the most extended emissions, highest S/N ratio, exact same redshift and comparable stellar masses, we see very different ionized to continuum color excess ratio. Between the two galaxies the one with the higher sSFR ( ID=7 ) has smaller color excess ratio, consistent with the findings of for higher redshift galaxies but inconsistent with , and the framework depicted by radiative transfer models (e.g. Charlot2000 ).

In the case of this study, if the more inclined galaxy ( ID=7 ) were face-on, it would have had less stellar continuum color excess and therefore larger ratio of ionized to stellar color excess similar to the other galaxy ( ID=5 ).

We specifically compared two of the galaxies in the sample ( ID=5 7 ) with the same redshift ( z=0.38 ) and comparable stellar mass ( Log(M_ * /M_ 9.65 ). Between the two, the one with higher SFR has smaller nebular to stellar color excess ratio.

gmw_00012900.name: ID=7
gmw_00012900.overview: The galaxy ID=7 is located at a redshift of z=0.38 [arXiv:1510.02506]. It has a stellar mass of approximately 10^9.65 solar masses, featuring extended emission, a high specific star formation rate, and a relatively high inclination [arXiv:1510.02506].

sources.summary: The paper compares ID=7 with ID=5: both have extended emission, high S/N, the same redshift and similar stellar mass, but ID=7 has higher sSFR, smaller color-excess ratio, and greater inclination.

quotes.quote: By examining ID=5 and ID=7 , two galaxies with the most extended emissions, highest S/N ratio, exact same redshift and comparable stellar masses, we see very different ionized to continuum color excess ratio. Between the two galaxies the one with the higher sSFR ( ID=7 ) has smaller color excess ratio, consistent with the findings of for higher redshift galaxies but inconsistent with , and the framework depicted by radiative transfer models (e.g. Charlot2000 ).

In the case of this study, if the more inclined galaxy ( ID=7 ) were face-on, it would have had less stellar continuum color excess and therefore larger ratio of ionized to stellar color excess similar to the other galaxy ( ID=5 ).

We specifically compared two of the galaxies in the sample ( ID=5 7 ) with the same redshift ( z=0.38 ) and comparable stellar mass ( Log(M_ * /M_ 9.65 ). Between the two, the one with higher SFR has smaller nebular to stellar color excess ratio.

gmw_00012901.name: J1331+2932
gmw_00012901.overview: J1331+2932 is a confirmed blazar that is the most likely counterpart to a Fermi LAT gamma-ray source [arXiv:1902.07561]. High-resolution radio observations reveal a compact core-jet morphology with a bright core, a weak southwestern jet, and a high brightness temperature indicating relativistic beaming [arXiv:1902.07561].

sources.summary: EVN observations give J1331+2932 a precise VLBI position, compact core-jet morphology, high brightness temperature, and relativistic beaming; the authors confirm it as a blazar and likely Fermi LAT counterpart.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: right ascension 13 ^ h 31 ^ m 01.83259 ^ s and declination 29 ^ 32 ^ 16.5099 ^

quotes.quote: The coordinates of the blazar candidate could be determined more accurately than before: right ascension 13 ^ h 31 ^ m 01.83259 ^ s and declination 29 ^ 32 ^ 16.5099 ^ . The estimated error in the position is 0.5 mas. The 5-GHz EVN image of J1331+2932 using fringe-fitted data is displayed in Fig.  image . The source shows a compact radio structure typical for blazars, a bright core and a weak jet component to the south-west.
This yields that the minimum brightness temperature of J1331+2932 is T_B 1.3 10^ 11  K.

This indicates relativistic beaming which implies that the candidate is indeed a blazar. Using typical values of 5 15 for the Lorentz factor, the jet inclination angle can be estimated using = 1 1- , where is the bulk speed of the jet in units of the speed of light. From this, we obtain 14^ for the jet in J1331+2932. Based on our EVN data, we confirm that J1331+2932 is a blazar and thus the most likely counterpart of the Fermi LAT source.

gmw_00012902.name: J0805 0111
gmw_00012902.overview: The galaxy J0805 0111 is a radio source included in the Candidate Gamma-Ray Blazar Survey [arXiv:0709.1735]. Its NVSS counterpart exhibits a marginally resolved radio jet structure [arXiv:0709.1735].

sources.summary: The paper flags J0805 0111 as one of two CGRaBS sources with probably overestimated spectral index and FoM because NVSS jet structure led to a spurious 1.4 GHz counterpart.

quotes.quote: Sources J0805 0111 and J1639 + 1632 have nominal CGRaBS spectral indices (and thus FoMs) that are almost certainly overestimates. Their NVSS counterparts have marginally resolved jet structure, and the NVSS decompositions offset the core toward the jet. A faint, spurious counter-jet component was introduced and, being slightly closer to the 8.4 GHz position, was selected as the 1.4 GHz counterpart, leading to a highly inverted spectral index and a high FoM. We include these sources in the survey since they satisfy the CGRaBS prescription; a more careful treatment of the NVSS counterparts would give a smaller spectral index and FoM. This effect is quite rare, occurring in CGRaBS for only these 2 sources (out of 1625, or 0.12 ) and in CRATES for no more than 20 sources (out of 11,131, or 0.18 ).

gmw_00012903.name: J1639 + 1632
gmw_00012903.overview: J1639 + 1632 is a radio source included in the Candidate Gamma-Ray Blazar Survey [arXiv:0709.1735]. Its NVSS counterpart exhibits a marginally resolved jet structure, which led to a spurious counterpart selection and an overestimated spectral index [arXiv:0709.1735].

sources.summary: The paper flags J1639 + 1632 as one of two CGRaBS sources with probably overestimated spectral index and FoM because NVSS jet structure led to a spurious 1.4 GHz counterpart.

quotes.quote: Sources J0805 0111 and J1639 + 1632 have nominal CGRaBS spectral indices (and thus FoMs) that are almost certainly overestimates. Their NVSS counterparts have marginally resolved jet structure, and the NVSS decompositions offset the core toward the jet. A faint, spurious counter-jet component was introduced and, being slightly closer to the 8.4 GHz position, was selected as the 1.4 GHz counterpart, leading to a highly inverted spectral index and a high FoM. We include these sources in the survey since they satisfy the CGRaBS prescription; a more careful treatment of the NVSS counterparts would give a smaller spectral index and FoM. This effect is quite rare, occurring in CGRaBS for only these 2 sources (out of 1625, or 0.12 ) and in CRATES for no more than 20 sources (out of 11,131, or 0.18 ).

gmw_00012904.name: SDSS J003135.57+003421.2
gmw_00012904.overview: SDSS J003135.57+003421.2 is a quasar at a redshift of 2.2300 [arXiv:1806.02016]. Its spectra reveal four narrow absorption line systems, with two of these systems showing weakened variations over time [arXiv:1806.02016].

sources.summary: The paper lists this z=2.2300 quasar with four narrow absorption line systems and reports weakened variations in systems at velocity offsets 21,306 and 22,329 km/s.

quotes.quote: Quasar SDSS J003135.57+003421.2 with z_ em =2.2300 . It was spectroscopically observed by the SDSS on MJD = 52262 , 55182, 55443 and 57006. In the quasar spectra, we detect 4 \ NALs with z_ abs =1.7333 , 1.9979, 2.0082 and 2.0246, and _ =49 588 , 22 329 , 21 306 and 19 682 \ from the quasar system. A weakened system with W_r^ 1548 =-3.9 _ W_r^ 1548 had _ =21 306 . The system with _ =22 329 \ also showed a variation of W_r^ 1548 =-2.8 _ W_r^ 1548 .

gmw_00012905.name: SDSS J004856.34+005648.1
gmw_00012905.overview: SDSS J004856.34+005648.1 is a quasar located at a redshift of 2.3230 [arXiv:1806.02016]. Its spectra reveal three narrow absorption line systems, including one system that exhibited a weakening and subsequent strengthening trend over multiple observations [arXiv:1806.02016].

sources.summary: This z=2.3230 quasar has three narrow absorption line systems, with the 1611 km/s system weakening and then strengthening across three SDSS observations.

quotes.quote: Quasar SDSS J004856.34+005648.1 with z_ em =2.3230 . It was spectroscopically observed by the SDSS on MJD = 51913 , 55201 and 55451. In the quasar spectra, we detect 3 \ NALs with z_ abs =1.8929 , 2.2651 and 2.3052, and _ =43 318 , 5272 and 1611 \ from the quasar system. The system with _ =1611 \ was weakened first by W_r^ 1548 =-3.8 _ W_r^ 1548 from MJD=51913 to 55201, and then was enhanced by W_r^ 1548 =1.6 _ W_r^ 1548 from MJD=55201 to 55451.

gmw_00012906.name: SDSS J015017.70+002902.4

gmw_00012907.name: SDSS J024457.18-010809.8

Its spectrum displays strong Lyman-alpha, nitrogen V, and carbon IV absorption troughs [arXiv:astro-ph/0008122], as well as a significantly variable narrow absorption line system [arXiv:1806.02016].

sources.summary: The paper links SDSSp J024457.19--010809.9 to PSS 0244--0108, reports new spectroscopy, and describes it as a BAL quasar with strong Ly+N V and C IV troughs that complicate redshift determination.

quotes.quote: No information on the other quasar, SDSSp J024457.19--010809.9, has been published in the literature, but it is reported at the DPOSS website http://astro.caltech.edu/ ^ george/z4.qso as PSS 0244--0108. We obtained its spectrum in order to measure its spectral properties.

SDSSp J024457.19--010809.9 ( z=3.96 ). This is the previously-known quasar PSS 0244--0108, which has a reported redshift of z=4.01 on the DPOSS website http://astro.caltech.edu/ ^ george/z4.qso . It is a BAL quasar. Strong BAL troughs due to Ly +N V and C IV are clearly visible in the spectrum. The C IV BAL trough has a velocity of 17200 km s ^ 1 and FWHM of 8600 km s ^ 1 . The redshift determination is not straightforward for BAL quasars due to the presence of strong absorption features.

gmw_00012918.name: SDSS J022508.07+001707.2
gmw_00012918.overview: SDSS J022508.07+001707.2 is a quasar located at a redshift of approximately 0.527 [arXiv:1304.2835]. The source becomes bluer as it brightens, exhibiting broad Mg II emission line variability that correlates with changes in its continuum [arXiv:1304.2835].

sources.summary: The paper uses SDSS J022508.07+001707.2 as an example whose spectra become bluer when brighter, with residual Mg II emission indicating broad-line variability tied to continuum changes.

quotes.quote: For our sample, an example is shown in Fig. 3 for SDSS J022508.07+001707.2 ( z=0.527 ). It can be directly seen from the spectra that the source becomes bluer when it goes brighter, which is also evident from the spectrum difference. Intriguingly, the residual of Mg II line is apparent in the spectrum difference.

Therefore, the broad profile of the residual Mg II line implies that the broad Mg II line is also varying along with the variations in the continuum. This is in good agreement with the photoionization model and that the thermal emission is responsible for the continuum, although there might be certain time delay between their variations.

gmw_00012919.name: likely host galaxy of GRB 001025A
gmw_00012919.overview: The likely host galaxy of GRB 001025A is an object with a redshift constrained to less than 2.7 [arXiv:astro-ph/0509424]. It exhibits an irregular morphology and a color indicative of active star formation [arXiv:astro-ph/0509424].

sources.summary: The paper identifies an extended VLT object in the XMM-Newton error circle as the likely GRB 001025A host, reports its B/R magnitudes, limits its redshift, and notes irregular, star-forming colors.

quotes.quote: Figure host shows a 12 12 arcsec ^2 section of the last epoch VLT image with the XMM-Newton 2 error circle overplotted. The 2 error circle is consistent with the eastern part of what seems to be either a galaxy with two main components or a random projection of two unrelated objects. Following piro , we estimate that the probability for a chance alignment is less than 1 , so we consider this object the likely host galaxy of GRB 001025A. The total magnitude of this complex is R=24.01 0.04 and B=25.13 0.12 corrected for a modest foreground extinction of E(B-V)=0.07 schlegel .

The B band detection of the likely host galaxy implies a redshift z<2.7 . The magnitude and color of the galaxy are consistent with a redshift around two, but they do not constitute strong constraints on the redshift.
The appearance of the candidate host galaxy is similar to other GRB hosts, i.e., an irregular morphology and a color that for a redshift of z>0.3 is indicative of active star formation.

gmw_00012920.name: host galaxy of GRB 030115
gmw_00012920.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 030115 is a small irregular galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0401062]. It has a bluer companion galaxy located 1.3 arcseconds to the northeast [arXiv:astro-ph/0401062].

sources.summary: The paper measures the GRB 030115 host in HST imaging, reporting F160W and ACS magnitudes, identifying it as a small irregular galaxy, and resolving a bluer companion to the northeast.

quotes.quote: IRAF aperture photometry of the stacked NICMOS image gives a magnitude of 24.8 for the host galaxy in the F160W filter ( H band), when calibrated using the zero point photometry keywords in the HST header files of the images see http://www.stsci.edu/hst/nicmos/documents/handbooks/DataHandbookv5/ .

It clearly shows the small irregular host galaxy of the GRB with no obvious point source remaining, as well as the bright comparison USNO catalog star used for photometric calibration. The small companion to the host galaxy at 1.3'' to the Northeast, reported by gar03 , is also resolved in these images. It is comparatively more blue than the host, only 0.3 magnitudes dimmer than the host in the F606W filter, and then progressively fainter in the longer wavebands.
We find a magnitude of 26 for the central region of the host galaxy in both filters, F606W and F814W. This gives a surface brightness of 24 magnitudes per square arcsecond.

gmw_00012921.name: SWIRE3 J032825.92-271701.3
gmw_00012921.overview: SWIRE3 J032825.92-271701.3 is a candidate host galaxy for a complex radio double system with a connecting jet [arXiv:2008.05276]. The galaxy is well separated from one of the main radio components, but it exhibits low-level radio emission near its infrared position [arXiv:2008.05276].

sources.summary: The paper discusses SWIRE3 J032825.92-271701.3 as a possible host galaxy for radio components 148-151-141, formerly a complex radio double with a connecting jet, and notes low-level radio emission near the candidate host.

quotes.quote: The radio components 148 - 151 - 141 (shown in Figure  img:C141-C148-C151 ) associated with SWIRE3 J032825.92-271701.3 also appear in the reference catalogue but not as a triple, because there is no central radio component associated with an IR source. Instead they form complex 1 with the comment: ``Radio double with connecting jet'' . The ILP has identified the IR source SWIRE3 J032825.92-271701.3 as the host galaxy. While this is well separated from the radio component C148, there is low-level radio emission associated with this SWIRE source, so it possible that this is the correct host galaxy, even though it does not seem to be associated with the listed central radio component.

gmw_00012922.name: SWIRE3 J033210.74-272635.5
gmw_00012922.overview: SWIRE3 J033210.74-272635.5 is a complex, tailed radio galaxy [arXiv:2008.05276]. Its three radio components form a triple structure with bent tails, which likely result from an interaction with an intra-cluster medium [arXiv:2008.05276].

sources.summary: The paper treats SWIRE3 J033210.74-272635.5 as a complex, tailed radio source whose three radio components were not manually classed as a triple but are correctly classified as one by positional modeling.

quotes.quote: The radio components 440 - 446 - 437 associated with SWIRE3 J033210.74-272635.5 shown Figure  img:C437-C440-C446 also appear in the reference catalogue as complex 3 with the comment ``weird complex source with tails. z(g)'' . It was not classified as a triple in the reference catalogue because to do so would ignore the bent tails, probably resulting from an interaction with an intra-cluster medium. However, here we focus only on the positions of the components, and in those terms this source is correctly classified as a triple.

gmw_00012923.name: source 95
aliases_external: Luo et al. CDF-S Chandra source 95
gmw_00012923.overview: Source 95 is a highly obscured, high-redshift active galactic nucleus candidate [arXiv:1605.08189, arXiv:1209.4193]. Photometric analyses suggest a redshift peak at z = 5.22 [arXiv:1605.08189] or z = 5.729 [arXiv:1209.4193], though X-ray spectral fitting indicates an alternative redshift near 2.8 [arXiv:1605.08189].

sources.summary: The paper treats source 95 as a marginal Compton-thick AGN candidate, noting a surprising redshift above 5, a photometric-redshift peak at z=5.22, and an alternative X-ray-fit redshift near 2.8.

quotes.quote: However, only three of these can be considered as CT AGN: sources 95, 230, and 309 ( Chandra ids), although source 95 is only marginally CT. In the case of source 95, it is very surprising to be able to detect a near-CT AGN at redshift higher than 5. We examined the probability density function (PDF) for the photometric redshift in this case hsu14 . Although the PDF is somewhat flat towards lower redshifts, there is a clear peak at z = 5.22 (see Fig. 95_photz ). We also attempted to constrain the redshift value directly from X-ray spectral fitting.

There seems to be an emission feature around 1.8 keV, although it is not very significant. Assuming that this feature is real and corresponds to the Fe K emission line, we derived a z 2.8 and a resulting column density of a few times 10 ^ 23 cm ^ 2 .

sources.summary: The paper treats XID 139 as a likely high-redshift source with z=5.729, no GOODS optical counterpart, near-infrared detection, and a highly obscured column density if placed at z=3.7.

quotes.quote: We note the presence of 3 sources at z>5 , for which we investigated in more detail the redshift information. Two of them (XID 139 and 197) have only one photometric redshift z=5.729 and 6.071, respectively, from which also provides a secondary solution of z=4.385 for XID 197 Luo10 , while XID 485 has two different photometric redshifts ( z=7.62 from Luo10 , with secondary solution z=3.309 , and z=4.42 from Santini09 ). These objects have no optical counterparts in the Great Observatories Origins Deep Survey (GOODS; XID 139 and 485) or Galaxy Evolution from Morphologies and SEDs (GEMS; XID 197, which is not in the GOODS field) observations, while they are significantly detected by the Infrared Array Camera (IRAC) in the near-IR. Thus, a high-redshift ( z>3 ) solution appears probable.

If they were placed at z=3.7 (the median redshift of the sample), the best-fitting N_H would be 50^ +23 _ 18 10^ 22 , 42^ +20 _ 24 10^ 22 and 118^ +41 _ 28 10^ 22 cm^ 2 for XID 139, 197 and 485, respectively (i.e., they would still be in the highly obscured class).

gmw_00012924.name: source 170
aliases_external: Luo et al. CDF-S Chandra source 170
gmw_00012924.overview: Source 170 is an active galactic nucleus that was detected in only two variability epochs [arXiv:1605.08189]. Although it appears unabsorbed in some analyses, its low count rate also makes a highly absorbed, reflection-dominated spectrum possible [arXiv:1605.08189].

sources.summary: The paper finds source 170 only weakly constrained: it was detected in two variability epochs, appears unabsorbed in this analysis, but prior work and low counts leave a reflection-dominated CT interpretation possible.

quotes.quote: We were able to extract fluxes and errors for our entire sample except for source 170, which was only detected in two epochs, and source 230, which was only detected in three epochs.
Finally, although most of the sources are obscured (N _ H > 10 ^ 22 cm ^ 2 , and > 10 ^ 23 cm ^ 2 in most cases), we also found that two sources seem to be unabsorbed (sources 170 and 326).

In the case of source 170, brightman14 found it to be highly absorbed (almost CT), and we only found a low upper limit for the amount of absorption. Given the extremely low number of counts, a flat reflection dominated spectrum cannot be rejected in this case. Besides, the large uncertainty in its computed X-ray luminosity makes it consistent with the CT region.

gmw_00012925.name: ILTJ160658.74+550607.0
gmw_00012925.overview: ILTJ160658.74+550607.0 is a candidate high-redshift Lyman-alpha emitter and radio galaxy [arXiv:2011.08203]. Although initially modeled at a redshift of z=6.6, spectral energy distribution fitting incorporating far-infrared data suggests the source is more likely an emission line galaxy at z=1.47 [arXiv:2011.08203].

sources.summary: The paper treats ILTJ160658.74+550607.0 as a high-z LAE/radio-galaxy candidate, but SED fitting with FIR data favors z=1.47 [O ii] emission over z=6.6 Ly, with follow-up needed.

quotes.quote: The SED fitting results of the high-z LAE candidate ILTJ160658.74+550607.0 are shown in Fig. fig:sed_2_sources . The ^2_ value lies within a small range and is lowest at z 4.9 when fitting with the original errors.

The evidence for Ly not being the source of emission is less strong in the case of ILTJ160658.74+550607.0 (shown in Fig. fig:sed_2_sources ); however, the FIR SED shape, consisting of SPIRE and PACS observations, is best reproduced by the z=1.47 model and therefore seems to be the actual redshift. Follow-up observations are needed to confirm the redshift of this source. If this source is a radio galaxy at z=6.6 , it would be the most distant radio galaxy known to date.

Unlike in Duncan2020 , in the Bagpipes modelling FIR fluxes have been included in the fit, which, for the source ILTJ160658.74+550607.0, helps break the degeneracy between the z=1.5 and 6.6 solutions.

gmw_00012926.name: VER J0521+211
gmw_00012926.overview: VER J0521+211 is an active galactic nucleus classified as an intermediate-frequency-peaked BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2211.00524]. Because it lacks optical emission features, its redshift remains uncertain, with limits estimated between 0.108 and 0.31 [arXiv:2211.00524].

sources.summary: AGN VER J0521+211 is an uncertain-redshift IBL BL Lac object; the paper uses VERITAS/Fermi-LAT gamma-ray data and redshift limits to investigate photon-ALP oscillation effects.

quotes.quote: In this work, we focus our attention on the photon-ALP oscillation effects on TeV gamma-ray spectral irregularities from the uncertain redshift active galactic nuclei (AGN) VER J0521+211. VER J0521+211 ( RA=05^h21^m45^s , Dec=21^ 12'51.4'' , J2000) is classified as the intermediate frequency peaked BL Lac (IBL) object, which was first observed by the Very Energetic Radiation Imaging Telescope Array System (VERITAS) in 2009 . Since the lack of optical emission features of the IBL object, the redshift of VER J0521+211 is still unknown. Many studies show the redshift limits of this source with 0.108 z_0 0.34 . Recently, VERITAS reported the TeV gamma-ray observations of VER J0521+211 in 2013 and 2014 with the Fermi-LAT and VERITAS data , suggesting the redshift upper limits z_0 0.31 .

gmw_00012927.name: the Elliot Arc
gmw_00012927.overview: The Elliot Arc is a galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.9057 [arXiv:1810.03929]. It is strongly lensed by a massive galaxy cluster situated at a redshift of z=0.3838 [arXiv:1810.03929].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the Elliot Arc as a z=0.9057 galaxy strongly lensed by a massive z=0.3838 cluster, with the lens recorded in the XXL redshift catalogue.

quotes.quote: Incidentally, we note that one fibre was placed at the centre of a gravitational lens independently rediscovered by one of the co-authors (GW) upon visual inspection of DECam images, but already known as `the Elliot Arc ' 2011ApJ...742...48B . This is ` a z = 0.9057 galaxy that is being strongly lensed by a massive galaxy cluster at a redshift of z=0.3838 '. The lens is listed in our redshift catalogue as XXL-AAOmega J235138.01-545254.2 at z=0.379. The cluster is SCSO J235138-545253 2010ApJS..191..340M . Unfortunately, its position is in one of the outermost XXL pointings, XXLs053-08; moreover, because it was at 15 from its centre, it was not processed by Xamin , which stops at 13 , even though extended emission is visible in the X-ray image.

gmw_00012928.name: z_ abs =2.418 system toward SDSS J143912 + 111740
aliases_local: z_ abs =2.418 system toward SDSS J143912 + 111740
aliases_external: z_abs=2.418 DLA system toward SDSS J143912.04+111740.5
gmw_00012928.overview: The z_abs=2.418 system toward SDSS J143912+111740 is a high-redshift damped Lyman-alpha galaxy [arXiv:0809.3785]. This unique system contains detected HD, H2, and CO molecules, exhibiting a solar-level chemical enrichment alongside a low deuterium astration factor that suggests an intense infall of primordial gas [arXiv:0809.3785].

sources.original_name: z_ abs =2.418 system toward SDSS J143912 + 111740
sources.summary: The paper treats the z_abs=2.418 DLA system toward SDSS J143912.04 + 111740.5 as a high-redshift DLA galaxy with HD, H2, CO, solar metallicity, and low deuterium astration from inferred primordial gas infall.

quotes.quote: We present the detection of deuterated molecular hydrogen (HD) in the remote Universe in a damped Lyman- cloud at z_ abs =2.418 toward the quasar SDSS J143912.04 + 111740.5. This is a unique system in which H _2 and CO molecules are also detected. The chemical enrichment of this gas derived from Zn ii and S ii is as high as in the Sun.

We measure N( HD )/2 N( H_2 )=1.5^ +0.6 _ 0.4 10^ 5 , which is significantly higher than the same ratio measured in the Galaxy and close to the primordial D/H ratio estimated from the WMAP constraint on the baryonic matter density ( _ b ). This indicates a low astration factor of deuterium that contrasts with the unusually high chemical enrichment of the gas. This can be interpreted as the consequence of an intense infall of primordial gas onto the associated galaxy.

gmw_00012929.name: GRB 020813 host galaxy
gmw_00012929.overview: The GRB 020813 host galaxy is a high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.255 [arXiv:astro-ph/0409717, arXiv:astro-ph/0212554]. It exhibits [O II] emission indicating active star formation [arXiv:astro-ph/0212554], along with metal absorption lines that show no detected variability [arXiv:astro-ph/0212554].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes the GRB 020813 host galaxy at z=1.255 through absorption systems, deriving Fe II/Mg II abundances, uncertain saturated Mg II components, and higher columns than GRB 021004 likely due to different gas ionization.

quotes.quote: For this GRB we considered the absorption systems at the following velocities with respect to the redshift of the host galaxy, which was assumed to be 1.255 (Barth et al. 2003): v=0 km/s (system A in figure grb020813 ), v=--106 km/s (system B) and v=--306 km/s (system C). Note that the system A and system B lines are strongly blended with the exception of the Fe 2 2374 line. We also consider the system at z=1.2234. We fitted simultaneously the 6 lines of the 3 absorption systems in figure grb020813 . Table 7 gives for the 3 systems the best fit Fe 2 and Mg 2 abundances along with the velocity shift of each system with respect to the redshift of the host galaxy. Mg 2 lines of systems A and B are strongly saturated and therefore their column density estimates are more uncertain.

Finally, we note that the Fe 2 and Mg 2 column densities found in GRB 020813 are 10--100 times higher than those in GRB 021004. This is likely due to a much higher ionization of the gas in the latter case, rather than a large difference in the total absorbing column of gas or to highly non solar metal abundances (note that in both cases the [ Fe 2 / Mg 2 ] ratio is consistent with meteoritic abundances). We also note the similarity between the velocity shift between the z=1.2234 system and the GRB 020813 host galaxy and the shift of the z=2.296 and z=2.298 systems with respect to the GRB 021004 host galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper identifies the z=1.255 system as the likely GRB host, with metal absorption, [O II] emission indicating star-forming activity, and no detected absorption-line variability.

quotes.quote: Additional features from the z=1.255 system include lines of Al 3 , Mn 2 , Mg 1 , Zn 2 , and Cr 2 . One emission line is also present in the spectrum, centered at 8404.0 \ and consistent with [ O 2 ] 3727 at z = 1.255 . We conclude that the z=1.255 system is the likely host of the GRB, since it is the highest-redshift absorption system detected and its [ O 2 ] emission line is an indicator of star-forming activity. Ionization of the surrounding interstellar medium (ISM) by the GRB could cause temporal variations in the equivalent widths of interstellar absorption lines within the host galaxy pl98 , and we have measured the equivalent widths of the absorption lines in the hour 1, 2, and 3 spectra separately to search for any variations. The differences in equivalent widths are consistent with the level of measurement uncertainty, and we conclude that no variability is detected.

gmw_00012930.name: B2250+194
gmw_00012930.overview: B2250+194 is a radio galaxy that hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1509.07529]. The galaxy contains a compact radio structure, which has been utilized as a reference source in VLBI astrometric observations [arXiv:1509.07529].

sources.summary: The paper treats B2250+194 as the active nucleus of a radio galaxy used as a compact VLBI reference source, with its brightness peak and proper-motion limit supporting astrometric frame checks.

quotes.quote: The other two were the active nucleus of the radio galaxy, B2250+194 , and the unidentified source (most likely also a quasar or a radio galaxy), B2252+172 .

The remaining sources were both rather compact, so that the brightness peak could simply be taken as the reference point in the structure; their disadvantages were that B2250+194 was located relatively far away from \ and B2252+172 was rather faint (with an unknown redshift).

In addition, we used geodetic and astrometric VLBI observations regularly conducted over many years to determine the limit on the proper motion of two of the three reference sources, 3C 454.3 and B2250+194. These determinations were made without consideration of a particular reference point in the structure of the two sources but were made with respect to a celestial reference frame, CRF.

gmw_00012931.name: B2252+172
gmw_00012931.overview: B2252+172 is an unidentified compact radio source located at a cosmological distance [arXiv:1509.07529]. It is most likely a quasar or a radio galaxy, characterized by its faintness and an unknown redshift [arXiv:1509.07529].

sources.summary: The paper treats B2252+172 as an unidentified compact reference source, probably a quasar or radio galaxy; its faintness, unknown redshift, and later inclusion shaped the VLBI observing sequence.

quotes.quote: These sources needed to be at cosmological distances, be sufficiently strong and compact for VLBI observations, and located nearby to \ on the sky so as to minimize systematic astrometric errors. We selected three reference sources, with the strong quasar, 3C454.3 , as the main one. The other two were the active nucleus of the radio galaxy, B2250+194 , and the unidentified source (most likely also a quasar or a radio galaxy), B2252+172 .

The remaining sources were both rather compact, so that the brightness peak could simply be taken as the reference point in the structure; their disadvantages were that B2250+194 was located relatively far away from \ and B2252+172 was rather faint (with an unknown redshift).

For the remaining 12 sessions, B2252+172 was included in the observations and the sequence was altered to 3C 454.3 (80 s) - IM Peg (125 s) - B2250+194 (80 s) - 3C 454.3 (80 s) - IM Peg (125 s) - B2250+194 (80 s) - B2252+172 (90 s).

gmw_00012932.overview: 0850+581 is a distant quasar located at a redshift of z=1.318 [arXiv:0810.2240]. Very Long Baseline Interferometry observations of the source reveal a large radio core shift between the 2.3 and 8.6 GHz bands [arXiv:0810.2240].

sources.summary: The paper presents VLBA observations of the distant quasar 0850+581, finding an unusually large radio core shift and using it to infer inner jet geometry, nuclear physics, and astrometric-position implications.

quotes.quote: We have found an intriguing case of the distant quasar 0850+581 (redshift z=1.318 , SDSS release 2) for which the core shift was estimated to be 1.5 mas between 2.3 and 8.6 GHz Kovalev_cs_2008 . The total error of the core shift value for this object is dominated by blending of the first bright jet component with the core at long wavelengths. However, the distance between the core and this component was about 0.5 mas. This means that the huge core shift value about 1 mas measured between 2.3 and 8.6 GHz and 2.3 and 15 GHz must be real. So far, this is one of the largest core shifts detected.

Application of this to the quasar 0850+581 with a large core shift effect allowed to determine geometry and physics in the inner region of compact jet. The distant quasar 0850+581 is an ICRF source ICRF98 and is monitored in astrometric/geodetic VLBI sessions. Results of this study can be used to improving the absolute position of the object and for aligning positions measured at different observing bands.

gmw_00012933.name: id43
gmw_00012933.overview: The object id43 is a dwarf galaxy system [arXiv:2405.18685]. It hosts an active X-ray source whose emission exceeds the expected contribution from X-ray binaries by a factor of 500 to 1500 [arXiv:2405.18685].

sources.summary: The paper identifies id43 as a dwarf system hosting an X-ray source and describes it as the only non-LLAGN source, with emission far exceeding expected X-ray-binary contributions.

quotes.quote: The X-ray sources reside in dwarf systems labeled as id3, id10, id34, id43, id81, id86, and id91 in Table tab1 through Table tab3 .
On the other hand, the X-ray source from id43, the only non-LLAGN source, exceeds the expected XRB contribution by a factor of 500.

One X-ray source, id81, is consistent with the XRB emission; the only non-LLAGN source, id43, exceeds the XRB predicted emission by a factor of 1500; and in the other five sources, the XRB contributions are expected to account for 0.6 12 with a median value of 5 .

gmw_00012934.name: id81
gmw_00012934.overview: The object id81 is a dwarf galaxy system that hosts an observed X-ray source [arXiv:2405.18685]. This X-ray emission is consistent with the galaxy's expected X-ray binary population, making the presence of an active galactic nucleus unlikely [arXiv:2405.18685].

sources.summary: The paper identifies id81 as a dwarf-system X-ray source but finds its emission consistent with X-ray binaries; the expected XRB contribution exceeds the observation, so it is removed from the AGN sample.

quotes.quote: The X-ray sources reside in dwarf systems labeled as id3, id10, id34, id43, id81, id86, and id91 in Table tab1 through Table tab3 .
We find that one X-ray source, id81, is in line with the expected emission from the XRB population.

Therefore, we find that in one case, id81, the expected XRB contribution to the X-ray emission exceeds the observed X-ray emission, thus, the presence of an AGN is unlikely and id81 is removed from the final AGN sample.

gmw_00012935.name: ID 565713
gmw_00012935.overview: ID 565713 is a flat-spectrum candidate that is suspected to be a bright active galactic nucleus or blazar [arXiv:1810.00878]. It is located in a region of low extinction and lacks a PACS counterpart, with its surroundings showing a loose clustering of background galaxies [arXiv:1810.00878].

sources.summary: The paper flags ID 565713 as a suspicious flat-spectrum candidate that could be a bright AGN/blazar, citing its off-cloud low-extinction location, lack of a PACS counterpart, and nearby background galaxies.

quotes.quote: The last new flat-spectrum candidate (ID 565713) discussed here is a rather suspicious YSO candidate. It could also be a bright AGN (Blazar), since it is located a bit off from the cloud ( 30 ) to the Galactic north of L1641 at (l,b) (211.94,-18.51) , in a region of low extinction ( A_ K,Herschel 0.15 mag ). It is the only of the six new flat-spectrum sources that dose not have a PACS counterpart. Furthermore, the surroundings show a loose clustering of background galaxies.

gmw_00012936.overview: 0259+0048 is a quasar that exhibits an unusually hard X-ray spectrum [arXiv:astro-ph/0611120]. Even after accounting for an intrinsic neutral absorber, the source maintains a hard X-ray continuum that may indicate more complex absorption [arXiv:astro-ph/0611120].

sources.summary: After adding intrinsic absorption in spectral fits, 0259+0048 still has a hard X-ray continuum. The authors suggest complex absorption may explain it, but low counts prevent stronger modeling.

quotes.quote: We included an intrinsic neutral absorber for sources 0259+0048, 0918+5139, 1002+5542, and 1411+5205, despite the fact that they have < 200 counts. These sources initially exhibited unusually hard X-ray spectra ( _X 1 ), so we fit _X and N_H simultaneously to test if these low values of _X were caused by unrecognized absorption. Even after including an absorber at the quasar redshift, sources 0259+0048, 0918+5139, and 1002+5542 still have rather hard X-ray continua, with photon induces of 1.02 0.24 , 0.92 0.33 , and 1.27 0.15 , respectively. Such hard X-ray spectra could be the result of more complex absorption, such as an ionized or partial covering absorber. However, the low number of counts for these sources preclude obtaining meaningful spectral fits for more complex models.

gmw_00012937.overview: The object 0918+5139 is a quasar that exhibits an unusually hard X-ray spectrum [arXiv:astro-ph/0611120]. Even after accounting for an intrinsic neutral absorber, it maintains a hard X-ray continuum that may indicate more complex absorption [arXiv:astro-ph/0611120].

sources.summary: After adding intrinsic absorption in spectral fits, 0918+5139 still has a hard X-ray continuum. The authors suggest complex absorption may explain it, but low counts prevent stronger modeling.

quotes.quote: We included an intrinsic neutral absorber for sources 0259+0048, 0918+5139, 1002+5542, and 1411+5205, despite the fact that they have < 200 counts. These sources initially exhibited unusually hard X-ray spectra ( _X 1 ), so we fit _X and N_H simultaneously to test if these low values of _X were caused by unrecognized absorption. Even after including an absorber at the quasar redshift, sources 0259+0048, 0918+5139, and 1002+5542 still have rather hard X-ray continua, with photon induces of 1.02 0.24 , 0.92 0.33 , and 1.27 0.15 , respectively. Such hard X-ray spectra could be the result of more complex absorption, such as an ionized or partial covering absorber. However, the low number of counts for these sources preclude obtaining meaningful spectral fits for more complex models.

gmw_00012938.overview: 1002+5542 is a quasar that exhibits a hard X-ray spectrum [arXiv:astro-ph/0611120]. This hard X-ray continuum persists even after accounting for an intrinsic neutral absorber, which may indicate more complex absorption [arXiv:astro-ph/0611120].

sources.summary: After adding intrinsic absorption in spectral fits, 1002+5542 still has a hard X-ray continuum. The authors suggest complex absorption may explain it, but low counts prevent stronger modeling.

quotes.quote: We included an intrinsic neutral absorber for sources 0259+0048, 0918+5139, 1002+5542, and 1411+5205, despite the fact that they have < 200 counts. These sources initially exhibited unusually hard X-ray spectra ( _X 1 ), so we fit _X and N_H simultaneously to test if these low values of _X were caused by unrecognized absorption. Even after including an absorber at the quasar redshift, sources 0259+0048, 0918+5139, and 1002+5542 still have rather hard X-ray continua, with photon induces of 1.02 0.24 , 0.92 0.33 , and 1.27 0.15 , respectively. Such hard X-ray spectra could be the result of more complex absorption, such as an ionized or partial covering absorber. However, the low number of counts for these sources preclude obtaining meaningful spectral fits for more complex models.

gmw_00012939.overview: The source 1411+5205 is a quasar that exhibits an unusually hard X-ray spectrum [arXiv:astro-ph/0611120]. It shows evidence of considerable intrinsic neutral absorption and may be a broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0611120].

sources.summary: The authors fitted 1411+5205 with intrinsic absorption because its spectrum was unusually hard and report considerable absorption, with a 95 percent column-density interval suggesting it may be a BAL QSO.

quotes.quote: We included an intrinsic neutral absorber for sources 0259+0048, 0918+5139, 1002+5542, and 1411+5205, despite the fact that they have < 200 counts. These sources initially exhibited unusually hard X-ray spectra ( _X 1 ), so we fit _X and N_H simultaneously to test if these low values of _X were caused by unrecognized absorption.

Source 1411+5205 shows evidence for considerable absorption, N_H 10^ 23 \ cm^ 2 , and may be a BAL QSO. A 95 confidence interval on the column density for this source is 1.8 10^ 22 \ cm^ 2 < N_H < 2.45 10^ 23 \ cm^ 2 .

gmw_00012940.name: GRB host
gmw_00012940.overview: This galaxy is identified as the host of the gamma-ray burst GRB 140903A [arXiv:1605.03573]. It is a compact, red system with a rest-frame absolute B-band magnitude of approximately -20.9 mag [arXiv:1605.03573].

sources.summary: The paper identifies a compact red host galaxy for GRB 140903A, reports its optical magnitudes and J2000 position, and treats it as the likely GRB host with rest-frame B luminosity about 0.8 L*.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: Using astrometry from nearby SDSS point sources for reference, we measure a (J2000.0) position of = 15^ h 52^ m 03.278^ s , = +27^ 36 10 68 .

quotes.quote: Using the images from 2016 March 17, we measure the following magnitudes for the underlying host galaxy: ^ = 20.58 0.09 , and i^ = 20.12 0.05 . From earlier observations we also measure g^ = 21.97 0.16 , and z^ = 19.66 0.08 , although we caution that these fluxes may include some afterglow contribution. The host is unresolved in all of our DCT images (seeing ranging from 0 8--2 0). Using astrometry from nearby SDSS point sources for reference, we measure a (J2000.0) position of = 15^ h 52^ m 03.278^ s , = +27^ 36 10 68 .

GRB 140903A is located on top of a compact and red galaxy, suggestive of an old system. Based on the galaxy sky densities in the band yasuda01 , we estimated a small probability of a chance association, P_ ch 3 4 bloom02,troja08 , and we therefore consider this galaxy as the GRB host. From our band measurement we derive a rest-frame absolute B band magnitude M_B 20.9 mag, or L_B 0.8 L_ * when compared to the luminosity function of galaxies at a similar redshift 0.2 < z < 0.4 willmer06 .

gmw_00012941.name: MaNGA plate-IFU 9509-9102
gmw_00012941.overview: MaNGA plate-IFU 9509-9102 is a spiral galaxy classified with an Sab-type morphology [arXiv:2402.00959]. It features a bulge and a disc component, which can be cleanly modeled when nearby foreground stars are masked [arXiv:2402.00959].

sources.summary: MaNGA plate-IFU 9509-9102 is an Sab galaxy used to show that foreground-star masking changes its bulge-disc fit, correcting distorted unmasked parameters and producing a cleaner, more physical model.

quotes.quote: Figure fig:star_mask compares the masked and unmasked fit results and mass-assembly histories for the galaxy with MaNGA plate-IFU 9509-9102, which has an Sab type morphology. The upper row depicts the final BUDDI fits after the foreground star within the hexagonal field of view has been masked out, and the middle row shows the fits from the previous run where the star was included in the fitting process.

From the disc model of the DR15 fit, it is clear that in the unmasked fit, GalfitM has tried to include the neighbouring star in the fit, resulting in an unusually extended light profile for the component, with a S ersic index of 0.2 (which is also the lower limit of the fits) and R_e of 22.38 (which is the most unfeasible and significantly affected parameter). Moreover, the disc axis ratio ( b/a=0.13 ) suggests a highly edge-on galaxy, which is not the case as seen from the SDSS image and the MaNGA datacube. Since the foreground star appears very close to the disc, the fit has assumed it to be a significant part of the disc and has completely shifted the position angle of the disc in order to incorporate it. The bulge has also been modelled as an elongated component (with a large R_e of 9.13 ) that has incorporated the light from the true disc component of the galaxy. In the new DR17 fit with the star masked out, GalfitM has modelled the bulge and the disc cleanly with a more physically motivated S ersic indices of 1 and 0.65 respectively.

gmw_00012942.name: NSA 64129
gmw_00012942.overview: NSA 64129 is a galaxy located at a right ascension of 149.70279675 degrees and a declination of 2.8786719 degrees [arXiv:2306.13639]. It is included in the XMM-Newton source catalog, though it was excluded from a Chandra X-ray Observatory sample because it straddled the edge of the CCD detector [arXiv:2306.13639].

sources.summary: The paper identifies NSA 64129 as the only Chandra-covered galaxy excluded from analysis because it straddled the CCD edge, while noting that it lies in the XMM source catalog.

quotes.quote: NSA 64129 is the only galaxy that was not analyzed. It is 9.07 arc-minutes from the nearest observation's center, and inspection of that observation's image shows that the galaxy straddles the edge of the CCD, thus we removed it from the CXO sample. NSA 64129 however lays within the XMM source catalog, as discussed in Section subsec: XMM data .

gmw_00012943.name: NSA 631492
gmw_00012943.overview: NSA 631492 is a low-mass dwarf galaxy [arXiv:2306.13639]. It has a low X-ray luminosity and falls outside the standard criteria for an X-ray active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2306.13639].

sources.summary: The paper identifies NSA 631492 as a dwarf and the least massive, lowest-luminosity X-ray object in the sample; unlike other X-ray detections, it falls outside the X-ray AGN criterion.

quotes.quote: There were two dwarf galaxies with CXO coverage - NSA 631492 and 631480.

NSA 631492 has the lowest luminosity of our whole X-ray population, at L_ 0.5-7.0 keV = 7.8 10^ 37 erg s^ 1 . It is also worth noting that NSA 631492 is the least massive object in this study, at ( M _ * / M _ ) = 6.89 and it lacks an optical spectrum from SDSS.

The notable exception is NSA 631492, which is the lowest mass, lowest X-ray luminosity object in the sample. finds that LINER type galaxies have excess X-ray emission across broad 0.5-10 keV for their SFR compared to normal non-AGN galaxies. They separate their X-ray AGN candidates as those having X-ray luminosities more than 0.6 dex above the X-ray luminosity predicted from their SFR. All but NSA 631492 lay within criteria for X-ray AGN.

gmw_00012944.name: NSA 64145
gmw_00012944.overview: NSA 64145 is a galaxy that hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2306.13639]. It exhibits persistent, bright broadband X-ray emission that was observed consistently between 2004 and 2013 [arXiv:2306.13639].

sources.summary: NSA 64145 has bright Chandra emission and an XMM counterpart, with consistent broadband X-ray emission from 2004-2006 and 2013 that the authors interpret as persistent AGN activity.

quotes.quote: As seen in Table tab:lum table , NSA 64145, NSA 64272, and NSA 64286 have both bright X-ray emission within archival CXO data and an associated source within the XMM Source Catalog. Thus, we can evaluate whether these sources have persistent or variable/transient X-ray emission. We present the GALEX near-UV light curves for these three objects from in Figure fig:light curves of 64145 64272 64286 . h We find consistent and persistent broadband X-ray emission for NSA 64145, NSA 64272, and NSA 64286.

For NSA 64145, the CXO observations for CXO were taken in June 2013 and the XMM observations were taken between December 2004 and May 2006. Thus, NSA 64145 demonstrates persistent X-ray emission over near decade-long timescale.
We consider the X-ray emission from the three sources above to be most likely due to an AGN as opposed to a variable source such as a tidal disruption event (TDE).

gmw_00012945.name: NSA 64272
gmw_00012945.overview: NSA 64272 is a galaxy that hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2306.13639]. It exhibits bright, persistent broadband X-ray emission that was detected by both the Chandra and XMM-Newton observatories between 2003 and 2007 [arXiv:2306.13639].

sources.summary: NSA 64272 has bright Chandra emission and an XMM counterpart; its Chandra and XMM luminosities agree within half a dex across observations from 2003-2007, favoring persistent AGN emission.

quotes.quote: As seen in Table tab:lum table , NSA 64145, NSA 64272, and NSA 64286 have both bright X-ray emission within archival CXO data and an associated source within the XMM Source Catalog. Thus, we can evaluate whether these sources have persistent or variable/transient X-ray emission. We present the GALEX near-UV light curves for these three objects from in Figure fig:light curves of 64145 64272 64286 . h We find consistent and persistent broadband X-ray emission for NSA 64145, NSA 64272, and NSA 64286.

For NSA 64272, both its CXO and XMM X-ray luminosities are roughly consistent,within a half dex of each other. The XMM source catalog used observations from December 2003, May 2006, and May 2007. The CXO observation for this object was taken in January of 2007.
We consider the X-ray emission from the three sources above to be most likely due to an AGN as opposed to a variable source such as a tidal disruption event (TDE).

gmw_00012946.name: NSA 208625
gmw_00012946.overview: NSA 208625 is a galaxy that exhibits stochastic UV variability [arXiv:2306.13639]. It has been detected in X-rays by Chandra, but its emission later dropped below the detection limit of XMM-Newton, suggesting either a changing-state active galactic nucleus or a prior tidal disruption event with residual accretion [arXiv:2306.13639].

sources.summary: NSA 208625 has a Chandra detection above its later XMM upper limit and stochastic UV variability; the authors suggest either a prior TDE with residual accretion or a changing-state AGN.

quotes.quote: Interestingly, NSA 208625 has a CXO detection at a higher value than its XMM upper limit. All archival CXO observations of this object were taken between August and October of 2005. XMM observed this field on December 26th, 2015. The UV light curve, constructed with data collected between May 4th, 2009 and March 14th, 2011, shows stochastic variability rather than a burst-like light curve.

A possible scenario for this object is a TDE that occurred prior to the CXO observations and faded by the time of the XMM observations. The intermediate UV variability could be from residual accretion following the TDE. Alternately, this object could be a changing state AGN. Further study of object is needed to confirm either scenario.

gmw_00012947.name: 3FGL J2247.8+4413
gmw_00012947.overview: 3FGL J2247.8+4413 is a BL Lacertae object identified with NVSS J224753+441317 [arXiv:1607.03006]. This active galactic nucleus is very faint and exhibits variable behavior [arXiv:1607.03006].

sources.summary: The paper treats 3FGL J2247.8+4413, identified with NVSS J224753+441317, as a BL Lac object near the field; it is faint, variable, considered for contamination, and judged negligible during transient detections.

quotes.quote: In the vicinity of \ there is a \ source, 3FGL J2247.8+4413, which is identified with NVSS J224753+441317, a BL Lac object Fermi3fgl . It is located at galactic coordinates (l,b)= ( 100 72,-13 25 ) , at a distance of 0 93 from the position of . We considered this source at the time of analyzing \ data in order to take into account possible contamination. However, this is a very faint source with a flux of F(E>1 GeV = (5.3 0.5) 10^ 10  cm ^ 2  s ^ 1 and variable behaviour. Hence, in the short time periods when \ detected the transient emission the effect of 3FGL J2247.8+4413 on the detection is negligible.

gmw_00012948.name: RX J2243.1+4441
gmw_00012948.overview: RX J2243.1+4441 is a radio galaxy that exhibits a radio morphology resembling classical Fanaroff-Riley II type sources [arXiv:1607.03006]. It features a stable bright core with faint jet signatures and two radio lobes, alongside a soft X-ray spectrum that peaks around 0.5 keV [arXiv:1607.03006].

sources.summary: The paper describes RX J2243.1+4441 as a radio galaxy or quasar candidate with sparse multiwavelength data, FR-II-like morphology, faint jets/lobes, and a soft X-ray spectrum.

quotes.quote: However, there is little information about RX J2243.1+4441, its behaviour and characteristics, due to the lack of optical counterpart and scarce radio and X-ray data. This source can be classified as a radio galaxy through its radio morphology Marcote_thesis : it presents a bright core at the center of the galaxy with a radio flux of 2.2 mJy beam ^ 1 , stable over month timescales with faint signatures of a jet, and two radio lobes expanding towards the intergalactic medium spanning more than 50 ^ . However, the available radio data at only one frequency does not allow for a spectral characterization of the source. The radio morphology of this object resembles the classical FR-II type radio galaxies. While FR I-type radio galaxies have been found to emit in gamma rays in several cases Fermi3fgl , FR-II type seem to be more rare in the GeV sky 2010ApJ...720..912A, Grandi2012 , disfavouring, although not excluding, RX J2243.1+4441 as the putative counterpart of .

In the X-rays, a revision of the XMM-Newton data (Obs ID 0723610201), shows that the radio galaxy exhibits a spectrum with peak around 0.5 keV and fades away above 5 keV. The spectral fit with an absorbed powerlaw model yields a photon index =1.8 0.4 and a flux in the 0.2 5.0 keV range of F (0.2-5 keV )=(6.0 0.7) 10^ 14 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 .

gmw_00012949.name: J1419 0838
gmw_00012949.overview: J1419 0838 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar classified as a blazar [arXiv:2303.09263]. It features a compact milliarcsecond-scale core with Doppler-enhanced emission from a relativistic jet, alongside mid-infrared variability linked to active galactic nucleus activity [arXiv:2303.09263].

sources.summary: The paper characterizes J1419 0838 as a flat-spectrum radio quasar/blazar with compact radio emission, Doppler-enhanced jet signatures, flat spectrum, and significant mid-infrared variability unrelated to reported flares.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: right ascension 14^ h 19^ m 22.556072^ s , declination 08^ 38^ 32.14018^ taken from the Radio Fundamental Catalogue version 2022c

quotes.quote: NVSS J141922 083830 (J1419 0838) was previously classified as a flat-spectrum radio quasar, based on its ray, X-ray, optical, and near-infrared properties. However, no detailed analysis was published about its radio emission. Here we utilised publicly available archival radio data at pc and kpc scales to characterise its radio emission, both quantitatively and qualitatively. The quasar has a single compact mas-scale `core' component at all five VLBI epochs between 2013 and 2018, and shows high measured brightness temperature values that indicate Doppler-enhanced emission from a relativistic jet pointing close to the line of sight.

The radio source has indeed a flat (inverted) spectrum. Based on the radio data, we confirm that the quasar belongs to the blazar subclass of FSRQs. Based on WISE data, we found that the MIR light curve of the quasar shows both short- and long-term variability, most likely connected with AGN phenomena. Neither the intra-day, nor the monthly light variations indicate obvious correlation with the flaring events identified in the optical and ray light curves.

gmw_00012950.name: damped system at z=2.3 towards PH957
aliases_local: damped system at z=2.3 towards PH957
gmw_00012950.overview: The damped system at z=2.3 towards PH957 is a high-redshift galaxy system [arXiv:astro-ph/9810381, arXiv:astro-ph/0101029]. Its metal-line profiles exhibit the majority of their absorption within a narrow 20 km/s interval [arXiv:astro-ph/0101029].

sources.summary: For PHL 957/Q0100 + 13, the paper updates earlier work with FeII 1611, revised CrII and NiII column densities, an O upper limit, and LBQS sample membership.

quotes.quote: Q0100 + 13, z = 2.309 The majority of our results on PHL 957 (a.k.a. \ Q0100 + 13) were published by Wolfe et al. \ (1994). The major exception is we now present a measurement of the Fe abundance based on the previously unidentified FeII 1611 profile. Also, we measure column densities in light of new f and values, in particular those for the CrII and NiII transitions.

Note the O abundance is based on the very weak OI 1355 profile and we report it as a 3 upper limit. Figure vpQ0100 presents the velocity plots and Table Q0100-clm lists the measured ionic column densities for this system. This damped system is included in the statistical sample of the LBQS survey.

sources.original_name: damped system at z=2.3 towards PH957
sources.summary: The paper uses the z=2.3 damped system toward PH957 to check ESI resolution, comparing ESI and HIRES absorption-line column densities and finding close agreement for weak lines.

quotes.quote: To quantify this potential systematic error, we analyzed an ESI echellette spectrum of the very bright quasar PH957. There is a known damped system at z=2.3 towards PH957 with previous HIRES observations wol94,pro99 which reveal that the metal-line profiles exhibit the majority of absorption within a 20 km/s interval, i.e. \ well within the ESI resolution. We compared the column density measurements determined from the apparent optical depth method sav91 from 10 absorption lines observed with both ESI and HIRES. For absorption lines with < 0.5 all of the column density values agreed to within the 2 statistical error and 80 were within 1 . Even several obviously saturated profiles yielded column densities to within 0.2 dex.

gmw_00012951.name: PSS1432+39
gmw_00012951.overview: PSS1432+39 is a high-redshift galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0101029] that contains a damped absorption system [arXiv:astro-ph/0101029]. Because its iron profiles are blended or unreliable, its metallicity is estimated using nickel and chromium observations [arXiv:astro-ph/0101029].

sources.summary: The paper says the PSS1432+39 damped system lacked reliable Fe II profiles, so metallicity was estimated from Ni and Cr observations with an added 0.05 dex uncertainty.

quotes.quote: In one case (PSS1432+39) all of the observed Fe II profiles were blended with significant absorption features or too weak/strong to provide a reliable column density measurement. To estimate the metallicity we relied on the observations of other ions, in particular Ni ^+ and Cr ^+ which show very nearly solar relative abundances (i.e. \ [ Ni/Fe ] =0) in nearly every damped system. For this system, we adopted an additional 0.05 dex error.

gmw_00012952.name: HUDF-480
gmw_00012952.overview: HUDF-480 is a high-redshift Lyman-break galaxy candidate [arXiv:0804.4874]. The galaxy is faint, very compact, and has infrared colors consistent with a star-forming galaxy at a redshift of approximately 7 [arXiv:0804.4874].

sources.summary: HUDF-480 is one of four z>6.8 LBG candidates; the paper describes it as faint and compact, previously reported, and detected by Spitzer with colors matching a z~7 star-forming galaxy.

quotes.quote: All SExtractor sources that satisfy the above color criterion were visually inspected; four z>6.8 LBG candidates were identified after removal of spurious detections (e.g., stellar diffraction spikes, edge artifacts, etc.).
All four z dropouts are rather faint ( H_ 160 27 ) and very compact (they are essentially unresolved at the resolution of the NICMOS images).

One of the z_ 850 dropouts in our analysis (HUDF-480) had already been reported in previous works bouw04b,bouw06,coe06 , and it has also been detected in Spitzer images labb07 , with infrared colors in very good agreement with a star-forming galaxy at z 7 .

gmw_00012953.name: HUDF-708
gmw_00012953.overview: HUDF-708 is a high-redshift Lyman-break galaxy candidate [arXiv:0804.4874]. The galaxy is faint, very compact, and severely blended in available Spitzer space telescope data [arXiv:0804.4874].

sources.summary: HUDF-708 is identified as a z>6.8 LBG candidate. The paper describes the candidate set as faint and compact, while noting that HUDF-708 is severely blended in available Spitzer data.

quotes.quote: All SExtractor sources that satisfy the above color criterion were visually inspected; four z>6.8 LBG candidates were identified after removal of spurious detections (e.g., stellar diffraction spikes, edge artifacts, etc.).
All four z dropouts are rather faint ( H_ 160 27 ) and very compact (they are essentially unresolved at the resolution of the NICMOS images).

The remaining z_ 850 dropouts in our sample are either undetected ( <2 ; NICP34-66/80)) or severely blended (HUDF-708) in the existing, publicly available Spitzer data (from the IRAC followup of GOODS for the HUDF http://data.spitzer.caltech.edu/popular/goods/ and from the SIMPLE survey for NICP34 http://data.spitzer.caltech.edu/popular/simple/ ).

gmw_00012954.name: NICP34-66
gmw_00012954.overview: NICP34-66 is a high-redshift Lyman-break galaxy candidate [arXiv:0804.4874]. The galaxy is undetected in existing Spitzer data and is consistent with having a slightly larger dust extinction than other similar candidates [arXiv:0804.4874].

sources.summary: NICP34-66 is one of the z>6.8 LBG candidates. The paper says it has slightly larger inferred extinction than the other candidates and is undetected in existing Spitzer data.

quotes.quote: All SExtractor sources that satisfy the above color criterion were visually inspected; four z>6.8 LBG candidates were identified after removal of spurious detections (e.g., stellar diffraction spikes, edge artifacts, etc.).

As evident from Fig. fig:JH_ZJ , three out of our four high- z candidates support relatively small amount of dust, E(B-V)=0.15 , while the last one (NICP34-66) is consistent with slightly larger extinction.

The remaining z_ 850 dropouts in our sample are either undetected ( <2 ; NICP34-66/80)) or severely blended (HUDF-708) in the existing, publicly available Spitzer data (from the IRAC followup of GOODS for the HUDF http://data.spitzer.caltech.edu/popular/goods/ and from the SIMPLE survey for NICP34 http://data.spitzer.caltech.edu/popular/simple/ ).

gmw_00012955.name: NICP34-80
gmw_00012955.overview: NICP34-80 is identified as a candidate Lyman-break galaxy at a redshift greater than 6.8 [arXiv:0804.4874]. However, it is a faint, low-confidence candidate that is likely a spurious detection [arXiv:0804.4874].

sources.summary: NICP34-80 is treated as a z>6.8 LBG candidate but flagged as low-confidence: it is faint, less secure, and likely spurious based on negative-image tests.

quotes.quote: All SExtractor sources that satisfy the above color criterion were visually inspected; four z>6.8 LBG candidates were identified after removal of spurious detections (e.g., stellar diffraction spikes, edge artifacts, etc.).
The faintest source (NICP34-80) has a S/N of 4.8 in the 0 3 aperture, and of only 2 in the aperture. Thus NICP34-80 is a less secure high- z candidate.

In order to estimate the reality of NICP34-80, which is only detected at 2 \ in , we repeated our analysis on `negative images', obtained by multiplying the real images by 1 (after masking out all detected sources). One `negative source' is detected with S/N( ) >4.5 , S/N( ) >2 and a pixel flux distribution similar to NICP34-80. Thus, we conclude that NICP34-80 is likely a spurious detection.

gmw_00012956.name: SDSS J030238.16+000203.4
gmw_00012956.overview: SDSS J030238.16+000203.4 is a blue broad-line quasar located at a redshift of z=1.35 [arXiv:astro-ph/0507434]. It features an intrinsic X-ray absorber that is unusually dust-free compared to typical active galactic nuclei [arXiv:astro-ph/0507434].

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J030238.16+000203.4 as a blue broad-line quasar at z=1.35 with intrinsic X-ray absorption, high 2-10 keV luminosity, and an unusually dust-free absorber.

quotes.quote: The third object (SDSS J030238.16+000203.4, z=1.35, marked as 3 in the figures) is more unusual, in having an optical spectrum typical of a blue broad line quasar (FWHM(MgII,CIII ] ) 4,000 km s ^ 1 ), with an X-ray spectrum absorbed by an intrinsic column density N_H=2.5_ 0.9 ^ +1.2 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 . The intrinsic 2-10 keV luminosity is 1.5 10^ 44 erg s ^ 1 . The absorber is significantly more dust-free than normal in AGNs, with an upper limit for the dust to gas ratio 1/50 of the Galactic value, compared to typical AGN values of 1/10 (Maccacaro, Perola Elvis 1982, Maiolino et al. 2001). These properties make this object one of the few known high redshift X-ray absorbed quasars.

gmw_00012957.name: SDSS J093533.01+612738.6
gmw_00012957.overview: SDSS J093533.01+612738.6 is a quasar that has been classified at a redshift of z=0.475 [arXiv:astro-ph/0507434]. However, it lacks clear spectral lines in its SDSS spectrum and exhibits a flat X-ray spectrum with a strong emission line consistent with an iron K line at z ~ 1.4 [arXiv:astro-ph/0507434].

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J093533.01+612738.6 as a quasar with an unusually flat X-ray spectrum, no clear SDSS spectral lines, and a strong possible iron K line implying z about 1.4.

quotes.quote: The fourth interesting object is the only one with a spectral index significantly flatter than the average (SDSS J093533.01+612738.6, =0.6 0.2 , Fig. 2, marked as 4 in the figures). This object is classified as a z=0.475 quasar. However, there are no clear spectral lines in the SDSS spectrum, and the X-ray spectrum, in addition to a flat continuum, shows a strong emission line (EW=1.2 0.4 keV) consistent with an iron K line at z 1.4 (Fig. 3). If confirmed, this would be one of the few high redshift Compton thick AGNs (e.g. Norman et al. 2002), and a rare case of a redshift being first determined in X-rays.

gmw_00012958.name: J095254.10+021932.8
gmw_00012958.overview: J095254.10+021932.8 is a quasar at a redshift of z = 2.1473 [arXiv:1310.6575]. Its spectrum shows two newly emerged C IV narrow absorption systems with high velocity offsets that partially occult the background emission [arXiv:1310.6575].

sources.summary: The paper treats J095254.10+021932.8 as a z=2.1473 quasar whose SDSS-III spectrum newly shows two C IV narrow absorption systems with high velocity offsets and partial coverage.
