quotes.quote: The quasar J095254.10+021932.8 with z_ e =2.1473 was observed by SDSS-I/II on 30 December 2000, and reobserved by SDSS-III on 25 March 2011. The time interval of the two observations is 1186.4 days at quasar rest-frame. We find that two C IV 1548,1551 narrow absorption systems, which are respectively located at z_ abs =2.0053 ( R-component ) and z_ abs =1.8011 ( B-component ), are obviously imprinted on the SDSS-III spectrum. However, these two absorption systems can not be observed from the SDSS-I/II spectrum.

With respect to the quasar, the velocities offset is 13,800 km s^ 1 for the R-component , and 34,700 km s^ 1 for the B-component . We also find that both the two absorbers partially occult the background emission sources. The effective coverage fraction is C_ f =0.30 0.05 and C_ f =0.17 0.04 for the R-component and B-component , respectively.

gmw_00012959.name: SDSSJ132012.33+142037.1
gmw_00012959.overview: SDSSJ132012.33+142037.1 is classified as a Population A quasar [arXiv:2205.14499]. In this source, the broad component of the H-beta emission line dominates the profile, with a small, strongly blueshifted component also contributing to the emission [arXiv:2205.14499].

sources.summary: The paper presents SDSSJ132012.33+142037.1 as a Population A quasar example whose H broad component dominates the emission line, with a smaller blueshifted H component showing strong blueward asymmetry.

quotes.quote: We choose to present SDSSJ132012.33+142037.1 as an example of Pop. A and SDSSJ120147.90+120630.2 as Pop. B. As it can be seen, in the case of the Pop. A source the H BC profile represents the majority of the contribution to the full emission line. In this source there is also a small contribution of the H blueshifted component, which presents a strong asymmetry towards lower wavelengths.

gmw_00012960.name: UDF0295
gmw_00012960.overview: UDF0295 is classified as an extremely red object [arXiv:0902.2630]. While most diagnostic methods categorize it as a dusty, star-forming galaxy, its faintness and photometric uncertainty lead some color-color criteria to classify it as an old galaxy [arXiv:0902.2630].

sources.summary: The paper treats UDF0295 as one of four EROs whose classification disagrees by method: most diagnostics call it dusty/star-forming, while the color-color criterion places it near the old-galaxy side, likely because it is faint.

quotes.quote: There are 4 EROs (UDF0295, UDF0565, UDF0629 and UDF0702) which are classified as DGs by the SED fitting method, but are located at the left-hand side of the thick dot-dashed line. These galaxies are all close to the dividing line and very faint in K-band, the photometric uncertainty of those faint EROs may cause this discrepancy.

For 4 EROs (UDF0295, UDF0565, UDF0629 and UDF0702) in our sample, which are classified as DGs by the other 3 methods, but are located at the left-hand side of the discriminating line, and classified as OGs by the (i-K) versus (J-K) method. Considering these EROs are close to the dividing line and faint, the photometric uncertainty of them may cause this discrepancy. We classified them as OGs.

gmw_00012961.name: UDF0565
gmw_00012961.overview: UDF0565 is classified as an extremely red object [arXiv:0902.2630]. While spectral energy distribution fitting indicates it is a dusty, star-forming galaxy, color-color diagnostics alternatively classify it as an old, passive galaxy due to its faintness in the K-band [arXiv:0902.2630].

sources.summary: The paper treats UDF0565 as one of four EROs whose classification disagrees by method: most diagnostics call it dusty/star-forming, while the color-color criterion places it near the old-galaxy side, likely because it is faint.

quotes.quote: There are 4 EROs (UDF0295, UDF0565, UDF0629 and UDF0702) which are classified as DGs by the SED fitting method, but are located at the left-hand side of the thick dot-dashed line. These galaxies are all close to the dividing line and very faint in K-band, the photometric uncertainty of those faint EROs may cause this discrepancy.

For 4 EROs (UDF0295, UDF0565, UDF0629 and UDF0702) in our sample, which are classified as DGs by the other 3 methods, but are located at the left-hand side of the discriminating line, and classified as OGs by the (i-K) versus (J-K) method. Considering these EROs are close to the dividing line and faint, the photometric uncertainty of them may cause this discrepancy. We classified them as OGs.

gmw_00012962.name: UDF0629
gmw_00012962.overview: UDF0629 is classified as an extremely red object [arXiv:0902.2630]. While spectral energy distribution fitting classifies it as a dusty, star-forming galaxy, color-color diagnostics instead place it near the old galaxy population due to its faintness in the K-band [arXiv:0902.2630].

sources.summary: The paper treats UDF0629 as one of four EROs whose classification disagrees by method: most diagnostics call it dusty/star-forming, while the color-color criterion places it near the old-galaxy side, likely because it is faint.

quotes.quote: There are 4 EROs (UDF0295, UDF0565, UDF0629 and UDF0702) which are classified as DGs by the SED fitting method, but are located at the left-hand side of the thick dot-dashed line. These galaxies are all close to the dividing line and very faint in K-band, the photometric uncertainty of those faint EROs may cause this discrepancy.

For 4 EROs (UDF0295, UDF0565, UDF0629 and UDF0702) in our sample, which are classified as DGs by the other 3 methods, but are located at the left-hand side of the discriminating line, and classified as OGs by the (i-K) versus (J-K) method. Considering these EROs are close to the dividing line and faint, the photometric uncertainty of them may cause this discrepancy. We classified them as OGs.

gmw_00012963.name: Seyfert galaxy at z =0.157
gmw_00012963.overview: This object is a Seyfert galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.157 [arXiv:1112.5049]. It acts as a soft X-ray point source situated near the galaxy cluster RXCJ2149.9-1859 [arXiv:1112.5049].

sources.summary: The paper treats the coordinate-identified Seyfert galaxy near RXCJ2149.9-1859 as a soft X-ray contaminating point source, giving its redshift and coordinates and describing how its emission was deblended from the cluster.

quotes.quote: The cluster, RXCJ2149.9-1859, contains an X-ray point source that is offset by about 2.4 arcmin from the X-ray centre of the cluster. The point source is significantly softer than the cluster emission and originates from a Seyfert galaxy at z =0.157. It is located at 21:49:58 in RA and -18:59:24 in Dec and is denoted by an asterisk in the lower panels of Fig. 3. The flux from this source was deblended from the cluster emission.

The contribution of the Seyfert galaxy to the cluster X-ray emission is clearly noticeable as a much softer source than the cluster emission north-east of the cluster centre. It was deblended by cutting off the contaminated sector of the cluster emission and filling it with the remaining azimuthal average.

gmw_00012964.name: J022836.08+000939.2
gmw_00012964.overview: J022836.08+000939.2 is a quasar [arXiv:2105.12599]. The object was observed multiple times by the Baryon Oscillation Spectroscopic Survey (BOSS) and previously during the SDSS-I project [arXiv:2105.12599].

sources.summary: The paper uses eight BOSS observations of J022836.08+000939.2 as a correction test case; its corrected BOSS spectrum agrees with SDSS-I, and wavelength-dependent corrections visibly change the spectral shapes.

quotes.quote: fig:spec_corr shows the BOSS observation of J022836.08+000939.2 (plate 3647, MJD 56596, fibre 776, 4 15 minute exposures, co-added), before and after correcting for the fibre offset problem. The SDSS-I observation is also shown (unaffected by the fibre offset problem, plate 2635, MJD 54114, fibre 621). It can be seen that the corrected BOSS spectrum is in very good agreement with the SDSS-I spectrum.

fig:spec_obs illustrates all eight BOSS observations of J022836.08+000939.2, before and after applying our correction for the fibre offset problem. The correction functions for this set of observations are shown in fig:corr , which shows clearly that the effect of wavelength dependence is significant. The effect the corrections have on the spectral shapes of the observations is evident from fig:corr ; the corrections lower the fluxes at wavelengths 4538 , while increasing the fluxes at wavelengths 4538 .

gmw_00012965.name: J022954.42-005622.5
gmw_00012965.overview: J022954.42-005622.5 is a quasar [arXiv:2105.12599]. Its spectral data consistency is improved by applying Margala, constant-seeing, and DR16 corrections [arXiv:2105.12599].

sources.summary: For J022954.42-005622.5, Margala and constant-seeing corrections improve consistency, the wavelength-dependent correction does not improve on them, and DR16 performs best by improving the highest wavelength regions.

quotes.quote: J022954.42-005622.5, Table tab:consistency_75018778 : The Margala correction and our =const. correction again perform very similarly, both improving on the uncorrected data consistency. Curiously (and unlike J022836.08+000939.2), our =f( ) correction fails to improve on the two =const. corrections (with no obvious explanation). Moreover, the DR16 correction makes little or no improvement in the lowest two wavelength regions, but clearly improves consistency in the two highest wavelength regions. Overall, the DR16 correction again works best.

gmw_00012966.name: J023308.31-002605.0
gmw_00012966.overview: J023308.31-002605.0 is a quasar [arXiv:2105.12599]. Spectroscopic observations of this object have been analyzed using various correction methods, including the Margala, constant-seeing, and DR16 corrections, to improve the consistency of its uncorrected spectra [arXiv:2105.12599].

sources.summary: For J023308.31-002605.0, both Margala and constant-seeing corrections improve the uncorrected spectra, the wavelength-dependent version helps at higher wavelengths, and DR16 ranks as the best correction.

quotes.quote: J023308.31-002605.0, Table tab:consistency_827763272 : Again, the Margala correction and our =const. correction again perform very similarly, both significantly improving on the uncorrected case. Our =f( ) correction is marginally worse than the two =const. methods for the lowest wavelength region but improves consistency for three higher wavelength regions. The ordering of worst to best correction is again (Margala + our =const. method), our =f( ) , and DR16.

gmw_00012967.name: J023259.60+004801.7
gmw_00012967.overview: J023259.60+004801.7 is a quasar that exhibits anomalous spectral behavior [arXiv:2105.12599]. Applying fiber-offset corrections to its individual spectra worsens their agreement, with the degradation becoming more pronounced at longer wavelengths [arXiv:2105.12599].

sources.summary: J023259.60+004801.7 behaves anomalously: all four fibre-offset corrections worsen agreement between its individual spectra, with uncorrected spectra most consistent and the degradation increasing with wavelength.

quotes.quote: J023259.60+004801.7, Table tab:consistency_110814178 : The results from this quasar gives peculiar results. All four fibre offset correction methods make the agreement between individual spectra conspicuously worse. The individual spectra are most consistent when no correction at all is applied. This can be seen clearly in fig:J023259correctionsB . The effect gets worse as wavelength increases.

gmw_00012968.name: J1310+3233
gmw_00012968.overview: J1310+3233 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar located at a redshift of 1.6 [arXiv:1411.3431]. Its unfiltered light curve shows a long-term rise in luminosity, along with possible flux variations on a timescale of days [arXiv:1411.3431].

sources.summary: The paper presents J1310+3233 as a z=1.6 flat-spectrum radio quasar whose unfiltered light curve shows a long-term rise and possible day-scale variation relevant to accretion-disk emission.

quotes.quote: Figure 7 shows the light variations of a flat-spectrum radio quasar (FSRQ) J1310+3233 with a redshift of 1.6 heal08 . The unfiltered light curve indicates that, the luminosity of this quasar has a long-term rise with a possible variation on a time scale of days. Analysis of this light variation could help to understand the flux contribution of the accretion disk to the quasar emission at different timescales.

gmw_00012969.name: SDSS J085543.40-001517.7
gmw_00012969.overview: SDSS J085543.40-001517.7 is a bright quasar located at a redshift of 2.427 [arXiv:1006.0143, arXiv:1005.5499]. It hosts a central black hole with a mass of approximately 1.4 to 3.9 billion solar masses and exhibits a high bolometric luminosity [arXiv:1005.5499].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J085543.40-001517.7 as a bright z=2.427 LAMOST quasar in the redshift desert, with properties included here and near-IR colors useful for recovering such optically star-like quasars.

quotes.quote: Among 8 new quasars, SDSS J085543.40-001517.7 is a very bright one ( i=16.44 ) and was identified as a z=2.427 quasar. This is the first quasar in the redshift desert discovered by LAMOST and its detailed properties have been reported in paper I. For the completeness, we also include some of its properties in this paper.

We noticed that two of eight new quasars have redshifts larger than 2.2. Besides SDSS J085543.40-001517.7 ( z=2.427 ) (see Paper I), SDSS J090504.87+000800.5 ( z=2.773 ) is also a quasar in the 'redshift desert'. These quasars are very difficult to be identified because of their similar optical colors as stars. However, they can be recovered by using the near-IR colors.

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J085543.40-001517.7 as a newly confirmed z=2.427 quasar, exceptionally bright in i band, missed by SDSS optical colors, with a very massive central black hole and high bolometric luminosity.

quotes.quote: A quasar candidate, SDSS J085543.40-001517.7, which was selected by a recently proposed criterion involving near-IR Y-K and optical g-z colors, was identified spectroscopically as a new quasar with redshift of 2.427 by the LAMOST commissioning observation in December 2009 and confirmed by the observation made with the NAOC/Xinglong 2.16m telescope in March 2010. This quasar was not targeted in the SDSS spectroscopic survey because it locates in the stellar locus of the optical color-color diagrams, while it is clearly separated from stars in the Y-K vs. g-z diagram. Comparing with other SDSS quasars we found this new quasar with i magnitude of 16.44 is apparently the brightest one in the redshift range from 2.3 to 2.7. From the spectral properties we derived its central black hole mass as (1.4 3.9) 10^ 10 M_ and the bolometric luminosity as 3.7 10^ 48 , which indicates that this new quasar is intrinsically very bright and belongs to the most luminous quasars in the universe.

gmw_00012970.name: SDSS J090504.87+000800.5
gmw_00012970.overview: SDSS J090504.87+000800.5 is a quasar located at a redshift of z=2.773 [arXiv:1006.0143]. Because its optical colors are highly similar to those of stars, it is classified as a "redshift desert" quasar that is difficult to identify without near-infrared color selection [arXiv:1006.0143].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J090504.87+000800.5 as a z=2.773 redshift-desert quasar, difficult to find from optical colors but recoverable and separated from stars using near-IR/optical color selection.

quotes.quote: We noticed that two of eight new quasars have redshifts larger than 2.2. Besides SDSS J085543.40-001517.7 ( z=2.427 ) (see Paper I), SDSS J090504.87+000800.5 ( z=2.773 ) is also a quasar in the 'redshift desert'. These quasars are very difficult to be identified because of their similar optical colors as stars. However, they can be recovered by using the near-IR colors.

Obviously two quasars with redshifts larger than 2.2 locate in the stellar locus in all three optical color-color diagrams, but are separated from stars in the Y-K vs. g-z diagram and meets the selection criterion proposed by Wu Jia (2010).

gmw_00012971.name: N1-008
gmw_00012971.overview: N1-008 is a bright 170-micron infrared source [arXiv:astro-ph/9910428]. Optical imaging of its error circle reveals a very bright, extended spiral galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/9910428].

sources.summary: The paper describes N1-008 as the brightest 170 micron source in the sample but hard to fit, with unexpectedly low SCUBA flux and a bright spiral counterpart in the error circle.

quotes.quote: The object in our sub-sample which is brightest at 170 , N1-008, is hard to fit with any reasonable SED; it has approximately half the 850 \ and 450 \ flux density expected from even a z = 0 source with the same 170 \ flux density. This suggests that there might be more than one source contributing to the ISOPHOT flux, which is not particularly unlikely, since the FIRBACK Survey is operating near the confusion limit. On the other hand, optical images show little in the error circle except for a very bright obvious spiral galaxy, and so from that point of view this is not a case where we expect more than one source in the ISOPHOT beam.

Another possibility is that our JCMT beam did not include all the flux, since optically this object appears quite extended. However, we would expect the sub-mm emission to be more concentrated than the optical, and hence we are likely to have included most of the dust emission within the beam. This object will be discussed more extensively in Lagache et al. (in preparation).

gmw_00012972.name: N1-034
gmw_00012972.overview: N1-034 is an infrared galaxy that exhibits a relatively poor spectral energy distribution fit [arXiv:astro-ph/9910428]. This poor fit is attributed to either low SCUBA flux densities relative to ISOPHOT, or high SCUBA 450-micron flux densities compared to 850-micron flux densities [arXiv:astro-ph/9910428].

sources.summary: The paper reports a poor SED fit for N1-034, attributing it to unusual SCUBA versus ISOPHOT fluxes or 450-to-850 micron behavior that requires better data.

quotes.quote: Relatively poor values of ^2 are also found for N1-034 and N1-063. These result either from low SCUBA flux densities relative to ISOPHOT, or somewhat high SCUBA 450 \ flux densities compared with 850 . Higher SNR data, or data at additional wavelengths are required to determine whether these flux densities come from multiple objects, complex SEDs, or simply the low SNRs of the current data for these objects.

gmw_00012973.name: N1-038
gmw_00012973.overview: N1-038 is a strong 850-micron submillimeter source [arXiv:astro-ph/9910428]. Photometric estimates from submillimeter and far-infrared data imply a redshift range of 0.66 to 1.23, which is broadly consistent with a radio-to-far-infrared redshift estimate of 1.2 [arXiv:astro-ph/9910428].

sources.summary: The paper identifies N1-038 as a strong 850 micron source with an implied redshift range of 0.66-1.23; radio/far-infrared estimates give z=1.2 and agree broadly.

quotes.quote: We find that the objects with the highest 850 \ flux densities have the highest implied redshifts: e.g. \ 0.66 < z < 1.23 for N1-038.

Using the 20 cm VLA data from Ciliegi et al. (1999) and the explicit correlation (using = 1.5 , T_ d = 40 K) from Carilli Yun (1999), we find z = 0.6 , 1.2, 1.1 and 1.4 for N1-008, N1-038, N1-061 and N1-063, respectively (our four 850 \ detections). Except for N1-008 these are in broad agreement with the values obtained from the sub-mm and far-IR data alone.

gmw_00012974.name: N1-063
gmw_00012974.overview: N1-063 is an infrared galaxy with an estimated redshift of z = 1.4, derived from radio and far-infrared data [arXiv:astro-ph/9910428]. Photometric observations of the galaxy show a relatively poor spectral energy distribution fit, which may stem from low submillimeter flux densities [arXiv:astro-ph/9910428].

sources.summary: The paper finds N1-063 has a poor SED fit from SCUBA/ISOPHOT flux behavior, needs better data, and has a radio/far-infrared redshift estimate of z=1.4.

quotes.quote: Relatively poor values of ^2 are also found for N1-034 and N1-063. These result either from low SCUBA flux densities relative to ISOPHOT, or somewhat high SCUBA 450 \ flux densities compared with 850 . Higher SNR data, or data at additional wavelengths are required to determine whether these flux densities come from multiple objects, complex SEDs, or simply the low SNRs of the current data for these objects.

Using the 20 cm VLA data from Ciliegi et al. (1999) and the explicit correlation (using = 1.5 , T_ d = 40 K) from Carilli Yun (1999), we find z = 0.6 , 1.2, 1.1 and 1.4 for N1-008, N1-038, N1-061 and N1-063, respectively (our four 850 \ detections). Except for N1-008 these are in broad agreement with the values obtained from the sub-mm and far-IR data alone.

gmw_00012975.name: host of GRB 071117
gmw_00012975.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 071117 is located very close, at a distance of approximately 2 arcseconds, to a neighboring red galaxy [arXiv:1701.02312]. Because of this proximity, Spitzer observations could only establish an upper limit on the host galaxy's infrared flux [arXiv:1701.02312].

sources.summary: The paper says the host of GRB 071117 is close to a red galaxy, has only an infrared flux upper limit, and received SED fitting plus VLT/X-shooter spectroscopy.

quotes.quote: The host of GRB 071117 lies very close ( 2'' ) to a red galaxy, and, therefore, the spatial resolution of the Spitzer observations allowed us to obtain only an upper limit on its infrared flux. We therefore also performed a spectral energy distribution fitting using the host galaxy photometry (see Table 2) following the same prescriptions as , and found log(M _ / M _ ) 9.9 .

The SFR values were taken from with the exception of the host galaxies of GRB 061007, GRB 061121 and GRB 071117, not included in that work. We obtained the VLT/X-shooter spectroscopy of these three host galaxies (ESO programs 095.D-0560 and 085.A-0795, PI: S.D. Vergani and H. Flores, respectively). We processed the spectra using version 2.6.0 of the X-shooter data reduction pipeline Modigliani2010 , following the procedures described in .

gmw_00012976.name: GRB 061007
gmw_00012976.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 061007 has been observed using VLT/X-shooter spectroscopy [arXiv:1701.02312]. This spectroscopic data allowed researchers to measure emission-line fluxes to determine the galaxy's star formation rate and metallicity [arXiv:1701.02312].

sources.summary: The paper treats the GRB 061007 host as one of three hosts lacking prior SFR values, then reports VLT/X-shooter spectroscopy, emission-line fluxes, and derived SFR and metallicity.

quotes.quote: The SFR values were taken from with the exception of the host galaxies of GRB 061007, GRB 061121 and GRB 071117, not included in that work. We obtained the VLT/X-shooter spectroscopy of these three host galaxies (ESO programs 095.D-0560 and 085.A-0795, PI: S.D. Vergani and H. Flores, respectively). We processed the spectra using version 2.6.0 of the X-shooter data reduction pipeline Modigliani2010 , following the procedures described in .

The measured emission line fluxes are reported in Table tabflux . We determine the SFR from the fluxes (corrected by the extinction determined through the Balmer ratio), with the same prescriptions as . Following the same prescription as in , we determined the metallicity of the objects in the sample with the method on the strong emission line fluxes reported in the literature Piranomonte2015,Kruhler2015 or on those measured by us; in the relevant cases, the results are consistent within errors to those already reported in the literature.

gmw_00012977.name: GRB 050802
gmw_00012977.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 050802 is a faint galaxy with a low stellar mass below 10^9.2 solar masses [arXiv:1701.02312]. Based on its star formation rate limits, the galaxy has an estimated metallicity limit of 12+(O/H)<8.4 [arXiv:1701.02312].

sources.summary: The paper describes the GRB 050802 host as faint, not a dark-GRB host, below 10^9.2 solar masses, with SFR limits and an FMR metallicity limit of 12+(O/H)<8.4.

quotes.quote: We stress that the five galaxies in the sample for which we could not determine the metallicity (GRB 050318, GRB 050525, GRB 050802, GRB 060908, and GRB 091208B) are all faint galaxies, not hosting dark GRBs, and with stellar masses log(M _ * / M _ ) <9.2 (three of these galaxies have log(M _ * / M _ ) <8.7 ; see Vergani2015,Perley2016 ). A super-solar metallicity for a large portion of these host galaxies is therefore extremely unlikely. For two of these galaxies (GRB 050525 and GRB 050802) SFR limits are available ( Japelj2016 ; Palmerio et al. in preparation). Under the conservative hypothesis that they follow the FMR relation, we can derive limits on their metallicities from their SFR and stellar masses of 12 + ( O / H )<8.1, 8.4 , respectively.

gmw_00012978.name: SDSS J0909 + 4449
aliases_external: SDSS J0909+4449 lensed quasar system, image C reference position
gmw_00012978.overview: SDSS J0909 + 4449 is a strongly lensed quasar system at a redshift of z=2.788 [arXiv:1809.07337]. The system features three detected quasar images with a maximum separation larger than 10 arcseconds, which are lensed by a galaxy group at a redshift of 0.7 to 1.0 [arXiv:1809.07337].

sources.summary: The paper confirms SDSS J0909 + 4449 as a z=2.788 strongly lensed quasar system with three detected images, likely lensed by a z=0.7-1.0 galaxy group and possibly triply or quadruply imaged.

quotes.quote: The imaging and spectroscopic data jointly suggest that quasars A, B, and C are lensed images of a single quasar at z=2.788 . Their relatively large separations imply a group-scale or cluster-scale lens. This is supported by the presence of several extended objects with similar red colors that are classified as galaxies morphologically in the Data Release 6 (DR6) of the Legacy Surveys, namely G1--G6 (ordered by their z band brightness).

In this Letter, we present an analysis of SDSS J0909 + 4449, a system consisting of three z=2.788 quasars with nearly identical spectral shapes that are separated by up to 14 in projection. We confirm that SDSS J0909 + 4449 is a strongly lensed quasar because the positions of the three quasar images can be well reproduced by simple lens models. A group of galaxies at redshifts of 0.7--1.0 are believed to be associated with the lens. Among 200 known strongly lensed quasars to date, SDSS J0909 + 4449 is the fourth system to have a maximum image separation larger than 10 . Based on current data, it is still unclear whether SDSS J0909 + 4449 is triply imaged in a naked-cusp configuration, which makes it the third strongly lensed quasar with exactly three lensed images, or quadruply imaged with the fourth lensed image undetected.

gmw_00012979.name: G4
gmw_00012979.overview: The galaxy G4 is identified as a possible primary gravitational lens object [arXiv:1809.07337]. However, a lens model centered on G4 is less favored because it requires an unusually strong external shear and predicts a brighter undetected image [arXiv:1809.07337].

sources.summary: G4 is considered as a possible primary lens galaxy, but its centered model is less favored because it needs stronger external shear and predicts a brighter undetected image.

quotes.quote: To mitigate the problem of having more free parameters than constraints, we consider three sets of lens models with centers of the mass component fixed to the light centers of G4, G5, and FG, three galaxies that could possibly be the primary lens object, and use the lensmodel toolkit Keeton01 to verify whether any set of the model can fit the data.

A lens model with mass center fixed at G4 can also provide a good fit to the data, but with a slightly larger ^2 value (0.8). Moreover, it requires an unusually strong external shear (strength=0.57) and predicts D to be 40 brighter than in the above model, which make this model less favorable.

gmw_00012980.name: G5
gmw_00012980.overview: The galaxy G5 is identified as a possible primary gravitational lens object [arXiv:1809.07337]. A singular isothermal ellipsoid lens model with external shear centered on G5 successfully fits a three-image lensed configuration [arXiv:1809.07337].

sources.summary: G5 is treated as a possible primary lens galaxy; the G5-centered model fits the three-image hypothesis and is also used to test the four-image hypothesis.

quotes.quote: To mitigate the problem of having more free parameters than constraints, we consider three sets of lens models with centers of the mass component fixed to the light centers of G4, G5, and FG, three galaxies that could possibly be the primary lens object, and use the lensmodel toolkit Keeton01 to verify whether any set of the model can fit the data.
We find that only the lens model with mass center fixed at G5 can produce exactly three lensed images at locations that match the data well ( ^2 10^ 5 ).
We again use the SIE + external shear lens model with mass center fixed at G5 to test this hypothesis.

gmw_00012981.name: FG
gmw_00012981.overview: FG is a spectroscopically confirmed elliptical galaxy located in the foreground of a lensing system [arXiv:1809.07337]. Although it was evaluated as a potential primary lens object, a lens model centered on FG fails to provide a good fit to the data [arXiv:1809.07337].

sources.summary: FG is identified as a spectroscopically confirmed foreground elliptical and tested as a possible primary lens galaxy, but the FG-centered lens model fails to fit the system.

quotes.quote: Galaxy FG is spectroscopically confirmed as an elliptical galaxy in the foreground ( z=0.433 ) by the BOSS survey Dawson13 .

To mitigate the problem of having more free parameters than constraints, we consider three sets of lens models with centers of the mass component fixed to the light centers of G4, G5, and FG, three galaxies that could possibly be the primary lens object, and use the lensmodel toolkit Keeton01 to verify whether any set of the model can fit the data.
A lens model with mass center fixed at FG fails to provide a good fit.

gmw_00012982.name: source 22
gmw_00012982.overview: Source 22 is an optically selected quasar located at a redshift of z=0.82 [arXiv:astro-ph/0406345]. It is the only source in its sample to show X-ray obscuration at the 90 percent confidence level, and its optical spectrum displays a magnesium II absorption line that may indicate an outflow [arXiv:astro-ph/0406345].

sources.summary: The paper identifies source 22 as the only X-ray detected optically selected QSO significantly absorbed with flatter photon index, estimating high rest-frame column density and noting an optical Mg II absorption line possibly tied to X-ray absorption.

quotes.quote: There is a strong evidence for obscuration only in one case (source 22).

Using a flatter photon index, =1.7 , only one source ( 22) remains significantly absorbed at the 90 per cent confidence level. The rest frame column density of this object is estimated to be N_H=12.9^ +8.5 _ 7.0 10^ 22 cm^ 2 at z=0.82 . In Figure optical , plotting the (not calibrated in flux) 2QZ optical spectrum of this source, there is evidence for an absorption line at 4800 . Assuming this feature is intrinsic to the QSO this is then consistent with Mg ii absorption at an outflow velocity of 30 000 km/s. This absorption line, if real, may be directly associated with the material responsible for the X-ray absorption (e.g. Brandt et al. 2000).

gmw_00012983.name: source 38
gmw_00012983.overview: Source 38 is a 2QZ quasar that exhibits a broad Mg II absorption feature in its optical spectrum [arXiv:astro-ph/0406345]. This absorption feature may indicate that the object is a low-ionization broad-absorption line quasar with possible X-ray obscuration [arXiv:astro-ph/0406345].

sources.summary: The paper describes source 38 as a 2QZ QSO with possible X-ray obscuration, a broad Mg II absorption feature, and possible low-ionization BAL nature despite uncertain X-ray evidence.

quotes.quote: Only one source ( 38 in Table 1) has _ ox 2 and therefore could be significantly X-ray obscured. Unfortunately, the poor photon statistics (38 counts) do not allow us to firmly establish whether this source is obscured. The optical spectrum (not calibrated in flux) of this source however, presented in Figure optical1 , shows a broad absorption feature at 6200 . This can be interpreted as the Mg ii absorption line at an outflow velocity of 30 000 km/s that may be directly associated with any X-ray absorbing material.

We note that the strong Mg ii absorption feature of source 38 may indicate a low-ionization BAL QSO. This class of sources is believed to have high column densities associated with Compton-thick AGNs (Gallagher et al. 2002).

gmw_00012984.name: Source 106 of Mil2013
gmw_00012984.overview: Source 106 of Mil2013 is a radio-loud active galactic nucleus located within the infrared color region typical of elliptical galaxies [arXiv:1603.07140]. Very Long Baseline Interferometry observations detected a counterpart to its radio emission, with the missing radio flux likely representing resolved structures such as jets [arXiv:1603.07140].

sources.summary: The paper classifies Source 106 of Mil2013 as a radio-loud AGN in an elliptical-galaxy color region, detects a VLBI counterpart, and attributes missing radio flux to resolved AGN structures such as jets.

quotes.quote: Source 106 of Mil2013 is classified as a RL AGN by Bon2013 because of its high radio excess ( q_ 24 obs = 0.99 0.18; Lut2011 ) and its location in the IRAC colour-colour diagram, where it lies well within the region occupied by elliptical galaxies ( Log( S_ 5.8 m /S_ 3.6 m ) = 0.48 0.04; Log( S_ 8.0 m /S_ 4.5 m ) = 0.52 0.05 ; Lon2003, Sur2005 ). We found a VLBI counterpart (Fig. fig:Detections , left) coincident with the VLA position, with a peak flux density of 155 Jy (S/N ratio 9.1), or 43 of the 360 Jy measured by the VLA Mil2013 . The missing radio flux presumably represents resolved AGN structures, such as jets.

gmw_00012985.name: Source 851 of Mil2013
gmw_00012985.overview: Source 851 of Mil2013 is classified as a radio-quiet active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1603.07140]. It features a tentative VLBI counterpart that is offset by 0.1 arcseconds from its VLA position [arXiv:1603.07140].

sources.summary: The paper classifies Source 851 of Mil2013 as a radio-quiet AGN without radio excess and reports a tentative VLBI counterpart offset 0.1 arcseconds from the VLA position.

quotes.quote: Source 851 of Mil2013 is classified as a RQ AGN by Bon2013 because it has no radio excess ( q_ 24 obs = 0.34 0.03; Lon2003, Sur2005 ) and is located in the the AGN wedge of the IRAC colour-colour diagram ( Log( S_ 5.8 m /S_ 3.6 m ) = 0.26 0.02; Log( S_ 8.0 m /S_ 4.5 m ) = 0.14 0.01; Lon2003, Sur2005 ). We detected a VLBI counterpart (Fig. fig:Detections , centre) 0.1" away from the VLA position, with a peak flux density of 110 Jy, or 50 of the 222 Jy measured by the VLA Mil2013 . Our VLBI detection has a S/N ratio 5.5, and we therefore consider this detection as tentative.

gmw_00012986.name: Source 76 of Mil2013
gmw_00012986.overview: Source 76 of Mil2013 is classified as a radio-quiet active galactic nucleus with a high X-ray luminosity [arXiv:1603.07140]. It contains a detected VLBI counterpart with a positional offset that may be caused by extended emission, such as an active galactic nucleus jet [arXiv:1603.07140].

sources.summary: The paper classifies Source 76 of Mil2013 as a radio-quiet AGN with powerful X-ray emission, detects a VLBI counterpart, and argues its offset may reflect extended AGN-jet emission.

quotes.quote: Source 76 of Mil2013 is classified as a RQ AGN by Bon2013 because it has no radio excess ( q_ 24 obs = 0.53 0.03 ; Lon2003, Sur2005 ), a powerful X-ray luminosity ( L_ 2-8 keV = (4.2 0.3) 10^ 44 erg s ^ 1 ; Leh2005 ), and is located in the AGN wedge of the IRAC colour-colour diagram ( Log( S_ 5.8 m /S_ 3.6 m ) = 0.50 0.03; Log( S_ 8.0 m /S_ 4.5 m ) = 0.60 0.02; Lon2003, Sur2005 ). We detected a VLBI counterpart (Fig. fig:Detections , right) 1.2" away from the VLA position, with a peak flux density of 186 Jy (S/N ratio 8.1), or 69 of the 269 Jy of the VLA flux density Mil2013 . The offset between the two radio positions is significant, but our detection lies inside the (large) positional error ellipse of the radio source (2.9 1.6 arcsec ^2 ) reported by Mil2013 , is only 0.77" from the HST counterpart Rix2004 , and there is no other radio emission detected by the VLA, which is consistent with the measured VLBI detection. We therefore conclude that the VLBI detection is unambiguously the same source as the VLA detection, and the offset may be caused by extended emission, such as an AGN jet.

gmw_00012987.name: Source 728 of Mil2013
gmw_00012987.overview: Source 728 of Mil2013 is associated with a starburst galaxy [arXiv:1603.07140]. Although previously classified as a radio-loud active galactic nucleus due to strong X-ray emission and a radio excess, it lacks a VLBI counterpart and is likely misclassified [arXiv:1603.07140].

sources.summary: The paper classifies Source 728 of Mil2013 as a radio-loud AGN but finds no VLBI counterpart, notes its starburst-galaxy association near the radio/FIR correlation, and argues it is likely misclassified.

quotes.quote: Source 728 of Mil2013 is classified as a RL AGN by Bon2013 because of a slight radio excess, strong X-ray emission, and location in the the AGN wedge of the IRAC colour-colour diagram. We found no VLBI counterpart down to a 3 flux density level of 109 Jy, or 30 of the VLA flux density of 326 Jy Mil2013 .

It is also interesting to note that our undetected RL AGN (RL728) is associated with an SB galaxy and lies close to the radio/FIR correlation, based on the new FIR SFR estimation of Bon2015 . We can therefore argue that this is most likely a misclassified source.

gmw_00012988.name: RX J1031.6
gmw_00012988.overview: RX J1031.6 is an optical galaxy at a redshift of 0.2441 that is situated within a loose group [arXiv:astro-ph/0106248]. The galaxy's point-like X-ray emission is dominated by an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0106248], which exhibits flux variability and suffers from absorption by its host galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0106248].

sources.summary: The paper identifies RX J1031.6 as a point-like X-ray source coincident with an optical galaxy and dominated by an AGN; its X-rays suffer host-galaxy absorption, and archival ROSAT data show variability.

quotes.quote: L emonon ( ) analyzed the radial profile of RX J1031.6 obtained with the ROSAT HRI and found that the profile is consistent with a point source which coincides with an optical galaxy at a redshift of 0.2441, which has been measured during a spectroscopic run at the ESO 3.6m telescope (Pierre et al ). This suggests that the X-ray emission is dominated by an AGN, and consequently, that the object was misclassified as a ``bright" cluster from the low resolution RASS data. From the optical point of view, this cluster indeed appears as a loose group.

The N_ H value obtained here is much larger than the Galactic value (5.3 20 cm ^ 2 ; Dickey Lockman ), and this means that the X-ray emission from the AGN suffers absorption due to its host galaxy. There are two ROSAT HRI archival data of RX J1031.6. Each of them was observed on May 25, 1996, and Dec. 19, 1996. We analyzed them and found that the HRI counting rate of RX J1031.6 was ( 4.0 0.5 ) 2   s ^ 1 on May 25, 1996, and ( 2.4 0.4 ) 2   s ^ 1 on Dec. 19, 1999. Assuming the best-fitting power-law model to the ASCA data, these counting rates correspond to the fluxes of ( 2.8 0.4 ) 12  erg s ^ 1  cm ^ 2 and ( 1.7 0.3 ) 12  erg s ^ 1  cm ^ 2 in the 0.5 -- 10 keV band.

gmw_00012989.name: J0748-26B
gmw_00012989.overview: J0748-26B is a galaxy analyzed to demonstrate sky correction techniques for large-galaxy photometry [arXiv:1406.5918]. Its H-band intensity profile has been modeled using a fit from the profile minimum to its outer edges to establish a corrected profile [arXiv:1406.5918].

sources.summary: The paper uses J0748-26B to illustrate large-galaxy sky correction: its H-band profile has uncorrected and corrected versions, a fitted outer profile region, and a measured median sky level.

quotes.quote: As an example for one galaxy (J0748-26B), Fig.  fig:selfcor shows the H band intensity profile. The red profile is uncorrected. The blue line shows the fit that has been made from the minimum in the profile to the outer edges.
The fit region is demarcated by two vertical lines. The horizontal line shows the measured median sky value. The blue profile shows the corrected intensity profile.

gmw_00012990.name: galaxy F
gmw_00012990.overview: Galaxy F is classified as a merger by both major and minor merger diagnostic techniques [arXiv:2303.06249]. Visually, it appears to be a post-coalescence merger featuring a low-surface-brightness shell and high shape asymmetry [arXiv:2303.06249].

sources.summary: The paper describes galaxy F as a merger flagged by both major and minor classifications, with high shape asymmetry, a low-surface-brightness shell, and a post-coalescence merger appearance.

quotes.quote: We show an example of two diagnostic diagrams in Figure fig:1237653589018018166 for the major (top) and minor (bottom) merger classifications for galaxy F from Figure fig:distabc . This galaxy is classified as a merger by both major and minor merger fiducial classifications, with high LD1 and corresponding p_ merg values in the upper left informational panel. The lower panel on the left hand image lists the three leading terms and their corresponding contribution to the value of LD1; here, shape asymmetry and asymmetry are important predictors for both classifications.

Looking first at the major merger panels, shape asymmetry followed by the A_s*A cross term are the most influential terms. In the right panel, the asymmetry for this galaxy is low while the shape asymmetry is high. This is due to the low surface brightness of the shell feature.

By eye, this galaxy looks like a post-coalescence merger with a shell from the merger event; the minor merger technique is relying both upon the high concentration (also measured by M_ 20 ) and the shell feature to identify it as a merger. This galaxy and others like it demonstrate that the LDA classification succeeds in the case of concentrated early-type galaxies.

gmw_00012991.name: J154925.24-003717.8
gmw_00012991.overview: J154925.24-003717.8 is a galaxy identified as an outlier in the luminosity-metallicity distribution [arXiv:1206.6300]. It is the faintest galaxy in its sample, with an estimated lower-branch oxygen abundance of 8.14 [arXiv:1206.6300].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J154925.24-003717.8 as an L-Z outlier, noting it is the faintest and most prominent outlier and that lower-branch oxygen-abundance calibration gives 8.14.

quotes.quote: J154925.24-003717.8 and J003806.59+000421.9 are two outliers in the L-Z distribution.

The most prominent outlier is the faintest galaxy of the sample, for which the assumption of an upper O/H branch could be incorrect skillman89 . Adopting the calibration of kewleydopita2002 for the lower branch, we obtained a value of =8.14 .

gmw_00012992.name: J003806.59+000421.9
gmw_00012992.overview: J003806.59+000421.9 is a galaxy identified as an outlier in the luminosity-metallicity distribution [arXiv:1206.6300]. It exhibits an extremely low gas-phase metallicity, which is likely caused by an underestimation of its absorption-line correction [arXiv:1206.6300].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J003806.59+000421.9 as the second L-Z outlier, with an extremely low gas-phase metallicity probably caused by underestimated absorption-line correction.

quotes.quote: J154925.24-003717.8 and J003806.59+000421.9 are two outliers in the L-Z distribution.
The second outlier has an extremely low value of the gas-phase metallicity. This is probably due to an underestimation of the \ absorption-line correction.

gmw_00012993.name: GMRT J033152.9 100843
gmw_00012993.overview: GMRT J033152.9 100843 is a candidate ultra-steep spectrum high-redshift radio galaxy [arXiv:1110.2467]. Optical imaging down to an R-band magnitude of 23 detected no counterpart, suggesting the galaxy is likely located at a redshift greater than 0.5 [arXiv:1110.2467].

sources.summary: The paper treats GMRT J033152.9 100843 as a USS high-redshift radio-galaxy candidate; Liverpool Telescope GRI imaging found no optical counterpart, and K-z arguments suggest z>0.5.

quotes.quote: These sources are possibly high-redshift radio galaxies but optical identification is required for clarification. The follow-up observations were conducted with the Liverpool Telescope; in all cases no sources are detected down to an R magnitude of 23. By applying models and using the K-z relation we are able to suggest that these sources are possibly at high redshift.

Fig. LT_1 shows R band images of GMRT J033152.9 100843, VLSS J033315.9 085119 and GMRT J033326.6 084205. In all the observing bands no source can be seen at the location of the radio source. Deeper high-resolution multi-band optical observations are required to detect the optical counterparts of these sources and especially to resolve and characterise them.

We have presented R band limits of 23 mag, and these correspond to K band limits in the general range of 17-20 . By using correlations between K band magnitude and redshift we can tentatively suggest that these are sources with z>0.5 .

gmw_00012994.name: VLSS J033315.9 085119
gmw_00012994.overview: VLSS J033315.9 085119 is a candidate high-redshift radio galaxy [arXiv:1110.2467]. Optical imaging of the source found no counterpart down to an R-band magnitude of 23, suggesting the galaxy is likely located at a redshift greater than 0.5 [arXiv:1110.2467].

sources.summary: The paper treats VLSS J033315.9 085119 as a USS high-redshift radio-galaxy candidate; Liverpool Telescope GRI imaging found no optical counterpart, and K-z arguments suggest z>0.5.

quotes.quote: These sources are possibly high-redshift radio galaxies but optical identification is required for clarification. The follow-up observations were conducted with the Liverpool Telescope; in all cases no sources are detected down to an R magnitude of 23. By applying models and using the K-z relation we are able to suggest that these sources are possibly at high redshift.

Fig. LT_1 shows R band images of GMRT J033152.9 100843, VLSS J033315.9 085119 and GMRT J033326.6 084205. In all the observing bands no source can be seen at the location of the radio source. Deeper high-resolution multi-band optical observations are required to detect the optical counterparts of these sources and especially to resolve and characterise them.

We have presented R band limits of 23 mag, and these correspond to K band limits in the general range of 17-20 . By using correlations between K band magnitude and redshift we can tentatively suggest that these are sources with z>0.5 .

gmw_00012995.name: GMRT J033326.6 084205
gmw_00012995.overview: GMRT J033326.6 084205 is a candidate ultra-steep spectrum high-redshift radio galaxy [arXiv:1110.2467]. Optical observations down to an R-band magnitude of 23 detected no counterpart, suggesting the galaxy is likely located at a redshift greater than 0.5 [arXiv:1110.2467].

sources.summary: The paper treats GMRT J033326.6 084205 as a USS high-redshift radio-galaxy candidate; Liverpool Telescope GRI imaging found no optical counterpart, and K-z arguments suggest z>0.5.

quotes.quote: These sources are possibly high-redshift radio galaxies but optical identification is required for clarification. The follow-up observations were conducted with the Liverpool Telescope; in all cases no sources are detected down to an R magnitude of 23. By applying models and using the K-z relation we are able to suggest that these sources are possibly at high redshift.

Fig. LT_1 shows R band images of GMRT J033152.9 100843, VLSS J033315.9 085119 and GMRT J033326.6 084205. In all the observing bands no source can be seen at the location of the radio source. Deeper high-resolution multi-band optical observations are required to detect the optical counterparts of these sources and especially to resolve and characterise them.

We have presented R band limits of 23 mag, and these correspond to K band limits in the general range of 17-20 . By using correlations between K band magnitude and redshift we can tentatively suggest that these are sources with z>0.5 .

gmw_00012996.name: Carina III
gmw_00012996.overview: Carina III is classified as a dwarf spheroidal galaxy [arXiv:2311.04982]. It is located in close proximity to the dwarf galaxy Carina II, with an angular separation of only 0.3 degrees between the two systems [arXiv:2311.04982].

sources.summary: The paper treats Carina III as a dSph target close to Carina II, finds their fitted normalizations weakly correlated, and notes Carina III has one of the largest J factors.

quotes.quote: To investigate the impact of this we consider Carina II and Carina III, which are the two targets in our sample with the smallest separation of 0.3^ . We fit the ROI with the normalizations of the two sources and the Galactic diffuse free to vary and find the correlation between the normalizations of Carina II and Carina III to be -0.16, indicating they are not highly correlated.
In fact, Carina III and Willman 1 have the two largest J factors of all the dSphs (neglecting upper limits), and Tucana II and Reticulum II have the ninth and twelfth largest.
For instance, the difference between the Measured and Benchmark sample at M_ 200 GeV for the b b channel is almost entirely due to the exclusion of Carina III from the Measured sample.

gmw_00012997.name: Tucana II
gmw_00012997.overview: Tucana II is a dwarf spheroidal galaxy [arXiv:2311.04982]. It is identified as a high detection-significance source with a relatively large J-factor [arXiv:2311.04982].

sources.summary: Tucana II is listed among high detection-significance dSphs, has one of the larger J factors, and helps produce a low-mass TS peak consistent with background.

quotes.quote: Of these seven dSphs, Tucana II, Willman 1, Reticulum II, Horologium II and Bootes I were also reported as among the highest detection significance sources in the analysis of . Interestingly, the individual fields with higher TS tend to also have relatively large J factors. In fact, Carina III and Willman 1 have the two largest J factors of all the dSphs (neglecting upper limits), and Tucana II and Reticulum II have the ninth and twelfth largest.

The presence of the low-mass peak is consistent with background within <2 local significance and appears to be due in particular to Hydrus I and Tucana II, which exhibit TS peaks at relatively low mass values (see Figure fig:TSvm_individual ). Similar low-mass TS peaks are also observed in the blank fields.

gmw_00012998.name: Sagittarius
gmw_00012998.overview: The Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal galaxy is located at a relatively low Galactic latitude [arXiv:2311.04982]. It is in a likely non-equilibrium dynamical state and lies in close proximity to a gamma-ray source associated with the globular cluster M54 [arXiv:2311.04982].

sources.summary: The paper flags Sagittarius as a special dSph case because of low Galactic latitude, a likely non-equilibrium state, and nearby M54-related gamma-ray emission.

quotes.quote: Sagittarius is flagged due in part to its relatively low Galactic latitude (cf. ), likely non-equilibrium dynamical state , as well as its proximity to a nearby gamma-ray source. Indeed, the coordinates for Sagittarius lie just outside the 95 radius of the globular cluster M54 (4FGL J1855.1-3025), which was first included in 4FGL-DR3. Recent dedicated studies have presented possible scenarios for both DM and millisecond pulsar origins of the gamma-ray emission in the vicinity of Sagittarius and the M54 globular cluster, though neither explanation has been confirmed .

gmw_00012999.name: Willman 1
gmw_00012999.overview: Willman 1 is a dwarf spheroidal galaxy that exhibits evidence of tidal stripping and non-equilibrium dynamics [arXiv:2311.04982]. It is characterized by one of the largest J factors [arXiv:2311.04982] and D factors [arXiv:1910.05017] among studied dwarf spheroidal galaxies.

sources.summary: Willman 1 is treated as a special dSph due to non-equilibrium or tidal-stripping evidence, and the analysis reports high detection significance and one of the largest J factors.

quotes.quote: Some dSphs in the sample show evidence of non-equilibrium dynamics and tidal-stripping and are thus flagged as ``Special'' cases. These are Willman 1 and Bo o tes III Tucana III would also fall into this criteria , though it has already been excluded due to the unresolved velocity dispersion. .

Of these seven dSphs, Tucana II, Willman 1, Reticulum II, Horologium II and Bootes I were also reported as among the highest detection significance sources in the analysis of . Interestingly, the individual fields with higher TS tend to also have relatively large J factors. In fact, Carina III and Willman 1 have the two largest J factors of all the dSphs (neglecting upper limits), and Tucana II and Reticulum II have the ninth and twelfth largest.
Other dSphs with the highest local significance include Willman 1, Horologium II and Hydrus 1.

sources.summary: The paper notes Willman 1 has one of the largest D factors among selected dSphs, but large D-factor uncertainty keeps its contribution from affecting combined sensitivity.

quotes.quote: Notably, Triangulum II and Willman 1 have almost the largest D factors among the 19 selected dSphs, and t Triangulum II is located near the center of LHAASO FOV. However, their contributions do not significantly affect the combined sensitivity. The reason is that the statistical uncertainties of their D factors are considerably large, owing to the lack of data from the dSph kinematic observations.

gmw_00013000.name: Horologium II
gmw_00013000.overview: Horologium II is a dwarf spheroidal galaxy [arXiv:2311.04982]. It is located in close spatial proximity to a blazar candidate, which may act as a contaminating source of gamma-ray emission [arXiv:2311.04982].

sources.summary: Horologium II is flagged near a blazar candidate, appears among higher-significance dSphs, and has peak TS 10.8 with global significance 2.2 but lower local significance than Reticulum II.

quotes.quote: The dSphs that are flagged by this criteria are Bo o tes I, Crater II, Antlia II, Horologium II, and Virgo I.
Of these seven dSphs, Tucana II, Willman 1, Reticulum II, Horologium II and Bootes I were also reported as among the highest detection significance sources in the analysis of .

We also note Hydrus I and Horologium II, which have peak TS values of 10.3 and 10.8, and corresponding global significances of 2.2 , though their maximum local significances are lower than that of Reticulum II. This is due to the broader TS distribution of the blank fields at the lower masses that are best fit to Hydrus I and Horologium II. Additionally, Horologium II was flagged (see Section sec:sample ) due to spatial coincidence ( 0.02^ separation) with a blazar candidate in the WIBRaLS catalog, which may be a contaminating source of gamma-ray emission for this target.

gmw_00013001.name: Hydrus I
gmw_00013001.overview: Hydrus I is a dwarf spheroidal galaxy [arXiv:2311.04982]. In joint likelihood analyses, it exhibits a low-mass test statistic peak that is consistent with background noise [arXiv:2311.04982].

sources.summary: Hydrus I has peak TS 10.3 and global significance 2.2, with lower local significance than Reticulum II, and contributes to a low-mass TS peak consistent with background.

quotes.quote: We also note Hydrus I and Horologium II, which have peak TS values of 10.3 and 10.8, and corresponding global significances of 2.2 , though their maximum local significances are lower than that of Reticulum II. This is due to the broader TS distribution of the blank fields at the lower masses that are best fit to Hydrus I and Horologium II.

The presence of the low-mass peak is consistent with background within <2 local significance and appears to be due in particular to Hydrus I and Tucana II, which exhibit TS peaks at relatively low mass values (see Figure fig:TSvm_individual ). Similar low-mass TS peaks are also observed in the blank fields. In part, these peaks in the joint likelihood analysis are a product of the uncertainty in the J factor.

gmw_00013002.name: SDSS J141007.74+541203.3
gmw_00013002.overview: SDSS J141007.74+541203.3 is a broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:1905.02407]. The object exhibits a variable C IV broad absorption line, where the equivalent width of the absorption lines is anticorrelated with the continuum flux [arXiv:1905.02407].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes the variable C IV BAL quasar SDSS J141007.74+541203.3 using 44 SDSS epochs and confirms an anticorrelation between BAL equivalent width and continuum flux.

quotes.quote: We present a statistical analysis of the variable C iv broad absorption line (BAL) in the quasar SDSS J141007.74+541203.3, which have been observed with 44 epochs by the Sloan Digital Sky Survey Data Release 14. Grier2015 has recently concluded that the most likely cause of the variability of the BAL in SDSS J141007.74+541203.3 is a rapid response to changes in the incident ionizing continuum. In this paper, we confirm the anticorrelation between the equivalent width of BALs and the flux of the continuum based on the spectra of this quasar that show significant variations, which serve as another independent evidence for the view of Grier2015 .

gmw_00013003.name: J164452.70+430752.20
gmw_00013003.overview: The galaxy J164452.70+430752.20 exhibits a very hard effective X-ray spectrum [arXiv:1807.03868]. This spectral profile is associated with the largest intrinsic column density in its sample, which hints at a high level of absorption [arXiv:1807.03868].

sources.summary: The paper reports that J164452.70+430752.20 had an unconstrained low-energy X-ray photon index fixed at the hard boundary, suggesting absorption, and the largest intrinsic column density in the sample.

quotes.quote: For J164452.70+430752.20 we could not constrain the low-energy photon index, which hit the hard low boundary of the allowed parameter range (i.e. =-3 ). We therefore fixed it to that value, which hints at a significant level of absorption.
We note that J164452.70+430752.20 shows the largest intrinsic column density in the sample, as expected from its very hard effective spectrum (see previous section).

gmw_00013004.name: host galaxy of GRB 030323
gmw_00013004.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 030323 is a very faint high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 3.37 [arXiv:1301.4908, arXiv:astro-ph/0502494]. It contains low-metallicity gas with a low dust content and exhibits a modest star formation rate of roughly 1 solar mass per year [arXiv:1301.4908].

sources.summary: The chapter describes the GRB 030323 host as a very faint z=3.37 galaxy with high H I column density, low metallicity gas, little dust, and modest Ly emission star formation.

quotes.quote: As seen in Fig. andyhosts the host galaxy of GRB 030323 is among the faintest detected (it has a magnitude of about R=28 ).

Also seen are numerous low- and high-ionization lines at a redshift z=3.3718 0.0005 . The inferred neutral hydrogen column density, (N_ HI / cm ^ 2 ) = 21.90 0.07 , is very large -- higher than in DLAs seen against the light of QSOs (Wolfe et al. \ 2005).

From an analysis of the metal line strengths compared to the hydrogen column density, a sulphur abundance of about 0.05 solar is determined. An upper limit to the H _2 molecular fraction of 2N_ H _2 /(2N_ H _2 +N_ HI ) < 10^ 6 is derived from an analysis of the absence of molecular lines. In the DLA trough, a Ly emission line is detected, which corresponds to a star formation rate (not corrected for dust extinction) of roughly 1 M_ yr ^ 1 . All these results are consistent with the host galaxy of GRB 030323 containing low metallicity gas with a low dust content.

sources.summary: The paper says early localization enabled identification of the host galaxy of GRB 030323, whose faintness prevented redshift measurement from host-galaxy spectroscopy and implies star formation in faint high-redshift galaxies.

quotes.quote: Host galaxy identification \ and \ have very faint hosts: R 29.5 for \ , and V=28.0 for \ . The early localization of these two GRBs, and the quick identification of their afterglows, made possible the identification of the host galaxy of GRB 030323, and placed stringent limits on the magnitude of the host galaxy of GRB 020124.

The faintness of the host galaxies of these two bursts shows the impossibility to measure their redshifts from the spectroscopy of their host galaxies. \ and \ are examples of GRBs whose redshifts can only be measured at early times from the spectrum of the afterglow (unlike what was done for GRB 971214). The fact that two of the four GRBs known with z 3 occurred in faint galaxies, may indicate that a non-negligible fraction of star formation takes place in such faint galaxies.

gmw_00013005.name: LAE 1
gmw_00013005.overview: LAE 1 is a high-redshift Lyman-alpha galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 3 [arXiv:0710.3384]. It is the brightest Lyman-alpha source in its sample, is detected in X-rays, and is presumably an active galactic nucleus showing C IV emission [arXiv:0710.3384].

sources.summary: The paper identifies LAE 1 as the sample's brightest Ly source, x-ray detected and probably an AGN, with red IRAC colors, C IV emission, and unusual 8.0 micron brightness.

quotes.quote: It is interesting to note that there are four objects in the sample, corresponding to LAE 1, 11, 117, and 144 in the gronw07 catalog, with very red colors R-m_ 3.6 > 2 .

LAE 1, the brightest Ly source in the sample, is detected in x-ray and is presumably an AGN gronw07 . The similar R-m_ 3.6 colors of the remaining three x-ray undetected objects suggest that they may possibly be AGN as well. LAE 1, 11, and 117 are spectroscopically confirmed to be at z 3 . The x-ray detected object (LAE 1) shows C IV emission in its spectrum, while LAE 11 and 117 show only Ly emission (Lira et al., in preparation).

The four objects have R-m_ 4.5 colors ranging from 2.4<R-m_ 4.5 <4 , consistent with the colors of mid-IR selected AGN hicko07 . However, the observed R-m_ 4.5 colors can also be produced by a dusty stellar continuum with E(B-V) 0.5 . These four objects are the only 8.0 \ detected sources in the IRAC-uncontaminated sample and with m_ 8.0 ranging from 21 - 22 they are more than 2 mags brighter than the average IRAC-detected LAE ( AGN_color ).

gmw_00013006.name: LAE 11
gmw_00013006.overview: LAE 11 is a Lyman-alpha emitting galaxy spectroscopically confirmed to be at a redshift of approximately 3 [arXiv:0710.3384]. It exhibits very red colors and mid-infrared properties consistent with an active galactic nucleus, though it remains undetected in X-rays and shows only Lyman-alpha emission in its spectrum [arXiv:0710.3384].

sources.summary: The paper describes LAE 11 as a very red x-ray-undetected LAE at z 3, showing only Ly emission, with AGN-like mid-infrared colors and an x-ray luminosity upper limit.

quotes.quote: It is interesting to note that there are four objects in the sample, corresponding to LAE 1, 11, 117, and 144 in the gronw07 catalog, with very red colors R-m_ 3.6 > 2 . LAE 1, the brightest Ly source in the sample, is detected in x-ray and is presumably an AGN gronw07 . The similar R-m_ 3.6 colors of the remaining three x-ray undetected objects suggest that they may possibly be AGN as well.

LAE 1, 11, and 117 are spectroscopically confirmed to be at z 3 . The x-ray detected object (LAE 1) shows C IV emission in its spectrum, while LAE 11 and 117 show only Ly emission (Lira et al., in preparation). LAE 144 is observed, but no line was detected in its spectrum. LAE 11 and 117 are in the central part of CDF-S where the 0.5-8 keV (rest-frame 0.1-2 keV) flux limit of < 3 16 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 implies an upper limit of < 2.5 43 erg s ^ 1 for the objects' AGN x-ray luminosities.

The four objects have R-m_ 4.5 colors ranging from 2.4<R-m_ 4.5 <4 , consistent with the colors of mid-IR selected AGN hicko07 . However, the observed R-m_ 4.5 colors can also be produced by a dusty stellar continuum with E(B-V) 0.5 .

gmw_00013007.name: LAE 117
gmw_00013007.overview: LAE 117 is a high-redshift Lyman-alpha galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 3 [arXiv:0710.3384]. It exhibits very red colors and mid-infrared properties consistent with an active galactic nucleus, though its spectrum shows only Lyman-alpha emission and it remains undetected in X-rays [arXiv:0710.3384].

sources.summary: The paper describes LAE 117 as a very red x-ray-undetected LAE at z 3, showing only Ly emission, with AGN-like mid-infrared colors and an x-ray luminosity upper limit.

quotes.quote: It is interesting to note that there are four objects in the sample, corresponding to LAE 1, 11, 117, and 144 in the gronw07 catalog, with very red colors R-m_ 3.6 > 2 . LAE 1, the brightest Ly source in the sample, is detected in x-ray and is presumably an AGN gronw07 . The similar R-m_ 3.6 colors of the remaining three x-ray undetected objects suggest that they may possibly be AGN as well.

LAE 1, 11, and 117 are spectroscopically confirmed to be at z 3 . The x-ray detected object (LAE 1) shows C IV emission in its spectrum, while LAE 11 and 117 show only Ly emission (Lira et al., in preparation). LAE 144 is observed, but no line was detected in its spectrum. The four objects have R-m_ 4.5 colors ranging from 2.4<R-m_ 4.5 <4 , consistent with the colors of mid-IR selected AGN hicko07 . However, the observed R-m_ 4.5 colors can also be produced by a dusty stellar continuum with E(B-V) 0.5 .

gmw_00013008.name: LAE 144
gmw_00013008.overview: LAE 144 is a Lyman-alpha emitting galaxy that exhibits very red optical-to-mid-infrared colors [arXiv:0710.3384]. These colors suggest the galaxy may host an active galactic nucleus, though they could also be produced by a dusty stellar continuum [arXiv:0710.3384].

sources.summary: The paper flags LAE 144 as a very red LAE lacking a spectral line detection; its mid-infrared colors are AGN-like but could also arise from a dusty stellar continuum.

quotes.quote: It is interesting to note that there are four objects in the sample, corresponding to LAE 1, 11, 117, and 144 in the gronw07 catalog, with very red colors R-m_ 3.6 > 2 . LAE 1, the brightest Ly source in the sample, is detected in x-ray and is presumably an AGN gronw07 . The similar R-m_ 3.6 colors of the remaining three x-ray undetected objects suggest that they may possibly be AGN as well.
LAE 144 is observed, but no line was detected in its spectrum.

The four objects have R-m_ 4.5 colors ranging from 2.4<R-m_ 4.5 <4 , consistent with the colors of mid-IR selected AGN hicko07 . However, the observed R-m_ 4.5 colors can also be produced by a dusty stellar continuum with E(B-V) 0.5 . These four objects are the only 8.0 \ detected sources in the IRAC-uncontaminated sample and with m_ 8.0 ranging from 21 - 22 they are more than 2 mags brighter than the average IRAC-detected LAE ( AGN_color ).

gmw_00013009.name: TGSS J1054+5832
gmw_00013009.overview: TGSS J1054+5832 is a high-redshift radio galaxy [arXiv:2010.02515]. Optical observations of the source conducted in the i band have yielded a detection [arXiv:2010.02515].

sources.summary: The paper uses TGSS J1054+5832 as a high-redshift GMRT radio galaxy observed with ADFOSC, reporting an i-band optical detection and tabulated magnitudes and errors from DOT observations.

quotes.quote: Using ADFOSC, optical observations of a GMRT high red-shift (z 4.8 2 ) radio galaxy source (TGSS J1054+5832) were obtained on a dark night in SDSS and i pass bands. A statistically significant optical detection has been made in the i band (Omar 2019b). The magnitudes and associated errors of this object as well as sky brightness derived from these observations are listed in Table 1.

gmw_00013010.name: source 29
gmw_00013010.overview: Source 29 is an optically bright, extended galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.128 [arXiv:0904.2586]. It is classified as a type-2 active galactic nucleus based on its X-ray luminosity, and as a LINER using BPT diagnostics [arXiv:0904.2586].

sources.summary: The paper identifies source 29 as an optically bright, X-ray faint extended galaxy at z=0.128, classifies it as an AGN-2 by X-ray luminosity, and as a LINER by BPT diagnostics.

quotes.quote: All are extended objects in the R band image (sources 20, 29, 39, 42, 44, 48, 71) and their R magnitude is in the range 15<R<18 (and I<17 ). Four of these sources are classified spectroscopically as galaxies: source 29, source 39, source 42 and source 71, with spectroscopic redshifts of z=0.128 , z=0.126 , z=0.238 and z=0.129 respectively (see their spectra in Figure sdss-bootes ). Thus, these sources should be nearby, bright normal galaxies see e.g. barger01, tozzi01, horn01 . In addition, two sources ( 29 and 39) of the spectroscopically identified sources are classified as AGN-2 (based on the X-ray luminosity) with L_X [ 0.5-7 keV ] = 2.3 10^ 41 erg s ^ 1 , and L_X [ 0.5-7 keV ] = 2.2 10^ 41 erg s ^ 1 respectively. The two sources are classified as LINERS using the BPT diagram.

gmw_00013011.name: source 39
gmw_00013011.overview: Source 39 is an optically bright, extended galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.126 [arXiv:0904.2586]. It is classified as a type-2 active galactic nucleus based on its X-ray luminosity, and as a LINER using BPT diagnostics [arXiv:0904.2586].

sources.summary: The paper identifies source 39 as an optically bright, X-ray faint extended galaxy at z=0.126, classifies it as an AGN-2 by X-ray luminosity, and as a LINER by BPT diagnostics.

quotes.quote: All are extended objects in the R band image (sources 20, 29, 39, 42, 44, 48, 71) and their R magnitude is in the range 15<R<18 (and I<17 ). Four of these sources are classified spectroscopically as galaxies: source 29, source 39, source 42 and source 71, with spectroscopic redshifts of z=0.128 , z=0.126 , z=0.238 and z=0.129 respectively (see their spectra in Figure sdss-bootes ). Thus, these sources should be nearby, bright normal galaxies see e.g. barger01, tozzi01, horn01 . In addition, two sources ( 29 and 39) of the spectroscopically identified sources are classified as AGN-2 (based on the X-ray luminosity) with L_X [ 0.5-7 keV ] = 2.3 10^ 41 erg s ^ 1 , and L_X [ 0.5-7 keV ] = 2.2 10^ 41 erg s ^ 1 respectively. The two sources are classified as LINERS using the BPT diagram.

gmw_00013012.name: source 42
gmw_00013012.overview: Source 42 is an optically bright, extended galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.238 [arXiv:0904.2586]. Based on its optical and X-ray properties, it is classified as a normal, star-forming galaxy [arXiv:0904.2586].

sources.summary: The paper identifies source 42 as an optically bright, X-ray faint extended galaxy at z=0.238, describes it as nearby and bright, and classifies it as a normal, star-forming galaxy.

quotes.quote: All are extended objects in the R band image (sources 20, 29, 39, 42, 44, 48, 71) and their R magnitude is in the range 15<R<18 (and I<17 ). Four of these sources are classified spectroscopically as galaxies: source 29, source 39, source 42 and source 71, with spectroscopic redshifts of z=0.128 , z=0.126 , z=0.238 and z=0.129 respectively (see their spectra in Figure sdss-bootes ). Thus, these sources should be nearby, bright normal galaxies see e.g. barger01, tozzi01, horn01 .

The other two sources ( 42 and 71) are classified as normal galaxies based on their X-ray luminosity and classified as star-forming galaxies using the optical classification. Their X-ray luminosity are: L_X [ 0.5-7 keV ] = 6.7 10^ 41 erg s ^ 1 , and L_X [ 0.5-7 keV ] = 2.8 10^ 41 erg s ^ 1 respectively.

gmw_00013013.name: source 71
gmw_00013013.overview: Source 71 is an optically bright, extended galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.129 [arXiv:0904.2586]. Based on its optical spectrum and X-ray luminosity, it is classified as a normal, star-forming galaxy [arXiv:0904.2586].

sources.summary: The paper identifies source 71 as an optically bright, X-ray faint extended galaxy at z=0.129, describes it as nearby and bright, and classifies it as a normal, star-forming galaxy.

quotes.quote: All are extended objects in the R band image (sources 20, 29, 39, 42, 44, 48, 71) and their R magnitude is in the range 15<R<18 (and I<17 ). Four of these sources are classified spectroscopically as galaxies: source 29, source 39, source 42 and source 71, with spectroscopic redshifts of z=0.128 , z=0.126 , z=0.238 and z=0.129 respectively (see their spectra in Figure sdss-bootes ). Thus, these sources should be nearby, bright normal galaxies see e.g. barger01, tozzi01, horn01 .

The other two sources ( 42 and 71) are classified as normal galaxies based on their X-ray luminosity and classified as star-forming galaxies using the optical classification. Their X-ray luminosity are: L_X [ 0.5-7 keV ] = 6.7 10^ 41 erg s ^ 1 , and L_X [ 0.5-7 keV ] = 2.8 10^ 41 erg s ^ 1 respectively.

gmw_00013014.overview: The object 8135-12704 is a disk galaxy [arXiv:2104.07863] that contains a central region affected by a bulge or a bar [arXiv:2104.07863]. In its outer regions, the disk dynamical mass density agrees with the stellar mass density, indicating a low fraction of non-stellar matter [arXiv:2104.07863].

sources.summary: The paper uses galaxy 8135-12704 as a MaNGA example, showing its inclination and usable radial range, and finding its disc dynamical mass matches outer stellar mass but not necessarily the inner mass distribution.

quotes.quote: As an example, the inclination distribution of galaxy 8135-12704 after converting the obtained Qs is shown in Figure fig:restincl . We mark the radial `good' range in its SDSS band image and velocity field for galaxy 8135-12704, as shown in Figure fig:range . The central region affected by a bulge or bar and the outskirts with a large bin size are thus excluded in the measurements of PA and Q.

For galaxy 8135-12704, the disc dynamical mass density is in agreement with the outskirts of the stellar mass density, indicating a small fraction of non-stellar matter. In the inner region, although the velocity dispersion field is fitted by parameterising q_ ^2 and introducing an isotropic component, the thin-disc assumption in our model might be invalid because the possible existence of components other than the thin disc. Therefore, the disc dynamical mass density probably cannot trace the real mass distribution in the inner region.

gmw_00013015.name: 2MASS J15075554-6222336
gmw_00013015.overview: 2MASS J15075554-6222336 is a hypothetical galaxy located near the very-high-energy gamma-ray source HESS J1507-622 [arXiv:1404.0889]. It is modeled as a host galaxy harboring a supermassive black hole [arXiv:1404.0889].

sources.summary: The paper treats 2MASS J15075554-6222336 as a hypothetical galaxy hosting an SMBH, using its K-band brightness and position near HESS J1507-622 to test whether an SMBH could power the gamma-ray source.

quotes.quote: The infrared source closest to the centroid of \ is 2MASS J15075554-6222336 m_ K = 14.39 mag, corrected for galactic extinction of A_ K = 0.505 , schlafly2011 located at an angular distance of 230 . The brightest infrared source inside the extend of the VHE source is 2MASS J15070879-6216441 ( m_ K = 11.16 mag, again corrected for extinction) located at an angular distance of 517 cutri2003 http://simbad.u-strasbg.fr/simbad/ . For the rest of this section it is assumed that these two sources represent galaxies harboring SMBHs. The mass of the SMBH of these hypothetical galaxies as function of luminosity distance can be estimated from the following relations marconi2003 : log M_ BH (d_ L ) = 8.21 + 1.13 ( log L_ K (d_ L ) -10.9) with log (L_ K (d_ L )/L_ K, ) = 0.4 (3.28 - M_ K (d_ L )) and M_ K (d_ L ) = m_ K + 5 log (d_ L ) -5 Here, L_ K (d_ L ) is the luminosity of the host galaxy in the K-band and M_ K (d_ L ) is the absolute K-band magnitude of the host galaxy as function of the luminosity distance ( M_ K, = 3.28 mag is the absolute K-band magnitude of the sun). The masses of these hypothetical SMBHs as function of distance are plotted in Fig. figure:ext .
It is found that \ would have to radiate on the level of 1 (2MASS J15075554-6222336) and 0.05 (2MASS J15070879-6216441) of L_ Edd of a SMBH in these hypothetical galaxies.

gmw_00013016.name: 2MASS J15070879-6216441
gmw_00013016.overview: 2MASS J15070879-6216441 is a galaxy located within the spatial extent of a very-high-energy source [arXiv:1404.0889]. It is modeled as a host galaxy harboring a central supermassive black hole [arXiv:1404.0889].

sources.summary: The paper treats 2MASS J15070879-6216441 as a hypothetical SMBH-host galaxy inside the VHE source extent, using its corrected K-band brightness to estimate SMBH mass and required Eddington-level emission.

quotes.quote: The infrared source closest to the centroid of \ is 2MASS J15075554-6222336 m_ K = 14.39 mag, corrected for galactic extinction of A_ K = 0.505 , schlafly2011 located at an angular distance of 230 . The brightest infrared source inside the extend of the VHE source is 2MASS J15070879-6216441 ( m_ K = 11.16 mag, again corrected for extinction) located at an angular distance of 517 cutri2003 http://simbad.u-strasbg.fr/simbad/ . For the rest of this section it is assumed that these two sources represent galaxies harboring SMBHs. The mass of the SMBH of these hypothetical galaxies as function of luminosity distance can be estimated from the following relations marconi2003 : log M_ BH (d_ L ) = 8.21 + 1.13 ( log L_ K (d_ L ) -10.9) with log (L_ K (d_ L )/L_ K, ) = 0.4 (3.28 - M_ K (d_ L )) and M_ K (d_ L ) = m_ K + 5 log (d_ L ) -5 Here, L_ K (d_ L ) is the luminosity of the host galaxy in the K-band and M_ K (d_ L ) is the absolute K-band magnitude of the host galaxy as function of the luminosity distance ( M_ K, = 3.28 mag is the absolute K-band magnitude of the sun). The masses of these hypothetical SMBHs as function of distance are plotted in Fig. figure:ext .
It is found that \ would have to radiate on the level of 1 (2MASS J15075554-6222336) and 0.05 (2MASS J15070879-6216441) of L_ Edd of a SMBH in these hypothetical galaxies.

gmw_00013017.name: WXSC-2dF source 96128
gmw_00013017.overview: WXSC-2dF source 96128 is a typical spiral or disk galaxy located at a luminosity distance of 271.6 Mpc [arXiv:2301.05952]. Its spectral energy distribution features a stellar bump peaking in the H-band, which is characteristic of a luminous evolved stellar population [arXiv:2301.05952].

sources.summary: The paper uses WXSC-2dF source 96128 as a worked example: a typical spiral/disk galaxy with reported coordinates, WISE/2MASS photometry, redshift, luminosity distance, and SED features.

quotes.quote: As an example of using these prescriptions, we consider WXSC-2dF source 96128; Ra, Dec(J2000) = (358.70312, -33.04600 ). The SED is shown in Fig.  fig:SED , which includes 2MASS and WXSC measurements, as well as the template used to restframe-correct the observed fluxes.
