The observed W1 mag is 13.200 0.015 (1.6222 0.0225 mJy), and colors(mag) = 0.178 0.027, = 3.794 0.038, = 1.653 0.190. The CMB-frame redshift is 0.06057 (helio is 0.06150), and the corresponding luminosity distance is 271.6 Mpc (co-moving, 256 Mpc), so the distance modulus is 37.17 mag.

An example SED is shown in Fig.  fig:SED , a typical spiral/disk galaxy from the GAMA/KiDS-s sample in the WXSC. It shows the observed fluxes, as measured by 2MASS and \ (left panel), with the best fit spectral template indicated by the solid line. It features the well-known stellar "bump" that peaks in the H-band (1.6 ), characteristic of the luminous evolved population. The R-J tail of the continuum extends into the \ where the \ W1 3.4 \ and W2 \ bands pick up the emission.

gmw_00013018.name: SDSS J101012.77+560520.0
gmw_00013018.overview: SDSS J101012.77+560520.0 is a quasar located at a redshift of z=2.136 [arXiv:1912.05614]. It forms a close two-arcsecond doublet with a nearby red star, whose SDSS spectrum was contaminated by emission bleeding over from the quasar [arXiv:1912.05614].

sources.summary: Milliquas v6.2 added SDSS J101012.77+560520.0 as a z=2.136 quasar whose emission contaminated a nearby red star that SDSS-DR3Q had mistakenly accepted.

quotes.quote: A new quasar was presented by Milliquas v6.2: SDSS J101012.77+560520.0, v=18.7, z=2.136. This object eluded SDSS capture by being in a 2-arcsec doublet with a red star, SDSS J101012.65+560520.4. SDSS-DR3 http://classic.sdss.org/dr7/products/spectra/getspectra.html targetted the star and pipeline-classified it as a QSO (z=2.136) due to emission bleed-over from the true quasar, and it was so accepted by SDSS-DR3Q without noticing the doublet.
I found this quasar whilst reconciling Gaia astrometry to the doublet. SDSS has been notified and they have advised that they may add it into their upcoming SDSS-DR16Q release.

gmw_00013019.name: Q 13034+2942
gmw_00013019.overview: Q 13034+2942 is a quasar-like object that corresponds to a flat-spectrum, 21st-magnitude stellar source in SDSS imaging [arXiv:1912.05614]. Historical data indicates that this source is variable and was brighter during the 1960s, reaching 19th magnitude [arXiv:1912.05614].

sources.summary: The paper re-evaluates Q 13034+2942, giving B1950 and J2000 positions, identifying a quasar-like SDSS object, and explaining that the published finding chart pointed to a red dwarf.

quotes.quote: But then there's this, from Borra et al. 1996, AJ 111,1456, the quasar "Q 13034+2942" (called "130324+294245" in the paper) is the very first one on their list, and their first finding chart.

Furthermore, on their Table 4a they lead right off with it as positioned at B1950 130324.21+294245.8, lest there be any mistake. That translates to J2000 130547.40+292643.0 which shows up on the SDSS finding chart as a flattish-spectrum 21-mag stellar-psf which my own data reports as a variable object which was 19th magnitude in the 1960's -- so very quasar-like and all good.
Um, guys, your optical data did not include the true object. It's not there at all. They're pointing to the red dwarf because the quasar isn't on the chart.

gmw_00013020.name: PKS 0159+034
gmw_00013020.overview: PKS 0159+034 is a strong point radio source [arXiv:1912.05614]. It is associated with a faint stellar source that is classified as a high-confidence quasar candidate [arXiv:1912.05614].

sources.summary: Milliquas separates PKS 0159+034 from a misassigned Abell 293 redshift and retains it as a radio source associated with a high-confidence quasar candidate lacking redshift.

quotes.quote: Abell 293 was originally thought to be a (Parkes) radio galaxy cluster but Gioia et al. 1984-ApJ-283-495, in addition to discovering a background X-ray quasar with z=1.897 and v=19.7, reported that "also present in this field is a strong point radio source PKS 0159+034 whose position is not coincident with any of the X-ray or optical sources discussed here". In spite of this, VCV assigned both the name "PKS 0159+034" and the redshift 1.897 onto the primary Abell galaxy, thus conflating three objects into one.

Furthermore, my Milliquas algorithm shows the radio source PKS 0159+034, aka FIRST J020151.4+034309, to be associated with 98.6 confidence to a =21.8 g=22.1 stellar source which is furthermore calculated as 96 likely to be a quasar. But it has no redshift so will continue to appear in Milliquas as a candidate only, henceforth annotated with the name PKS 0159+034.

gmw_00013021.name: A4/22
gmw_00013021.overview: The designation A4/22 was originally associated with a small, passive galaxy [arXiv:1912.05614]. However, this identification was corrected to the nearby quasar SDSS J110205.85+295914.7, which has a spectroscopic redshift of 1.045 [arXiv:1912.05614].

sources.summary: Milliquas moves A4/22 from a passive nearby object to SDSS J110205.85+295914.7, matching its colors and photometric redshift to Schade's listed z=1.045 quasar.

quotes.quote: While checking over legacy quasars with unsuitable photometry, I came across the quasar "A4/22" from the "Very Faint Quasar Survey", D. Schade, 1991-AJ-102-869 -- the quasar is listed with z=1.045, v=20.00 and b=20.17. But upon inspection on an SDSS finding chart, the near object is seen to be just a small passive galaxy, so how did David Schade come to call that a quasar?

And sure enough, at 200 arcseconds due South of the designated spot I see SDSS J110205.85+295914.7 with g=20.00, =19.71 and u=20.25, a perfect photometric fit. Furthermore, it is a quasar candidate in Gordon Richards' NBCKDE-v3 catalogue (2015-ApJS-219-39) with a photometric redshift of 1.000, well-matched to Schade's spectroscopic redshift of 1.045. Somehow this object got moved 200 arcsec due North in the preparation of the paper -- looking at Table 4 of the paper, it looks like the declination of the object a4/5 was accidently copied over to a4/22.
I have moved the identification of "A4/22" over to J110205.85+295914.7, and the author has been informed.

gmw_00013022.name: KP 1229.0+07.8
gmw_00013022.overview: KP 1229.0+07.8 is a quasar located at a redshift of z=1.93 [arXiv:1912.05614]. It is optically identified with the flat-spectrum source SDSS J123134.53+073425.8 [arXiv:1912.05614].

sources.summary: Milliquas reports KP 1229.0+07.8 has z=1.93 and incorrect B1950 astrometry, moving the identification from a wrong SDSS galaxy to a flatter-spectrum source.

quotes.quote: KP 1229.0+07.8 from Sramek Weedman, 1978-ApJ-221-468, 19 on their list, z=1.93, v=20.5. The B1950 astrometry, duly reported by VCV, pointed to nothing (approx 25 arcseconds away from 3 nearest optical candidates). A coarse finding chart was provided from which, many years ago, I selected the wrong object, SDSS J123134.02+073440.2, a galaxy with =19.25, g=20.61. I have now moved this to the correct object, SDSS J123134.53+073425.8 with =21.73, g=21.96, a move of 16.3 arcseconds. Besides the flatter spectrum, it is also clearly a better match to the finding chart, although you do need to stare at it for a while.

gmw_00013023.name: IGR J12107+3822
gmw_00013023.overview: IGR J12107+3822 is classified as an intermediate Seyfert galaxy [arXiv:1201.3525]. It exhibits a very low dust-to-gas ratio, conspicuous X-ray absorption, and a spectrum that suggests complex absorption from a torus [arXiv:1201.3525].

sources.summary: The paper identifies IGR J12107+3822 as an intermediate Seyfert with very low E(B-V)/N_H, conspicuous X-ray absorption, and a well-constrained 2-10 keV spectrum suggesting complex torus absorption.

quotes.quote: Indeed we find that the E _ B-V /N _H ratio is around the Galactic standard for the majority of type 1 sources (Fig. Enh , right panel); the only two exceptions are IGR J12107+3822 and IGR J09253+6929, which have a very low E _ B-V /N _H . Both have a conspicuous X-ray column density and are intermediate Seyferts.

The first source has a good quality 2-10 keV spectrum and so we can assume that the value of the X-ray column density is well constrained; this could be an intermediate Seyfert with complex absorption where we see the nucleus not directly, but at some inclination angle so that our line of sight intercepts thicker material from the torus.

gmw_00013024.name: IGR J09253+6929
gmw_00013024.overview: IGR J09253+6929 is classified as an intermediate Seyfert galaxy [arXiv:1201.3525]. It exhibits a conspicuous X-ray column density and a very low ratio of E(B-V) to hydrogen column density [arXiv:1201.3525].

sources.summary: The paper describes IGR J09253+6929 as an intermediate Seyfert with low E(B-V)/N_H and conspicuous X-ray absorption, but notes its low-quality X-ray spectrum leaves the column-density ratio uncertain.

quotes.quote: Indeed we find that the E _ B-V /N _H ratio is around the Galactic standard for the majority of type 1 sources (Fig. Enh , right panel); the only two exceptions are IGR J12107+3822 and IGR J09253+6929, which have a very low E _ B-V /N _H . Both have a conspicuous X-ray column density and are intermediate Seyferts.

The second source has a low quality X-ray spectrum with huge errors in the estimate of the N _H and a fixed photon index; it is thus possible that, with better statistics, its position in the graph shifts to a E _ B-V /N _H ratio closer to the dotted line.

gmw_00013025.name: ID 1155
gmw_00013025.overview: ID 1155 is a compact radio source located at a redshift of 0.01 [arXiv:2308.05192]. It exhibits an observed spectrum that peaks near 152 MHz, suggesting it may be a young active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2308.05192].

sources.summary: The paper presents ID 1155 as a compact MPS source at z=0.01, with an observed spectrum peaking near 152 MHz and possible young-AGN interpretation.

quotes.quote: The top-panel of Figure fig:mps_spectra displays the observed spectrum of the source with ID 1155 at a redshift of 0.01. As depicted from the image of Figure fig:mps_spectra , it is a compact source at 400 MHz. The measured peak frequency of this source is 152.4 4.01 MHz, while the values of the spectral indices _ thick and _ thin are 3.35 1.17 and 0.62 0.02 , respectively.

The MPS sources identified in this study were found to span a redshift range of 0.01<z<3.38 , with only one source having spectroscopic redshift information. This range suggests that these sources could potentially be young AGN at higher redshifts.

gmw_00013026.name: ID 595
gmw_00013026.overview: ID 595 is a compact radio source located at a redshift of 0.18 [arXiv:2308.05192]. It is classified as a megahertz-peaked spectrum source that may represent a young active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2308.05192].

sources.summary: The paper treats ID 595 as a compact MPS source at z=0.18, fitting a 158 MHz peak and suggesting such sources could be young AGN with possible FFA.

quotes.quote: Also, the middle and bottom panels of Figure fig:mps_spectra present the fitted spectra for sources with ID 595 and 1194 and having their peak frequencies around 158.29 24.48 MHz and 168.07 28.69 MHz, respectively. These sources are also compact and detected at redshifts 0.18 and 1.04.

The MPS sources identified in this study were found to span a redshift range of 0.01<z<3.38 , with only one source having spectroscopic redshift information. This range suggests that these sources could potentially be young AGN at higher redshifts.

The parameters derived for the three MPS sources using eq. eq1 are similar to those presented in , where the authors suggest FFA as the most likely absorption mechanism based on the source's peak frequencies, linear sizes, and magnetic field. Therefore, it is possible that the bottom three sources in Table tab:MPS also exhibit FFA as the absorption mechanism.

gmw_00013027.overview: The galaxy 1194 is a compact megahertz-peaked spectrum source located at a redshift of 1.04 [arXiv:2308.05192]. It is identified as a potential young active galactic nucleus that may exhibit free-free absorption [arXiv:2308.05192].

sources.summary: The paper treats source 1194 as a compact MPS source at z=1.04, fitting a 168 MHz peak and suggesting such sources could be young AGN with possible FFA.

quotes.quote: Also, the middle and bottom panels of Figure fig:mps_spectra present the fitted spectra for sources with ID 595 and 1194 and having their peak frequencies around 158.29 24.48 MHz and 168.07 28.69 MHz, respectively. These sources are also compact and detected at redshifts 0.18 and 1.04.

The MPS sources identified in this study were found to span a redshift range of 0.01<z<3.38 , with only one source having spectroscopic redshift information. This range suggests that these sources could potentially be young AGN at higher redshifts.

The parameters derived for the three MPS sources using eq. eq1 are similar to those presented in , where the authors suggest FFA as the most likely absorption mechanism based on the source's peak frequencies, linear sizes, and magnetic field. Therefore, it is possible that the bottom three sources in Table tab:MPS also exhibit FFA as the absorption mechanism.

gmw_00013028.name: J0229+0044
gmw_00013028.overview: J0229+0044 is an active galactic nucleus and quasar located at a spectroscopic redshift of approximately 1.216 [arXiv:2008.11403]. Neutral hydrogen absorption detected toward the quasar arises from gas within the active galactic nucleus environment [arXiv:2008.11403].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0229+0044 with DEEP2 42053345, treats it as a quasar/AGN at z about 1.216, and finds its H i absorption arises from gas in the AGN environment.

quotes.quote: The AGN J0229+0044 is spatially coincident with an object identified in the DEEP2 survey (DEEP2 42053345), with a spectroscopic redshift of z = 1.2161 0.0045 from the O ii 3727 doublet The object was targeted in the DEEP2 survey at two different epochs and the DEEP2 catalog reports two redshifts, z=1.21639 and z=1.21577 ; these measurements are consistent within the 62 \ redshift error for redshift quality Q =3 objects Newman13 . We have used the mean of these two measurements as the AGN redshift, and have assumed the mean estimate to have a 1 uncertainty of 62 . . The quasar has g =23.43 0.25 from the Dark Energy Survey DES; Abbott18 and K =19.896 0.080 from the ALHAMBRA survey Moles08 , i.e. g K =3.53 0.26 .

The \ absorption towards J0229+0044, shown in Figure fig:spectra A , consists of a single component, centered at z=1.216557 0.000058 . This is in excellent agreement with the spectroscopic redshift of the DEEP2 object, indicating that the \ absorption arises from gas in the AGN environment. A single-component Gaussian fit to the \ line profile yields a peak optical depth of _ max = 0.727 0.083 and an FWHM of 56.1 7.4 .

gmw_00013029.name: J0229+0053
gmw_00013029.overview: J0229+0053 is a faint active galactic nucleus that is dim at optical and ultraviolet wavelengths [arXiv:2008.11403]. It exhibits strong, broad neutral hydrogen absorption that is likely associated with gas in the active galactic nucleus environment [arXiv:2008.11403].

sources.summary: The paper treats J0229+0053 as a faint AGN whose strong, broad H i absorption is probably associated with gas in the AGN environment rather than an intervening galaxy.

quotes.quote: The \ spectrum towards J0229+0053, shown in Fig. fig:spectra B , has two distinct features: a strong component centred at z=1.16299 and with a peak optical depth of 0.50 , and a wide weak wing that extends to 440 \ with respect to the main component. Unfortunately, the AGN does not currently have a spectroscopic redshift; its photometric redshift is z=0.86 0.22 Hsieh05 . The redshift of the main \ component is in broad agreement with the AGN's photometric redshift, but the large uncertainty in the photometric redshift means that we cannot formally rule out the possibility that the AGN is a background source i.e. that the absorption arises from an ``intervening'' galaxy; e.g. Kanekar14dla . However, the high velocity-integrated \ optical depth of the absorber suggests that it is likely to be an associated system; such high values have hitherto only been obtained from \ absorption in AGN environments e.g. Gereb15,Aditya18cjf,Kanekar14dla .

Further, the wide line profile, with V 640 \ between the nulls, is significantly larger than those seen in intervening systems, where V 200 \ Kanekar09 . We hence conclude that the \ absorption in J0229+0053 is likely to arise from gas in the AGN environment, i.e. that the AGN redshift is approximately equal to the \ absorption redshift. J0229+0053 is faint at ultraviolet and optical wavelengths, with V =24.09 0.16 , B =25.23 0.25 Hsieh05 , g= 24.31 0.29 DES; Abbott18 , and a non-detection in the GALEX NUV band. The object is detected in the UKIDSS survey, with K =20.19 0.20 Lawrence07 .

gmw_00013030.name: PSO J340.2041-18.6621
gmw_00013030.overview: PSO J340.2041-18.6621 is a high-redshift quasar [arXiv:1803.01424]. It was observed at a redshift of z = 5.98 based on its Lyman-alpha emission line [arXiv:1803.01424].

sources.summary: The paper says PSO J340.2041-18.6621 was rediscovered as a z=5.98 high-redshift quasar with NTT/EFOSC-2, with its spectrum confirming the quasar classification from the Ly emission line.

quotes.quote: PSO J340.2041-18.6621 A third high redshift quasar was observed with NTT EFOSC-2 on the night of 2015 June 01. Using the same VST ATLAS + WISE combined selection technique, we re-discovered the z=5.98 quasar, PSO J340.2041-18.6621, independently found by using the PanSTARRS survey. The spectrum confirmed the object as a quasar although we measured a slightly lower redshift of z = 5.98 0.03 , based on the Ly emission line, than that measured by of z = 6.03 . We include this quasar in Table table:table1 for completeness and its NTT spectrum is shown in Fig. fig:nttz98 .

gmw_00013031.name: cnuid272
gmw_00013031.overview: The galaxy cnuid272 is classified as an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2311.04832]. It is characterized as an obscured, Compton-thin source [arXiv:2311.04832].

sources.summary: The paper describes cnuid272 as an obscured Compton-thin AGN, simulating deep-survey HEX-P spectra and showing CTK-cover and column-density constraints for nominal and fainter flux cases.

quotes.quote: As presented by , two of the three sources (nuid117 and cnuid330) are classified as CT, and one (cnuid272) is obscured but Compton-thin.

For the faint and higher redshift AGN (nuid117 and cnuid272), CTK-cover is not constrained with previous data. HEX-P 's effective area beyond 20 keV will allow us to discern the subtle effects (e.g., a general sharpening of the Compton hump at > 10 keV) that CTK-cover has on the hard-band spectra of these AGN. To show this, we have simulated obscured HEX-P spectra with CTK-cover = 0.0, 0.3, and 0.6 .

Constraints on CTK-cover and 99.73 percentile values derived from the posterior distribution for cnuid272 in the deep survey (500 ks) are 0.31 ^ + 0.19 _ 0.30 (true CTK-cover =0.0 ), 0.37 ^ + 0.19 _ 0.36 (true CTK-cover =0.3 ), and >0.11 (true CTK-cover =0.6 ). In the case of cnuid272, this constraint is shown for 500 ks simulation of the object at its nominal flux, as well as an identical object that is ten times fainter as well as to the N_ H constraints of the existing NuSTAR observation in Figure fig:272_constraint .

gmw_00013032.name: 3FHL J0001.9 4155
gmw_00013032.overview: The active galactic nucleus 3FHL J0001.9 4155 is a source that has been reported as a high-synchrotron-peaked blazar [arXiv:2104.13333]. Its optical spectrum displays a single emission feature, leading to a tentative classification as a flat-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:2104.13333].

sources.summary: The paper reports one optical emission feature for 3FHL J0001.9 4155, tentatively classifies it as an FSRQ, and notes its prior HSP blazar classification in 4LAC.

quotes.quote: The source optical spectrum showed one emission feature at 5450 \ (EW = 88 ), resembling those observed in FSRQ spectra. However, the presence of only one such feature prevents us from successfully identifying it; thus, we tentatively classify it as an FSRQ. This source has been reported as an HSP blazar in the fourth catalog of AGN detected by the Fermi LAT (4LAC; Ajello2020zz ).

gmw_00013033.name: 3FHL J0102.8 2001
gmw_00013033.overview: 3FHL J0102.8 2001 is a high-synchrotron-peaked blazar [arXiv:2104.13333]. Its optical spectrum exhibits oxygen II emission and sodium D absorption features, which place the galaxy at an estimated redshift of 0.37 [arXiv:2104.13333].

sources.summary: The paper identifies O ii and NaD features in 3FHL J0102.8 2001, estimates z=0.37, notes mild disagreement with a 1WHSP lower limit, and reports its HSP blazar status.

quotes.quote: The optical spectrum for this source showed an emission feature at 5107 (EW=18 ) and an absorption feature at 8084 . These features have been attributed to O ii and NaD, respectively. This gives us an estimate of z= 0.37 for the source. This result is in mild disagreement with previously reported value by in the 1WHSP catalog, which found a lower limit of z> 0.38 for this source.
This source has been reported as an HSP blazar in 4LAC.

gmw_00013034.name: 3FHL J1042.2 4128
gmw_00013034.overview: 3FHL J1042.2 4128 is classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2104.13333] and has been reported as a high-synchrotron-peaked blazar [arXiv:2104.13333]. Its optical spectrum exhibits G-band and magnesium I absorption features, establishing a lower limit redshift of z > 0.21 [arXiv:2104.13333].

sources.summary: The paper identifies G-band and Mg i absorption in 3FHL J1042.2 4128, sets z>0.21, compares a previous featureless SOAR spectrum, and classifies the source as a BL Lac.

quotes.quote: In the optical spectrum for this source, features identified were; G band absorption feature at 5210 and Mg i feature at 6274 , respectively. These two features allowed us to establish a lower limit redshift of z> 0.21 for this source. A featureless optical spectrum for this source was obtained by using the 4.1 m SOAR telescope at Cerro Pachón, Chile. We classify this source as a BL Lac. This source has been reported as an HSP blazar in 4LAC.

gmw_00013035.name: 3FHL J1225.4 3447
gmw_00013035.overview: 3FHL J1225.4 3447 is a high-synchrotron-peaked blazar [arXiv:2104.13333] classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2104.13333]. Its optical spectrum exhibits G-band and calcium-iron absorption features, indicating a redshift greater than 0.39 [arXiv:2104.13333].

sources.summary: The paper reports G-band and Ca+Fe absorption in 3FHL J1225.4 3447, derives z>0.39, classifies it as a BL Lac, and notes its HSP blazar status.

quotes.quote: In the optical spectrum for this source, two absorption features were detected at 5994 , corresponding to G band, and 7336 , corresponding to Ca + Fe lines, respectively. This produces a redshift of z> 0.39 for the source, which we classify as a BL Lac. This source has been reported as an HSP blazar in 4LAC.

gmw_00013036.name: 3FHL J1328.9 5607
gmw_00013036.overview: 3FHL J1328.9 5607 is classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2104.13333]. Its optical spectrum exhibits G band and Mg I absorption features, which establish a lower redshift limit of z > 0.17 [arXiv:2104.13333].

sources.summary: The paper identifies absorption features in noisy spectra of 3FHL J1328.9 5607, derives z>0.17, notes consistency with a prior limit, and classifies the source as a BL Lac.

quotes.quote: The optical spectrum obtained for this source was noisy, but we were able to identify two absorption features at 5063 and 6099 , ascribed to G band and Mg i lines, respectively. This yields a lower limit of z> 0.17 for this source. Our proposed lower limit value is compatible with the value reported by of z> 0.13.
While the authors were unable to determine whether the source is a BL Lac or an FSRQ, they reported it as an LSP blazar, in agreement with 4LAC. We classify this source as a BL Lac.

gmw_00013037.name: 3FHL J1339.0 2359
gmw_00013037.overview: The blazar 3FHL J1339.0 2359 is classified as a flat-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:2104.13333]. It has a measured redshift of z = 0.65 and exhibits possible oxygen emission features [arXiv:2104.13333].

sources.summary: The paper notes 3FHL J1339.0 2359 was reported as a BL Lac, finds emission features consistent with z=0.65, classifies it as an FSRQ, and cites prior redshift work.

quotes.quote: This source has been reported as a BL Lac in the 4FGL catalog with a z of 0.657. Possible emission features at 6165 (O ii , EW = 47 ) and 8287 (O iii , EW = 22 ) agree with the measured redshift of z= 0.65. We classify this source as an FSRQ. The redshift for this source was first measured by , in the Optical Characteristic of Astrometric Radio Sources (OCARS) catalog, which was then referred by the VLBI catalog by . This source has been reported as an LSP blazar in 4LAC.

gmw_00013038.name: 3FHL J1353.8 3936
gmw_00013038.overview: 3FHL J1353.8 3936 is classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2104.13333]. It has also been identified as a high-synchrotron-peaked blazar with a redshift greater than 0.49 [arXiv:2104.13333].

sources.summary: The paper uses two absorption features in the noisy spectrum of 3FHL J1353.8 3936 to derive z>0.49, classifies it as a BL Lac, and notes its HSP blazar status.

quotes.quote: Our fairly noisy optical spectrum for this source allowed us to find two absorption features at 6440 (G band) and 7875 (Ca + Fe), respectively, yielding a redshift of z> 0.49. We classify this source as a BL Lac. This source has been reported as an HSP blazar in 4LAC.

gmw_00013039.name: 3FHL J1442.5 4621
gmw_00013039.overview: 3FHL J1442.5 4621 is a BL Lacertae object and blazar [arXiv:2104.13333]. Spectroscopic observations of the source reveal several absorption lines alongside an O III emission line, confirming a redshift of z=0.102 [arXiv:2104.13333].

sources.summary: The paper reports absorption and emission lines for 3FHL J1442.5 4621, derives z=0.102 confirming 6dF, and says its results agree with the 4FGL BL Lac classification.

quotes.quote: This source displays absorption lines at 5698 , 5806 , and 6492 , which are attributed to Mg i , Ca + Fe, and NaD lines, respectively. An emission line at 5517 \ (O iii , EW = 1.5 ) was also observed. This leads to a redshift of z= 0.102, confirming the measurement by the 6dF Galaxy Survey 6df . This source has been classified as a BL Lac in the 4FGL catalog. Our results are in agreement with this classification.

gmw_00013040.name: 3FHL J1542.1 2915
gmw_00013040.overview: 3FHL J1542.1 2915 is classified as a high-synchrotron-peaked blazar [arXiv:2104.13333]. Due to a noisy, featureless spectrum, the object is also tentatively classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2104.13333].

sources.summary: The paper says 3FHL J1542.1 2915 had a noisy featureless spectrum, tentatively classifies the source as a BL Lac, and notes its HSP blazar classification in 4LAC.

quotes.quote: A particularly noisy spectrum was obtained for this source, due to which we were unable to identify any features. Thus, we tentatively classify this source as a BL Lac. This source is an HSP blazar in 4LAC.

gmw_00013041.name: 3FHL J1647.3 6438
gmw_00013041.overview: 3FHL J1647.3 6438 is classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2104.13333] and a low-synchrotron-peaked blazar [arXiv:2104.13333]. Its optical spectrum exhibits a calcium II absorption doublet, which establishes a lower redshift limit of z > 0.82 [arXiv:2104.13333].

sources.summary: The paper finds a Ca ii absorption doublet in 3FHL J1647.3 6438, sets z>0.82, classifies it as a BL Lac, and notes its LSP blazar classification.

quotes.quote: The optical spectrum for this source exhibits an absorption doublet at 7195 \ and, 7230 . These lines correspond to Ca ii lines, yielding a lower limit of z> 0.82. We classify this source as a BL Lac. This source is classified as an LSP blazar in 4LAC.

gmw_00013042.name: 3FHL J1849.2 1647
gmw_00013042.overview: 3FHL J1849.2 1647 is classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2104.13333] and a high-synchrotron-peaked blazar [arXiv:2104.13333]. Its optical spectrum exhibits three absorption features, which suggest a redshift lower limit of either 0.64 or 0.67 [arXiv:2104.13333].

sources.summary: The paper reports three absorption features for 3FHL J1849.2 1647, gives two possible redshift lower limits, classifies the source as a BL Lac, and notes its HSP blazar class.

quotes.quote: The optical spectrum for this source displayed three absorption features at 8537 , 8659 , and 8805 . If the first two features are attributed to Mg i and Ca + Fe, then we obtain z> 0.64. On the other hand, if the feature at 8659 \ is due to Mg i , then the feature at 8805 can be attributed to Ca + Fe, yielding z> 0.67. We classify this source as a BL Lac. This source is classified as an HSP blazar in 4LAC.

gmw_00013043.name: 3FHL J1944.4 4523
gmw_00013043.overview: 3FHL J1944.4 4523 is classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2104.13333]. Its optical spectrum exhibits three absorption features that yield a redshift greater than 0.68, though the source has no reported spectral energy distribution class in the Fermi LAT catalogs [arXiv:2104.13333].

sources.summary: The paper detects three absorption features in 3FHL J1944.4 4523, derives z>0.68, classifies the source as a BL Lac, and notes no reported Fermi SED class.

quotes.quote: In the optical spectrum for this source, three absorption features were detected. These three features are at 7274 (G band), 8704 (Mg i ), and 8916 (Ca + Fe), yielding z> 0.68. We classify this source as a BL Lac. This source has no reported SED class in the Fermi LAT catalogs.

gmw_00013044.name: 3FHL J1944.9 2143
gmw_00013044.overview: 3FHL J1944.9 2143 is classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2104.13333]. Optical spectroscopy of the source reveals magnesium and calcium-iron absorption features, which establish a lower redshift limit of z > 0.41 [arXiv:2104.13333].

sources.summary: The paper finds Mg i and Ca+Fe absorption in 3FHL J1944.9 2143, derives z>0.41, notes agreement with 2WHSP, and classifies the source as a BL Lac.

quotes.quote: In this optical spectrum, absorption features were detected at 7289 and 7435 . These correspond to Mg i and Ca + Fe lines, respectively, yielding a lower limit, z> 0.41. This result agrees with the value reported by in the 2WHSP catalog. This lower limit value was obtained by the authors using the same technique as the 1WHSP catalog (described for the source 3FHL J0102.8--2001). We classify this source as a BL Lac.

gmw_00013045.name: 3FHL J1955.0 5640
gmw_00013045.overview: 3FHL J1955.0 5640 is classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2104.13333]. Optical spectroscopy of the source reveals two absorption features attributed to its host galaxy, establishing a lower redshift limit of z > 0.22 [arXiv:2104.13333].

sources.summary: The paper identifies two absorption features in 3FHL J1955.0 5640, derives z>0.22, classifies it as a BL Lac, and notes prior host-galaxy attribution of similar features.

quotes.quote: In the optical spectrum for this source, two absorption features were identified at 5255 (G band) and 7198 (NaD). This gives us a lower limit of z> 0.22 for this source. We classify this source as a BL Lac. Our results are in mild disagreement with the redshift value reported by of z= 0.22. The authors also observed only absorption features for this source that they attributed to the host galaxy instead.

gmw_00013046.name: 3FHL J2058.8 1442
gmw_00013046.overview: 3FHL J2058.8 1442 is a BL Lacertae object located at a confirmed redshift of z=0.07 [arXiv:2104.13333]. Optical observations of the source reveal O III emission and Mg I absorption features, though it has no reported spectral energy distribution class in the Fermi LAT catalogs [arXiv:2104.13333].

sources.summary: The paper reports O iii emission and Mg i absorption in 3FHL J2058.8 1442, confirms z=0.07, classifies it as a BL Lac, and notes no Fermi SED class.

quotes.quote: This source displayed an emission peak at 5375 that is due to O iii (EW = 0.4 ) and an absorption feature at 5574 , due to Mg i . This allows us to confirm the redshift of z= 0.07 for this source as reported by . We classify this source as a BL Lac. This source has no reported SED class in the Fermi LAT catalogs.

gmw_00013047.name: source 1
gmw_00013047.overview: Source 1 is a high-redshift galaxy candidate with a primary redshift solution at z=12.1 [arXiv:2208.02825]. It is securely detected in two MIRI bands and has a best-fit dust extinction of A_V=2.5 mag [arXiv:2208.02825].

sources.summary: The paper highlights source 1 as a z~12.1 high-redshift galaxy candidate with secure MIRI detections, main-sequence placement at z>8, and best-fit dust extinction of A_V=2.5 mag.

quotes.quote: In particular, we highlight source 1, the only object securely detected even in two MIRI bands (F770W and F1000W). The NIRCAM+MIRI photometry provides stronger constraints on the SED fitting, turning a primary solution at z=12.1 (see Fig fig:example_SED , with the source sitting on the extrapolated MS at z>8 (see Fig. fig:MS bottom-right). Compared to the other UV bright sources at the same cosmic epoch, 1 has an extinction best-fit of A_V=2.5 mag. A secondary dusty solution at z=3.4 is disadvantaged by the ^2 .

gmw_00013048.name: SDSS J1321-0041
gmw_00013048.overview: SDSS J1321-0041 is an extreme iron low-ionization broad absorption line (FeLoBAL) quasar [arXiv:2310.06216]. The galaxy hosts a strong broad absorption line outflow with sufficient kinetic luminosity to contribute to active galactic nucleus feedback [arXiv:2310.06216].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes SDSS J1321-0041 as an extreme FeLoBAL quasar, measuring ionic columns, density, outflow distance, kinetic luminosity, and concluding the outflow is strong enough to contribute to AGN feedback.

quotes.quote: We have identified a broad absorption line (BAL) outflow in the VLT/UVES spectrum of the quasar SDSS J1321-0041. The outflow shows troughs from Fe ii , and is therefore identified as an FeLoBAL. It is quite extreme among that population, as it shows C ii and Si ii BALs.

For this reason, we analyzed the spectrum of J1321-0041 to determine the outflow's kinetic luminosity, as well as the quasar's bolometric luminosity. We measured the ionic column densities from the absorption troughs in the spectrum, and determined the Hydrogen column density and ionization parameter using those column densities as our constraints. We also determined the electron number density n_e based on the ratios between excited state and resonance state column densities of Fe ii and Si ii .

We determined the kinetic luminosity of the outflow to be 8.4^ +13.2 _ 5.3 10^ 45 erg s ^ 1 , and the quasar's bolometric luminosity to be 1.72 0.13 10^ 47 erg s ^ 1 , resulting in a ratio of E _k/L_ Bol =4.8^ +7.7 _ 3.1 . We conclude that this outflow has sufficient kinetic luminosity to contribute to AGN feedback.

gmw_00013049.name: J123727.68+621036.3
gmw_00013049.overview: J123727.68+621036.3 is a radio-loud active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1207.2157]. The galaxy is extended in the radio regime and is identified as a possible Fanaroff-Riley class I source with twin radio jets [arXiv:1207.2157].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J123727.68+621036.3 as a possible FRI source and radio-loud AGN, notes radio extension and twin jets, and excludes it from the analysis.

quotes.quote: One of the three CDF-N sources, J123727.68+621036.3 appears to be a possible FRI source see e.g., 2003ApJS..146..267M . It is extended in radio and therefore it was not included in the original radio catalog that we cross-matched (see Section sec:cdf_sfr ). As this sources appears to be a radio-loud AGN with twin radio jets, we excluded it from the following analysis.

gmw_00013050.name: PKS 2142-758
gmw_00013050.overview: PKS 2142-758 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar located at a redshift of 1.139 [arXiv:1206.1782]. It is classified as a blazar that exhibits infrequent gamma-ray flares and rare active states [arXiv:1206.1782].

sources.summary: The paper treats PKS 2142-758 as a z=1.139 flat-spectrum radio quasar with infrequent gamma-ray flares, using a 2011 multiwavelength campaign to build an active-state SED and assess high-energy emission.

quotes.quote: PKS 2142-758 is a flat spectrum radio quasar at a redshift of 1.139 Jauncey1978 and it is not among the brightest Fermi detected extragalactic sources. Typical ray flares of this source reach a flux of around 1.0 10^ 6 ph cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 in the 100 MeV--300 GeV energy range and they happen infrequently. This object will be part of a broader study of blazars which are not the most extreme ray sources.

During the latest flaring period in 2011 a multi wavelength observing campaign was carried out using the Ceduna radio telescope, the Australian Telescope Compact Array (ATCA), the TANAMI VLBI Array, Swift , the Rapid Eye Mount Telescope (REM), and the Large Area Telescope (LAT) on board Fermi . These quasi-simultaneous data were used to construct a broadband SED of this object in its rare active state. We present these observations and the resulting SED and some preliminary analysis of the constraints they place on the high energy emission from this object.

gmw_00013051.name: G1
gmw_00013051.overview: G1 is a moderately faint galaxy with a stellar mass of approximately 10 to the power of 8.6 solar masses [arXiv:1612.06401]. It has a tentative detection in the F336W band but remains undetected in VIMOS U-band imaging [arXiv:1612.06401].

sources.summary: The paper treats G1 as a clean-sample galaxy with tentative 3-sigma F336W flux, wide-band SED coverage, moderate faintness, a stellar-mass estimate, and no VIMOS U detection.

quotes.quote: For two galaxies ( G1 and G2 ) we find a potential detection with 3 significance. In Fig. fig5 we show the images in different wavelengths of the four galaxies with flux detected at 3 . We also include their spectral energy distributions (SEDs) built over a wide spectral range. Galaxies G1 and G2 from the clean sample are moderately faint ( i_ 775 27.3 mag), while GC1 and GC2 from the contaminated sample are relatively bright ( i_ 775 24.7 mag).

Stellar masses of M_ /M_ 8.6, 9.2, 9.4 and 9.7 are obtained from the SED modelling Santini2015 for G1 , G2 , GC1 and GC2 , respectively. Furthermore, the galaxies G1 and G2 are not detected in the VIMOS U image. The non-detection of these two galaxies in this image therefore makes their detection in the F336W image tentative at best.

gmw_00013052.name: G2
gmw_00013052.overview: G2 is a moderately faint galaxy with an estimated stellar mass of approximately 1.58 billion solar masses [arXiv:1612.06401]. It exhibits an excess in K-band flux, which suggests the presence of strong emission lines at its redshift [arXiv:1612.06401].

sources.summary: The paper describes G2 as a clean-sample galaxy with tentative 3-sigma F336W flux, SED and stellar-mass estimates, K-band excess from strong lines, and no VIMOS U detection.

quotes.quote: For two galaxies ( G1 and G2 ) we find a potential detection with 3 significance. In Fig. fig5 we show the images in different wavelengths of the four galaxies with flux detected at 3 . We also include their spectral energy distributions (SEDs) built over a wide spectral range. Galaxies G1 and G2 from the clean sample are moderately faint ( i_ 775 27.3 mag), while GC1 and GC2 from the contaminated sample are relatively bright ( i_ 775 24.7 mag).

Stellar masses of M_ /M_ 8.6, 9.2, 9.4 and 9.7 are obtained from the SED modelling Santini2015 for G1 , G2 , GC1 and GC2 , respectively. Galaxies G2 and GC2 show an excess in the K band flux, suggesting that strong H + 4959,5007 emission lines enter the K band at their respective redshifts. Furthermore, the galaxies G1 and G2 are not detected in the VIMOS U image. The non-detection of these two galaxies in this image therefore makes their detection in the F336W image tentative at best.

gmw_00013053.name: GC1
gmw_00013053.overview: GC1 is a relatively bright galaxy with a stellar mass estimated at approximately 10 to the power of 9.4 solar masses [arXiv:1612.06401]. Its observed signal, once interpreted as Lyman continuum emission, is likely contamination from an interloping galaxy at a redshift of approximately 1.6 [arXiv:1612.06401].

sources.summary: The paper describes GC1 as a contaminated-sample galaxy with high flux-detection significance, wide-band SED coverage, relative brightness, a stellar-mass estimate, and a likely z 1.6 interloper signal.

quotes.quote: Fluxes for this sample are similarly crowded around zero, but there are two notable detections ( GC1 and GC2 ) with high ( 4 and 7.5 ) significance. In Fig. fig5 we show the images in different wavelengths of the four galaxies with flux detected at 3 . We also include their spectral energy distributions (SEDs) built over a wide spectral range. Galaxies G1 and G2 from the clean sample are moderately faint ( i_ 775 27.3 mag), while GC1 and GC2 from the contaminated sample are relatively bright ( i_ 775 24.7 mag).

Stellar masses of M_ /M_ 8.6, 9.2, 9.4 and 9.7 are obtained from the SED modelling Santini2015 for G1 , G2 , GC1 and GC2 , respectively. All four galaxies were also detected in the F336W image by Rafelski2015 using proper aperture-matched PSF corrected photometry, though we note that detections are of low significance in all cases with the exception of GC2 . Object GC1 has been already discussed by Vanzella2012 , who argue that the signal, believed to be LyC emission, is in fact emission from a z 1.6 interloping galaxy.

gmw_00013054.name: SWIRE3-J033234.23-293450.8
gmw_00013054.overview: SWIRE3-J033234.23-293450.8 is a starburst galaxy detected across all IRAC and MIPS bands [arXiv:1005.4353]. It is a strong 24-micrometer emitter with optical and infrared spectral energy distributions that confirm its starburst nature [arXiv:1005.4353].

sources.summary: The paper describes SWIRE3-J033234.23-293450.8 as the lowest-redshift H/ULIRG in the sample, a strong 24 micrometer emitter with IRAC/MIPS detections, emission lines, and starburst SED/diagnostic evidence.

quotes.quote: SWIRE3-J033234.23-293450.8 : It is one of the strongest 24 m emitters among all H/ULIRGs of our sample and also the one with the lowest redshift ( z=0.529 ). It has been detected in all IRAC and MIPS bands. Balmer lines, [ OII ] and [ OIII ] doublet are detected at its spectra. Both optical and IR SED fitting imply the presence of a starburst. Starburst's presence is also judged by the emission lines diagnostic diagrams (Figure 5).

gmw_00013055.name: SWIRE3-J033541.23-283414.0
gmw_00013055.overview: SWIRE3-J033541.23-283414.0 is a galaxy at a redshift of z=0.579 [arXiv:1005.4353]. It is a strong 24-micrometer emitter that hosts both a starburst and an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1005.4353].

sources.summary: The paper describes SWIRE3-J033541.23-283414.0 as a strong 24 micrometer emitter at z=0.579, with optical lines indicating a starburst plus broad H/MgII and SED evidence for AGN contribution.

quotes.quote: SWIRE3-J033541.23-283414.0 : The R-band magnitude of this source is R=20.60 . It is a strong 24 m emitter with detections in all IRAC and MIPS bands. As it can be seen by its spectra there is a very strong [ OII ] line. Very strong [ OIII ] doublet and H are clearly visible.

These narrow lines, based on the results of BPT diagrams, indicate the presence of a starburst. The detection of a quite broad H with a MgII line confirms the presence of an AGN. Both optical and IR SED fitting imply the presence of a starburst but with AGN contribution. The redshift of this sources is z=0.579 .

gmw_00013056.name: SWIRE3-J033532.19-284801.1
gmw_00013056.overview: SWIRE3-J033532.19-284801.1 is a young elliptical galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.807 [arXiv:1005.4353]. It hosts an active galactic nucleus with a dust torus and exhibits emission lines indicating active galactic nucleus activity [arXiv:1005.4353].

sources.summary: The paper presents SWIRE3-J033532.19-284801.1 as the strongest 24 micrometer emitter in the sample at z=0.807, with emission lines indicating AGN activity and SED fits implying a young elliptical galaxy plus AGN dust torus.

quotes.quote: SWIRE3-J033532.19-284801.1 : The R-band magnitude of this source is R=21.55 . It is the strongest 24 m emitter of our sample with detections in all IRAC and MIPS bands. As it can be seen by its spectra there is a strong [ OII ] line. H and [ OIII ] doublet lines are also visible but weaker.

A weak and broad MgII line is also visible at the blue spectrum. These lines indicate the presence AGN events. The source lies at z = 0.807 . Optical SED fitting imply the presence of a young elliptical galaxy and the IR SED fitting an AGN dust torus.

gmw_00013057.name: SWIRE3-J033220.88-293140.5
gmw_00013057.overview: SWIRE3-J033220.88-293140.5 is an ultraluminous infrared galaxy located at a redshift of z = 1.180 [arXiv:1005.4353]. It hosts a strong active galactic nucleus identified by optical and infrared spectral energy distribution fitting, as well as broad CIII and MgII emission lines [arXiv:1005.4353].

sources.summary: The paper describes SWIRE3-J033220.88-293140.5 as the strongest 24 micrometer ULIRG at z=1.180, detected in IRAC and 24 micrometer bands, with broad CIII/MgII lines and strong AGN evidence from spectra and SEDs.

quotes.quote: SWIRE3-J033220.88-293140.5 : It is the strongest 24 m emitter among all 5 ULIRGs, with detections in all IRAC bands and at 24 m. The R-band magnitude of this source is R=19.81 . We have detected 2 strong broad lines CIII and MgII . The presence of these lines makes it almost certain the presence of a very strong AGN which is also identified by optical and IR SED fitting. This source lies at z = 1.180 .

gmw_00013058.name: SWIRE3-J033528.91-283203.6
gmw_00013058.overview: SWIRE3-J033528.91-283203.6 is an ultraluminous infrared galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.962 [arXiv:1005.4353]. Spectral and energy distribution analyses indicate the presence of an active galactic nucleus alongside a starburst contribution [arXiv:1005.4353].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SWIRE3-J033528.91-283203.6 as the brightest ULIRG in the sample at z=1.962, detected in IRAC and 24 micrometer bands, with broad Ly, CIV, and CIII lines and AGN plus starburst SED evidence.

quotes.quote: SWIRE3-J033528.91-283203.6 : This ULIRG is the brightest one of our sample with R-band magnitude R=19.32 . It has been detected in all IRAC bands and at 24 m. As it can be seen by its spectra there are 3 strong and broad detected lines Ly , CIV and CIII . Both optical and IR SED fitting imply the presence of an AGN with significant starburst contribution. The redshift of this sources is z=1.962 .

gmw_00013059.name: SWIRE3-J033144.54-290505.6
gmw_00013059.overview: SWIRE3-J033144.54-290505.6 is a hyper-luminous infrared galaxy at a redshift of z=3.2 [arXiv:1005.4353]. It hosts a quasar, as indicated by the detection of four broad ultraviolet emission lines and spectral energy distribution fitting [arXiv:1005.4353].

sources.summary: The paper describes SWIRE3-J033144.54-290505.6 as the sample's only HLIRG, an extremely luminous high-redshift source detected in most IRAC/MIPS bands, with broad ultraviolet lines indicating a QSO.

quotes.quote: SWIRE3-J033144.54-290505.6 : This is the only HLIRG in our sample with bolometric infrared luminosity L_ IR =10^ 14.16 L_ . This is one of the brightest ( R=19.96 ) sources at the highest redshift ( z=3.2 ) in this sample. It has been detected in all IRAC and MIPS bands except 160 m. This source exhibits 4 broad lines Ly , NV , SiIV and CIV . The clear detection of these lines indicates the presence of a QSO in agreement with SED fitting.

gmw_00013060.name: M-8137-12703
gmw_00013060.overview: M-8137-12703 is an emission line galaxy characterized by a circumnuclear region with an intermediate configuration [arXiv:2005.12744]. Its structure features a disc and spaxels displaying both H II region-like and intermediate spectra [arXiv:2005.12744].

sources.summary: The paper presents M-8137-12703 as an INT-configuration MaNGA galaxy, showing H ii and intermediate spaxels plus radial H, gas-dispersion, and BPT-ratio distributions.

quotes.quote: As an example, the upper panel of Fig. figure:int-example shows the locations of the spaxels with the H ii region-like and and intermediate spectra on the image of the galaxy M-8137-12703. The spaxels of the intermediate type spectra in the circumnuclear region and in the disc are shown by different colors. The circle marks the kinematic center of the galaxy. The other panels of Fig. figure:int-example show the radial distributions of different characteristics (such as the H surface brightness, the gas velocity dispersion, and the BPT emission line ratios).
Fig. figure:int-example shows the galaxy M-8137-12703, an example of galaxies with the circumnuclear regions of the INT configuration.

gmw_00013061.name: M-8249-12703
gmw_00013061.overview: M-8249-12703 is a galaxy characterized by a star-forming circumnuclear region [arXiv:2005.12744]. This central region is surrounded by a ring of spaxels exhibiting intermediate-type spectra [arXiv:2005.12744].

sources.summary: The paper identifies M-8249-12703 as an SF+INT MaNGA example, with an SF-type circumnuclear region surrounded by intermediate-spectrum spaxels and radial emission-line diagnostics.

quotes.quote: In a several MaNGA galaxies (M-8137-09102, M-8249-12703, M-9500-12702), the circumnuclear region of the SF type is surrounded by a ring of spaxels of intermediate type spectra. For instance, panel a of Fig. figure:sf-ring-int shows the distribution of the H ii region-like spaxels (blue symbols), the spaxels with the intermediate spectra in the circumnuclear ring (red symbols), and the spaxels with the intermediate spectra in the disc (orange symbols). The circle marks the kinematical center of the galaxy. Panels b, , d, and e of Fig. figure:sf-ring-int show the radial distributions of different characteristics (surface brightness in the H emission line, gas velocity dispersion, and BPT line ratios) for this galaxy.

Fig. figure:sf-ring-int shows the galaxy M-8249-12703, an example of galaxies with the circumnuclear regions of the SF+INT configuration. The sequence of those configurations (from the AGN+INT through the INT (or SF+INT) to the SF) can be explained by an increase of the contribution of the SF to the radiation in the circumnuclear region.

gmw_00013062.name: Galaxy 1
gmw_00013062.overview: Galaxy 1 is an emission-line galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.2055 [arXiv:1710.03351]. It is positioned behind the main rim of a planetary nebula, which contaminates its hydrogen emission line [arXiv:1710.03351].

sources.summary: Galaxy 1 is reported at z=0.2055 behind the PN rim; its weak nitrogen/sulphur lines and integrated line redshifts are measured, while H is likely contaminated by PN emission.

quotes.quote: Galaxy 1 ( z=0.2055 ): J215933.45 392343.9 This galaxy is completely covered by the main rim of the PN. The spectral regions around the emission lines are given in Fig. G1_image . The nitrogen lines are only upper limits and the sulphur lines marginally detected since they are near the noise level. From the integrated spectrum of all 3 frames we obtained individual redshifts for the lines (.f. Table G1_tab ).

The H line shows a slightly smaller profile and a slightly lower redshift then all other lines. The profile even was somewhat smaller than that measured on night sky lines. Since we removed a very strong He i line of the PN at its red edge we assume a contamination and use only the blue part for a final fit of the intensity.

gmw_00013063.name: Galaxy 2
gmw_00013063.overview: Galaxy 2 is a faint emission-line galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.3720 [arXiv:1710.03351]. It is situated near a group of low-surface-brightness objects and exhibits exceptionally bright oxygen emission [arXiv:1710.03351].

sources.summary: Galaxy 2 is a faint z=0.3720 emission-line galaxy near other low-surface-brightness objects; its lines contrast well with the sky and its O ii emission is exceptionally bright.

quotes.quote: Galaxy 2 ( z=0.3720 ): J215931.28 392425.4 This galaxy appears very faint on the HST/F814W image. There is a whole group of such low surface brightness objects on the image visible within a radius of about 0.5 . However, the emission lines are in good contrast to the sky background and the noise (see Fig. G2_image , Table G2_tab )). The galaxy is, although dust extinction would affect the oxygen line luminosity more than the hydrogen lines, exceptionally bright in its O ii emission (Table G2_tab ).

gmw_00013064.name: Galaxy 3
gmw_00013064.overview: Galaxy 3 is an emission-line galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 0.4937 [arXiv:1710.03351]. It is the brightest of its associated sources in the optical continuum, and its emission-line ratios indicate that it is a composite-type galaxy [arXiv:1710.03351].

sources.summary: Galaxy 3 at z=0.4937 is linked to a USNO source and probable GALEX NUV detection; it is the brightest optical-continuum source and its line ratios indicate a composite type.

quotes.quote: Galaxy 3 ( z=0.4937 ): J215944.0 392015 The coordinates in the slit of galaxy 3 correspond fairly well to that of the source USNO B1 0506 0808786. Its m_ DSS J =21 1 also fits to the brightness of the continuum in the spectrum. The NED source GALEXASC J215944.22-392017.3 is 5 westwards half way to the about 15 magnitude 2MASS 21594450-3920191 source, which certainly was not in the slit of our spectrum. Although the coordinates of the GALEX source and the uncertainty of only 2 0 given in galex do not fit well, we tend to believe that we identified this source since it was detected only in the NUV band of the GALEX as a 5 source (Fig. G3_id ).

Despite the redshift of nearly 0.5 it is the brightest of the sources in the optical continuum (Fig. G3_image ). Values of ( O iii / H ) = -0.09 and ( O ii / H ) = +0.41 (Table G3_tab ) also indicate the galaxy being of composite type in the scheme of line_ratio2 .

gmw_00013065.name: Galaxy 4
gmw_00013065.overview: Galaxy 4 is a high-redshift emission line galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.8668 [arXiv:1710.03351]. Situated just northeast of Galaxy 3, it is the only target in its sample with an identified magnesium II ultraviolet line [arXiv:1710.03351].

sources.summary: Galaxy 4 is a z=0.8668 target just northeast of Galaxy 3, the highest-redshift source in the sample, and the only one with an identified Mg ii UV line.

quotes.quote: Galaxy 4 ( z=0.8668 ): J215943.7 392012 This target is only 3 2 northeast of galaxy 3. However, we found it to be the target with the highest redshift in our sample (Fig. G4_image ). It is the only target in our sample where the [ Mg ii ] 279.8 nm UV line could be identified in the spectra, too (Table G4_tab ).

gmw_00013066.name: Galaxy 5
gmw_00013066.overview: Galaxy 5 is an emission line galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.7424 [arXiv:1710.03351]. Because it lies very near the center of a planetary nebula, variable nebula emission lines prevent complete subtraction and obscure the galaxy's magnesium and hydrogen lines [arXiv:1710.03351].

sources.summary: Galaxy 5 at z=0.7424 lies near the PN center, where variable PN emission prevents complete subtraction; Mg ii and the galaxy's H line are obscured by contamination.

quotes.quote: Galaxy 5 ( z=0.7424 ): J215935.61 2317.5 This target is very near to the PN center. Thus, the large variations of the PN emission lines do not allow a complete removal of them (Fig. G5_spec ). Although the redshift should allow the detection of the [ Mg ii ] 279.8 nm UV line, we could not detect it as its wavelength coincidences exactly with that of the H line of the PN. The H line of the galaxy is lost in telluric emission lines of the OH molecule (Table G5_tab ).

gmw_00013067.name: Galaxy 6
gmw_00013067.overview: Galaxy 6 is an emission line galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.3137 [arXiv:1710.03351]. It lies behind a faint planetary nebula halo and is identified with a very weak WISE infrared source [arXiv:1710.03351].

sources.summary: Galaxy 6 at z=0.3137 lies behind the faint PN halo; key lines are lost to OH contamination, and the authors identify it with a weak WISE source despite positional mismatch.

quotes.quote: Galaxy 6 ( z=0.3137 ): J215942.4 392530 This target is located behind the thin faint PN halo. Although the wavelength range covers the spectral region around H , the [ N ii ] and [ S ii ] lines were not visible due to strong OH line contamination. (Fig. G6_spec , Table G5_tab ).

The positional uncertainties of 0 6 given in the catalog of WISE J215942.81 392533.2 do not fit to the 3 5 distance to our target. Nevertheless, we believe these objects to be identical. Visual inspection of the WISE image AllWISE at CDS/Aladin aladin show a very weak source at the detection limit and the bright stellar source WISE J215941.03 392547.1 nearby has a FWHM of 8 2 on the image (Fig. G6_id ). Thus the realistic positional error in the WISE data should be higher.

gmw_00013068.name: UGC 10655
gmw_00013068.overview: UGC 10655 is a galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 0.02869 [arXiv:2210.11752]. It served as the host galaxy for the supernova SN 2020rea and has an estimated metallicity of 12+log(O/H) = 8.56 [arXiv:2210.11752].

sources.summary: UGC 10655 is SN 2020rea's host at redshift 0.02869; the paper uses host proximity, extinction, and FLOYDS emission-line spectra to estimate 12+log(O/H)=8.56.

quotes.quote: SN 2020rea was spotted by Supernova and Gravitational Lenses Follow up (SGLF) team in the Zwicky Transient Facility (ZTF) data 2020TNSTR2463....1P on August 11, 2020 (JD=2459072.702) in the host galaxy UGC 10655 at a redshift of 0.02869 0.00015 1999PASP..111..438F .

SN 2020rea lies in the proximity of the host galaxy and hence extinction due to the host galaxy is also expected. To estimate the extinction due to the host galaxy we used the equivalent width of Na i D line in the spectra.

We have calculated the metallicity of the host galaxy of SN 2020rea using narrow emission line fluxes in the host galaxy spectrum taken on August 15, 2020 with LCO's FLOYDS spectrograph at Faulkes Telescope North (FTN). Prominent lines of H , [ N II ] , etc. are present in the host spectrum. There are several methods to measure the metallicity 1991ApJ...380..140M, Kewley_2002, 10.1111/j.1365-2966.2004.07591.x, Pilyugin_2005 . These calculations involve flux measurements of various emission lines. Using the N2 index calibration of , we estimate the metallicity of the host galaxy as 12+log(O/H) = 8.56 0.18 dex.

gmw_00013069.name: DLA towards SDSS J 081634 + 144612
aliases_external: DLA at z_abs=3.287 toward SDSS J081634+144612
gmw_00013069.overview: The damped Lyman-alpha system towards SDSS J081634+144612 is a high-redshift galaxy at z = 3.287 with an extremely large neutral hydrogen column density [arXiv:1205.2322]. Its interstellar medium contains dust and two molecular hydrogen absorption components with a kinetic temperature of approximately 75 K [arXiv:1205.2322].

sources.summary: The paper presents the z=3.287 QSO-DLA toward SDSS J081634+144612 as an extremely high-H i high-redshift system with measured metal abundances, molecular hydrogen components, ISM dust, and constrained H2-cloud conditions.

quotes.quote: We present the elemental abundance and H _2 content measurements of a Damped Lyman- (DLA) system with an extremely large H i column density, N (H i ) (cm ^ 2 ) = 22.0 0.10, at z_ abs = 3.287 towards the QSO SDSS J 081634 + 144612. We measure column densities of H _2 , C i , C i ^ , Zn ii , Fe ii , Cr ii , Ni ii and Si ii from a high signal-to-noise and high spectral resolution VLT-UVES spectrum. The overall metallicity of the system is [ Zn/H ] = 1.10 0.10 relative to solar. Two molecular hydrogen absorption components are seen at z=3.28667 and 3.28742 (a velocity separation of 52 km s ^ 1 ) in rotational levels up to J=3 .

From the observed abundance ratios we conclude that dust is present in the Interstellar Medium (ISM) of this galaxy, with a enhanced abundance in the H _2 bearing clouds. However, the total amount of dust along the line of sight is not large and does not produce any significant reddening of the background QSO. The physical conditions in the H _2 bearing clouds are constrained directly from the column densities of H _2 in different rotational levels, C i and C i ^ . The kinetic temperature is found to be T 75 K and the particle density lies in the range n_ H = 50 80 cm ^ 3 .

gmw_00013070.name: U4639
gmw_00013070.overview: The galaxy U4639 is classified as a luminous infrared galaxy [arXiv:1204.1152]. Spectral energy distribution fitting reveals that U4639 hosts an active galactic nucleus component at the 3-sigma confidence level, which is associated with a relatively weak X-ray source [arXiv:1204.1152].

sources.summary: The paper finds U4639 has a significant AGN component from SED fitting despite weak visual evidence, and associates it with a relatively weak X-ray source.

quotes.quote: The SED-fitting procedure found that for all but one source (U428) the presence of an AGN is consistent with the photometry, although for only nine sources ( 35 of the sample: U4639, U4950, U4958, U5150, U5152, U5153, U5652, U5805, and U5877) its presence is significant at the 3 confidence level (i.e., solutions with no torus emission have ^ 2 ^ 2 _ min +28.5).
Finally, there are few cases (i.e., U4639) where visual inspection of the SED decomposition is not strongly suggesting an AGN component, which is however required by the SED-fitting analysis.
Of the remaining six sources, one (U4639) has an association with a relatively weak \ source, while for the others only an upper limit to the \ emission can be placed (see Table xray_properties ).

gmw_00013071.name: U4642
gmw_00013071.overview: U4642 is an infrared galaxy that exhibits X-ray-luminous emission [arXiv:1204.1152]. This high luminosity is typical of an active galactic nucleus, suggesting the presence of a moderately obscured central engine [arXiv:1204.1152].

sources.summary: The paper flags U4642 as an X-ray-luminous source suggestive of AGN emission; spectral modeling fits the data but leaves its column density poorly constrained.

quotes.quote: X-ray luminosities, coupled with \ spectral analysis (see below), indicate the presence of an AGN in the three sources (U4950, U4958, and U5877) for which the SED fitting analysis and F10 already suggested an AGN. Also U4642 has an \ luminosity typical of AGN emission.
The same model applied to U4642 data provides good results, although the derived column density is poorly constrained ( N_ H =4.5^ +6.1 _ 4.3 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 ).
The \ luminosity of U4642 is also suggestive of moderately obscured AGN emission.

gmw_00013072.name: U4950
gmw_00013072.overview: U4950 is an infrared-luminous galaxy that hosts an active galactic nucleus detected at the 3-sigma confidence level [arXiv:1204.1152]. The galaxy exhibits a power-law spectral energy distribution and moderate-quality X-ray emission subject to absorption [arXiv:1204.1152].

sources.summary: U4950 is identified as an IR-luminous source with significant AGN emission, a power-law SED, strong X-ray counts, and absorption near 1.2e23 cm^-2.

quotes.quote: The SED-fitting procedure found that for all but one source (U428) the presence of an AGN is consistent with the photometry, although for only nine sources ( 35 of the sample: U4639, U4950, U4958, U5150, U5152, U5153, U5652, U5805, and U5877) its presence is significant at the 3 confidence level (i.e., solutions with no torus emission have ^ 2 ^ 2 _ min +28.5).

At a visual inspection, the presence of a nuclear component can be inferred for sources with a power-law SED (i.e., U4950 and U5877), for sources where the stellar component alone is not able to reproduce the datapoints around 3 m (i.e., U4958, U5153) or for sources where the starburst component, normalized to the far-IR datapoints, has already declined around 5--6 m (i.e. U5152, U5153, and U5805).

U4950 and U5877 are characterized by 1420 and 360 counts in the 0.5--8 keV band, which allow a moderate-quality \ spectral analysis. Both sources can be fitted using a powerlaw model, but the flat photon index ( 1 and + 1, respectively) is indicative of absorption, which has been estimated to be 1.2 ^ +0.3 _ 0.2 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 and 6.0 ^ +2.0 _ 1.5 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 , respectively, after fixing the photon index to 1.8.

gmw_00013073.name: U5153
gmw_00013073.overview: The galaxy U5153 is undetected by the Photodetector Array Camera and Spectrometer [arXiv:1204.1152]. Spectral energy distribution fitting indicates the presence of a starburst component to match mid-infrared data, as well as active galactic nucleus emission at a three-sigma confidence level [arXiv:1204.1152].

sources.summary: U5153 lacks PACS detection, needs a starburst component to match mid-IR data, and shows 3-sigma AGN emission from the SED fitting.

quotes.quote: For the 3 sources undetected by \ (U5050, U5152, and U5153), we consider the conservative 5 upper limits (2.2, 2.0 and 3.0 mJy at 70, 100 and 160 , respectively).

For the three sources with no PACS detection (U5050, U5152, and U5153), the starburst component is required by the SED-fitting procedure to reproduce the mid-IR spectral data, although its shape is not well constrained.

The SED-fitting procedure found that for all but one source (U428) the presence of an AGN is consistent with the photometry, although for only nine sources ( 35 of the sample: U4639, U4950, U4958, U5150, U5152, U5153, U5652, U5805, and U5877) its presence is significant at the 3 confidence level (i.e., solutions with no torus emission have ^ 2 ^ 2 _ min +28.5).

gmw_00013074.name: U5805
gmw_00013074.overview: U5805 is a luminous infrared galaxy that hosts an active galactic nucleus detected at the 3-sigma confidence level [arXiv:1204.1152]. Spectral energy distribution fitting indicates the presence of a nuclear component, with the galaxy's starburst component declining around 5 to 6 micrometers [arXiv:1204.1152].

sources.summary: U5805 is treated as a blended PACS source with far-IR upper limits; SED fitting indicates significant AGN emission and mid-IR evidence for a nuclear component.

quotes.quote: For PACS detections, the typical separation between 24 \ and PEP sources is less than 1 , with the exception of U5805 and U16526 ( 4 ). By visually checking the far-IR images, these sources appear as blends of more than one 24 m source. In these cases, the PACS flux densities are considered as upper limits.

The SED-fitting procedure found that for all but one source (U428) the presence of an AGN is consistent with the photometry, although for only nine sources ( 35 of the sample: U4639, U4950, U4958, U5150, U5152, U5153, U5652, U5805, and U5877) its presence is significant at the 3 confidence level (i.e., solutions with no torus emission have ^ 2 ^ 2 _ min +28.5).

At a visual inspection, the presence of a nuclear component can be inferred for sources with a power-law SED (i.e., U4950 and U5877), for sources where the stellar component alone is not able to reproduce the datapoints around 3 m (i.e., U4958, U5153) or for sources where the starburst component, normalized to the far-IR datapoints, has already declined around 5--6 m (i.e. U5152, U5153, and U5805).

gmw_00013075.name: Draco
gmw_00013075.overview: Draco is a dwarf spheroidal galaxy [arXiv:1910.05017]. It is located at a high latitude, making it a key target for decaying dark matter sensitivity studies [arXiv:1910.05017].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Draco as a high-latitude dSph with a large D factor and favorable LHAASO placement, making it one of the two sources dominating combined decaying-dark-matter sensitivity.

quotes.quote: We see that the combined sensitivity is dominated by two dSphs, Draco and Ursa Major II , with large D factors and favorable locations inside the LHAASO FOV.

In Fig. fig:Lhaaso-separate , we clearly see that the combined sensitivity is in particular dominated by high-latitude sources, such as Draco and Ursa Major II . For these sources, the LHAASO sensitivities would be significantly stronger than HAWC by 1 2 orders of magnitude. This is because LHAASO is located at a higher latitude of approximately 29^ in comparison with HAWC, which is located at the latitude of approximately 19 ^ . The dSphs at high latitudes, such as Draco and Ursa Major II , are located near the edge of the HAWC FOV, such that HAWC is insensitive to them. However, these sources could still contribute significant signals in the LHAASO FOV.
Our results show that the contributions of two sources, dSphs Draco and the Ursa Major II , significantly affect the combined sensitivity.

gmw_00013076.name: Ursa Major II
gmw_00013076.overview: Ursa Major II is a high-latitude dwarf spheroidal galaxy [arXiv:1910.05017]. Due to its large D factor and favorable sky location, it is a dominant source for combined sensitivity studies with the LHAASO observatory [arXiv:1910.05017].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Ursa Major II as a high-latitude dSph with a large D factor and favorable LHAASO placement, making it one of the two sources dominating combined sensitivity.

quotes.quote: We see that the combined sensitivity is dominated by two dSphs, Draco and Ursa Major II , with large D factors and favorable locations inside the LHAASO FOV.

In Fig. fig:Lhaaso-separate , we clearly see that the combined sensitivity is in particular dominated by high-latitude sources, such as Draco and Ursa Major II . For these sources, the LHAASO sensitivities would be significantly stronger than HAWC by 1 2 orders of magnitude. This is because LHAASO is located at a higher latitude of approximately 29^ in comparison with HAWC, which is located at the latitude of approximately 19 ^ . The dSphs at high latitudes, such as Draco and Ursa Major II , are located near the edge of the HAWC FOV, such that HAWC is insensitive to them. However, these sources could still contribute significant signals in the LHAASO FOV.
Our results show that the contributions of two sources, dSphs Draco and the Ursa Major II , significantly affect the combined sensitivity.

gmw_00013077.name: Triangulum II
gmw_00013077.overview: Triangulum II is classified as a dwarf spheroidal galaxy [arXiv:1910.05017]. It possesses one of the largest D factors among selected dwarf spheroidal galaxies, though this value is subject to a considerably large statistical uncertainty [arXiv:1910.05017].

sources.summary: The paper notes Triangulum II has one of the largest D factors and lies near the LHAASO field center, but its large D-factor uncertainty limits its contribution.

quotes.quote: Notably, Triangulum II and Willman 1 have almost the largest D factors among the 19 selected dSphs, and t Triangulum II is located near the center of LHAASO FOV. However, their contributions do not significantly affect the combined sensitivity. The reason is that the statistical uncertainties of their D factors are considerably large, owing to the lack of data from the dSph kinematic observations.

gmw_00013078.name: Ced 110 R1
gmw_00013078.overview: Ced 110 R1 is a compact radio source that is considered a background radio-galaxy candidate [arXiv:astro-ph/0302415]. It exhibits a steep negative spectral index and a weak level of linear polarization [arXiv:astro-ph/0302415].

sources.summary: The paper treats Ced 110 R1 as a background radio-galaxy candidate: the field's strongest new compact radio source, with a steep negative spectrum consistent with extragalactic steep-spectrum sources and weak linear polarization.

quotes.quote: Radio sources with negative spectral indices in our maps without any counterpart in the near-IR are therefore likely to be background objects, and as we are rather far from the Galactic plane, these objects are probably radio galaxies, although pulsars cannot be totally excluded. Ced 110 R1 : The previously unknown compact source Ced 110 R1 is by far the strongest 6 cm and 3.5 cm source in this survey. The rather large negative spectral index, =-1.09 0.03 , can be found among the various classes of extragalactic steep-spectrum sources, but would not be unusual for pulsars, which often have, however, still steeper spectra in this wavelength range (see e.g. \ Kaplan et al. \ ).

Ced 110 R1 can be detected also in the Stokes Q and U images (220 and 160 Jy, respectively), but not in Stokes V. As the I flux density is about 14.7 mJy, these results indicate linear polarization at the level 1.8 . Further observations to investigate the nature of this bright object seem warranted.

gmw_00013079.name: Ced 110 R2
gmw_00013079.overview: Ced 110 R2 is classified as a background radio galaxy that is visible only at the 6 cm wavelength [arXiv:astro-ph/0302415]. It is the second-brightest source at this wavelength, exhibiting a slightly extended elliptical shape and negative spectral-index limits [arXiv:astro-ph/0302415].

sources.summary: The paper classifies Ced 110 R2 among likely background radio galaxies, noting 6 cm-only detection, negative spectral-index limits, slight elliptical extension, second-brightest 6 cm flux, and proximity to CHXR 15.

quotes.quote: Ced 110 R2, Ced 110 R6 and Ced 110 R7 : These sources are visible at 6 cm only and for R2 and R7 the upper limits of the spectral indices are negative, suggesting optically thin synchrotron emission.

Most likely these sources background radio galaxies. Ced 110 R2 and Ced 110 R7 are slightly extended with elliptical shapes. Ced 110 R2 is the second brightest source at 6 cm. It lies at a distance of about 10 from the X-ray source CHXR 15, which Feigelson et al. \ ( ) identified with a star visible on the ESO/SERC Sky Survey plates with an R-band magnitude of 16.0.

gmw_00013080.name: Ced 110 R6
gmw_00013080.overview: Ced 110 R6 is classified as a likely background radio galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0302415]. The source is detected only at 6 cm, has no counterpart in other surveys, and lies in the direction of an extended emission ridge [arXiv:astro-ph/0302415].

sources.summary: The paper groups Ced 110 R6 with likely background radio galaxies, noting its 6 cm-only detection, large spectral-index upper limit near the map edge, no survey counterpart, and a coincidental alignment with extended emission.

quotes.quote: Ced 110 R2, Ced 110 R6 and Ced 110 R7 : These sources are visible at 6 cm only and for R2 and R7 the upper limits of the spectral indices are negative, suggesting optically thin synchrotron emission. The large upper limit of the spectral index of R6 is due to its location close to the outer edge of the map. Most likely these sources background radio galaxies.

The radio sources Ced 110 R6 and Ced 110 R7 have no counterpart in other surveys. The former object lies in the direction of the extended 200 m emission ridge, but the location is probably purely coincidental.

gmw_00013081.name: Ced 110 R7
gmw_00013081.overview: Ced 110 R7 is a radio source that is visible only at the 6 cm wavelength [arXiv:astro-ph/0302415]. It is slightly extended with an elliptical shape and is classified as a likely background radio galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0302415].

sources.summary: The paper groups Ced 110 R7 with likely background radio galaxies, citing 6 cm-only detection, a negative spectral-index upper limit, slight elliptical extension, and no counterpart in other surveys.

quotes.quote: Ced 110 R2, Ced 110 R6 and Ced 110 R7 : These sources are visible at 6 cm only and for R2 and R7 the upper limits of the spectral indices are negative, suggesting optically thin synchrotron emission.
Most likely these sources background radio galaxies. Ced 110 R2 and Ced 110 R7 are slightly extended with elliptical shapes.
The radio sources Ced 110 R6 and Ced 110 R7 have no counterpart in other surveys.

gmw_00013082.name: Cyg A
gmw_00013082.overview: Cyg A is a radio galaxy that serves as a microwave calibrator [arXiv:1001.4731]. Multi-frequency observations show that the galaxy does not exhibit yearly flux variation, with its core variability constrained to less than ten percent [arXiv:1001.4731].

sources.summary: The paper treats Cyg A as a microwave calibrator, finding WMAP fluxes broadly consistent with previous measurements, excluding one discrepant 33 GHz point, and seeing no significant yearly flux variation.

quotes.quote: Cyg A - The fluxes from janssen/etal:1974 at 22.29 GHz, wright/birkinshaw:1984 at 89 GHz, and wright/sault:1993 at 94 GHz are consistent with the \ results. The flux from hafez/etal:2008 at 33 GHz is lower than \ Ka-band flux by 2.7 , taking both flux uncertainties into account. We found that excluding the hafez/etal:2008 flux from the power-law fit to the combined data improved ^2_ from 2.89 to 0.79, so it was excluded for the fit plotted in Figure  fig:cygaspec and the fit parameters given in Table  tab:specfits .

Significant yearly flux variation is not seen for Cyg A and 3C58; the rms year-to-year variation is consistent with the uncertainties in each band. The lowest rms variation is in K band, and is 0.27 for Cyg A and 0.33 for 3C58. carilli/barthel:1996 give an upper limit of 10 on Cyg A core variability from observations at 5, 15, and 90 GHz over timescales from 10 months to 15 years.

gmw_00013083.name: galaxy 0
gmw_00013083.overview: Galaxy 0 is a stellar system characterized by high-quality observational data and a high signal-to-noise ratio [arXiv:2404.16401]. Its structural properties have been analyzed using its rotation curve and spatially resolved spaxel data [arXiv:2404.16401].

sources.summary: The paper uses galaxy 0 as a high-S/N MaNGA comparison: it has Good-quality data, high S/N, and a rotation curve used to compare methods.

quotes.quote: In the Marvin database, galaxy 0 is classified as ``Good'' quality data according to MaNGA criteria and exhibits a high S/N , with S/N values of spaxels defined following .
Shown in Figure fig:fig_SN , the S/N for this galaxy has a maximum of 47.5, and a median of 27 .
As a crosscheck, Figure fig:fig5 presents the rotation curve for the high S/N galaxy 0 , calculated under the same conditions as Figure fig:fig4 .

gmw_00013084.name: galaxy 1
gmw_00013084.overview: Galaxy 1 is a low signal-to-noise target observed as part of the MaNGA survey [arXiv:2404.16401]. It has a median signal-to-noise ratio of 5.55 and exhibits highly consistent gas-velocity estimates across different measurement methods [arXiv:2404.16401].

sources.summary: The paper identifies galaxy 1 as the highest-S/N low-S/N MaNGA target; its gas-velocity estimates perform well and show remarkable agreement across methods.

quotes.quote: Recall that the median S/N ranges from 5.55 for galaxy 1 to 1.64 for 10 .
For instance, galaxies 2 and 3 exhibit good results for stellar velocity in Figure fig:fig4 , while galaxies 1, 4, and 5 show better performance for gas velocity in Figure fig:fig4_2 .
Notably, galaxies 1 (median S/N = 5.55) and 4 (median S/N = 3.53) show remarkable agreement across all estimations.

gmw_00013085.name: galaxy 2
gmw_00013085.overview: Galaxy 2 is an astronomical object utilized in studies of galaxy structure [arXiv:2404.16401]. It has been analyzed as a low signal-to-noise ratio example to test rotation-curve recovery methods, including cross-correlation and anchor-spaxel placement [arXiv:2404.16401].
