sources.summary: The paper uses galaxy 2 as a detailed low-S/N example for cross-correlation, anchor-spaxel placement, and smoothing-scale tests of rotation-curve recovery.

quotes.quote: Figure fig:fig_correlation illustrates the cross-correlation coefficient for galaxy 2 , showing only the bins that passed the consistency criteria of Eq.
For instance, if the center spaxel for galaxy 2 is 37, then 33, 35, 37, 39, and 41 could be the anchor spaxels, resulting in a total of five spaxels being used.
Figure fig:fig_app1 illustrates the comparison of galaxy rotation curves for galaxy 2 using three different values: = 1.5, 6.0, and 10.0 .

gmw_00013086.name: galaxy 3
gmw_00013086.overview: Galaxy 3 is an astronomical object that exhibits good stellar-velocity results and reasonable agreement with pPXF estimations [arXiv:2404.16401]. In contrast, its gas-velocity estimates are characterized by numerous fluctuations [arXiv:2404.16401].

sources.summary: The paper reports that galaxy 3 gives good stellar-velocity results and reasonable pPXF agreement, while its gas-velocity estimates show numerous fluctuations.

quotes.quote: For instance, galaxies 2 and 3 exhibit good results for stellar velocity in Figure fig:fig4 , while galaxies 1, 4, and 5 show better performance for gas velocity in Figure fig:fig4_2 .
Galaxies 2 and 3, which gave satisfactory results in stellar velocity, have numerous fluctuations in gas velocity estimations.
For relatively higher S/N galaxies, pPXF could estimate reasonable results, such as in galaxies 1, 2, 3, and 4.

gmw_00013087.name: galaxy 4
gmw_00013087.overview: Galaxy 4 is a low signal-to-noise ratio target observed as part of the MaNGA survey [arXiv:2404.16401]. It exhibits strong gas-velocity performance, showing close agreement between different velocity estimation methods [arXiv:2404.16401].

sources.summary: The paper describes galaxy 4 as a low-S/N MaNGA target with strong gas-velocity performance and remarkable agreement between the authors’ estimates and pPXF.

quotes.quote: For instance, galaxies 2 and 3 exhibit good results for stellar velocity in Figure fig:fig4 , while galaxies 1, 4, and 5 show better performance for gas velocity in Figure fig:fig4_2 .
Galaxies 1 and 4 also demonstrate good matches with our estimations.
Notably, galaxies 1 (median S/N = 5.55) and 4 (median S/N = 3.53) show remarkable agreement across all estimations.

gmw_00013088.name: galaxy 5
gmw_00013088.overview: Galaxy 5 is a low signal-to-noise ratio target with a median signal-to-noise ratio of 3.00 [arXiv:2404.16401]. It exhibits better performance for gas velocity measurements than for stellar velocity measurements, with accurate estimations primarily confined to its central spaxels [arXiv:2404.16401].

sources.summary: The paper presents galaxy 5 as a low-S/N target with better gas-velocity performance, while pPXF becomes unreliable below its S/N and deteriorates toward the edges.

quotes.quote: For instance, galaxies 2 and 3 exhibit good results for stellar velocity in Figure fig:fig4 , while galaxies 1, 4, and 5 show better performance for gas velocity in Figure fig:fig4_2 .

Conversely, for galaxies with lower S/N values, such as those below galaxy 5 (median S/N = 3.00), pPXF tends to perform poorly, yielding unrealistic larger uncertainties when it goes to the outskirts.
For galaxy 5 onward, such as galaxies 5, 7, and 8, accurate estimations are primarily confined to the central spaxels, with pPXF results deteriorating notably towards the edges.

gmw_00013089.name: CTQ247A
gmw_00013089.overview: CTQ247A is a high-redshift damped Lyman-alpha system located at an absorption redshift of 2.5505 [arXiv:astro-ph/0303441]. It belongs to a triplet of absorbers that also includes CTQ247B and CTQ247C [arXiv:astro-ph/0303441].

sources.summary: The paper identifies CTQ247C as the lowest-H i member of the CTQ247 DLA triplet, with two low-ion sub-clumps, fewer detected species, O i fits, and Al ii measurement.

quotes.quote: CTQ247C ( z_ abs =2.6215 ): This is the lowest H i DLA of the triplet. For this DLA the bulk of the low ions are divided into 2 `sub-clumps' of 3 and 2 velocity components separated by 120 . Fig. fig_C shows the nonsaturated transitions used and their fits. Since this is the DLA with the lowest H i of the 3, fewer species are detected and limits are not very stringent. For example, for sulphur our 3 detection limit is log N ( S ii ) <14.3 .

We have attempted a fit for the N i triplet, but a combination of many weak components and Ly blending makes this a very uncertain result and we regard it as an upper limit due to possible contamination. On the other hand, the low H i allows us to profile fit 2 O i transitions, for which each one of the 5 components shows at least one nonsaturated line. Also, we report one of the few existing measurements of Al ii in DLAs (line positions were held fixed at the Si ii values), and one AODM measurement for Al iii .

sources.summary: The paper treats CTQ247A as the z_abs=2.5505 DLA in the CTQ247 system, with four-component low-ion metal absorption and fitted column densities for several detected species.

quotes.quote: CTQ247A ( z_ abs =2.5505 ): For this DLA we have identified nonsaturated transitions of N i , Si ii , S ii , Cr ii , Fe ii , and Zn ii , all of which show 4 velocity components. More (weaker) components are revealed by stronger transitions, e.g. Fe ii 2344 , but these do not contribute significantly to the total column densities. Therefore, in order to avoid introducing too many parameters at intrinsically weak transitions in relatively noisy parts of the spectrum, we proceeded by fitting 4 components. Fig. fig_A shows the nonsaturated transitions used and their fits.

From an analysis of the weaker components we estimate that the obtained column densities encompass 90 of the total column density; exclusion of weaker components will lead to only a 0.05 dex difference at N=15 . The S ii 1260 triplet lines fall on the red wings of the damped \ lines; consequently, they were fitted simultaneously with the H i fixed at the values found for the FORS data, hence the sub-unity values of the continuum in Fig. fig_A . In addition, we determine an AODM measurement for Mn ii and an upper limit for P ii .

sources.summary: The paper describes CTQ247B as the z_abs=2.5950 DLA, emphasizing its simple five-component metal-line structure, saturated O i, and unusually strong N i absorption.

quotes.quote: CTQ247B ( z_ abs =2.5950 ): This DLA has a relatively simple velocity structure. Although the strongest Si ii and Fe ii transitions have 5 velocity components, these span only 80 . As expected for such a high H i , O i is saturated and we consider it no further here. Conversely, N i which is often barely detected exhibits a large equivalent width in this system. In fact, two of the 1134 triplet transitions are saturated.

gmw_00013090.name: SDSS J2215-0045
gmw_00013090.overview: SDSS J2215-0045 is a broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:1101.4928]. The object is probably intrinsically unpolarized, exhibiting a low continuum polarization measurement of 0.30 percent and no convincing position-angle rotation through its spectral features [arXiv:1101.4928].

sources.summary: The paper notes that SDSS J2215-0045 has low continuum polarization, no convincing spectral polarization rise or position-angle rotation, and is probably intrinsically unpolarized.

quotes.quote: SDSS J2215-0045 This source has a continuum polarization measurement of 0.30 , and a maximum ISP of 0.95 , indicating that it is not likely intrinsically polarized. Nowhere in the spectrum does the polarization rise above 1 , and the position angle does not convincingly show any rotation through any spectral features. This object is at best very weakly polarized, but is probably intrinsically unpolarized.

gmw_00013091.name: QSO 2046-3433
gmw_00013091.overview: QSO 2046-3433 is a broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:1101.4928]. It exhibits unusually high polarization in its emission lines compared to the continuum, which likely indicates a geometry where the scattering region is blocked from the continuum source [arXiv:1101.4928].

sources.summary: The paper highlights QSO 2046-3433 for unusually high emission-line polarization, interpreting it as a blocked continuum-scattering geometry unlikely for face-on BALQSOs.

quotes.quote: However, we do note that there is one object, 2046-3433, that has unusually high polarization in the C 4 emission line ( p_e/p_c =2.56 ), and this is skewing the average for that line to higher values. Not including this source gives an average value of 0.92.

There is one object however, QSO 2046-3433 that has high polarization in emission lines compared to the continuum. This is most likely interpreted as a geometry in which the scattering region is blocked (possibly by lumps on the inner edge of a dusty torus) from the continuum source, while the broad-line region has a more clean line of sight to the scattering region. While this does not necessarily lend support to any one BALQSO model, it seems unlikely that this would be seen in face-on BALQSOs.

gmw_00013092.name: BAL QSO No. 7
gmw_00013092.overview: BAL QSO No. 7 is a broad-absorption line quasar characterized by quite red colors [arXiv:astro-ph/0207486]. The emission lines in its spectrum are almost completely masked by extremely broad absorption lines [arXiv:astro-ph/0207486].

sources.summary: The paper identifies BAL QSO No. 7 as an unusual quasar whose emission lines are mostly masked by broad absorption, with estimated emission and absorption redshifts and red colors.

quotes.quote: There is only one object with an unusual spectrum: the BAL QSO No. 7 where the emission lines are almost completely masked by extremely broad absortion lines. The best guess for the emission redshift is z_ em 1.7 , compared to z_ abs 1.5 for the strongest absorption lines ( C iv 1549 , Al iii 1860 , and the Fe ii multiplet at 2500 ). This object is a high-priority VPM QSO candidate with quite red colours (see below). There is no entry in the NED at this position. Objects like No. 7 are not very likely to be recognized by most other optical QSO surveys.

gmw_00013093.name: 3FGL 0032.5+3912
gmw_00013093.overview: 3FGL 0032.5+3912 is an elliptical galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.152 [arXiv:1705.09832]. Its optical spectrum suggests that the galaxy may host BL Lacertae activity [arXiv:1705.09832].

sources.summary: The paper associates 3FGL 0032.5+3912 with an optical source whose spectrum is characteristic of an elliptical galaxy at z = 0.152 and may host BL Lac activity.

quotes.quote: 3FGL 0032.5+3912 This object has been associated with an optical source that, once observed with the 1.82m telescope, showed the characteristic spectrum of an elliptical galaxy. The identification of absorption lines like the Ca II 3933,3969 doublet (detected at 4530 \ and 4572 ), together with Mg I 5175 and Na I 5893 (detected at 5961 \ and 6789 ) places the redshift of this object at z = 0.152 . Its optical spectrum is consistent with an elliptical galaxy that may host BLL activity, in agreement with the BLL classification suggested by the machine learning technique.

gmw_00013094.name: 3FGL 2358.5+3827
gmw_00013094.overview: 3FGL 2358.5+3827 is an active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of z = 0.201 [arXiv:1705.09832]. Its strong narrow emission lines and obscured central activity suggest it can be classified as a narrow-line radio galaxy [arXiv:1705.09832].

sources.summary: The paper reports emission-line spectroscopy of 3FGL 2358.5+3827, deriving z = 0.201, identifying obscured AGN activity, and suggesting classification as a narrow-line radio galaxy.

quotes.quote: 3FGL 2358.5+3827 When observed with the 1.22m telescope, this source shows a clear system of emission lines that can be identified as the close [ O II ] 3727,3929 doublet (detected as an unresolved feature at 4477 ) and the strong [ O III ] 4959,5007 doublet (falling at 5956 \ and 6013 ), which place this source at redshift z = 0.201 . The detection of strong narrow lines, together with an underlying continuum that becomes weaker at short wavelength, are suggestive of an obscured AGN activity. The source has a 1.4GHz flux measured by the NVSS of F_ 1.4 GHz = 57.4 1.8 mJy that, adopting a standard Cold Dark Matter Cosmology with H_0 = 70 km s^ 1 Mpc^ 1 , _ = 0.7 and _M = 0.3 , corresponds to a distance of 920.3 Mpc and to an intrinsic luminosity L_ = (5.49 0.17) 10^ 40 erg cm^ 2 s^ 1 . This suggests that this object could be classified as a Narrow Line Radio Galaxy (NLRG), in spite of the predicted classification as a BLL, although the detection of such type of objects in rays, at the inferred redshift, is extremely rare.

gmw_00013095.name: HDFS 10151
gmw_00013095.overview: HDFS 10151 is a point-like quasar candidate located at a redshift of approximately z=2.0 [arXiv:astro-ph/0507530]. Spectral energy distribution fitting shows its colors are more consistent with a quasar than a star, and it exhibits no measured proper motion within the errors [arXiv:astro-ph/0507530].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HDFS 10151 as a point-like z=2.0 quasar candidate whose SED colors fit quasar templates better than stars, with no significant measured proper motion.

quotes.quote: Figure 8 shows that there are 4 point-like sources with SEDs better explained by quasars than stars. HDFS 10151 ( z=2.0 ), 1945 ( z=0.2 ), 2007 ( z=4.7 ), and 2178 ( z=4.0 ) have colors more consistent with being quasars.

These four objects have proper motions in the range 2.56 -- 4.91 8 mas yr ^ 1 (see Table 2), and are not moving (within the errors); they demonstrate that our proper motion measurements are reliable. We identified 4 quasars and 42 stars, including 36 K0 or later type stars, with our SED fitting technique.

gmw_00013096.name: HDFS 1945
gmw_00013096.overview: HDFS 1945 is a point-like quasar candidate [arXiv:astro-ph/0507530]. Spectral energy distribution fitting indicates that the object is located at a redshift of approximately z=0.2 [arXiv:astro-ph/0507530].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HDFS 1945 as a point-like z=0.2 quasar candidate rather than a halo white dwarf, based on SED colors and non-significant proper motion.

quotes.quote: Figure 8 shows that there are 4 point-like sources with SEDs better explained by quasars than stars. HDFS 10151 ( z=2.0 ), 1945 ( z=0.2 ), 2007 ( z=4.7 ), and 2178 ( z=4.0 ) have colors more consistent with being quasars.

These four objects have proper motions in the range 2.56 -- 4.91 8 mas yr ^ 1 (see Table 2), and are not moving (within the errors); they demonstrate that our proper motion measurements are reliable. We identified 4 quasars and 42 stars, including 36 K0 or later type stars, with our SED fitting technique.

Six of the faint blue objects (HDFS 1945, 2007, 2178, 441, 1020, 261) have proper motions in the range 2.56 to 10.27 mas yr ^ 1 . Our SED fitting analysis showed that HDFS 1945, 2007, and 2178 have colors more consistent with being quasars than stars.

gmw_00013097.name: HDFS 2007
gmw_00013097.overview: HDFS 2007 is a point-like active galactic nucleus candidate [arXiv:astro-ph/0507530]. Spectral energy distribution fitting indicates that the object is a quasar at a redshift of approximately z=4.7 [arXiv:astro-ph/0507530].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HDFS 2007 as a point-like z=4.7 quasar candidate rather than a halo white dwarf, based on SED colors and non-significant proper motion.

quotes.quote: Figure 8 shows that there are 4 point-like sources with SEDs better explained by quasars than stars. HDFS 10151 ( z=2.0 ), 1945 ( z=0.2 ), 2007 ( z=4.7 ), and 2178 ( z=4.0 ) have colors more consistent with being quasars.

These four objects have proper motions in the range 2.56 -- 4.91 8 mas yr ^ 1 (see Table 2), and are not moving (within the errors); they demonstrate that our proper motion measurements are reliable. We identified 4 quasars and 42 stars, including 36 K0 or later type stars, with our SED fitting technique.

Six of the faint blue objects (HDFS 1945, 2007, 2178, 441, 1020, 261) have proper motions in the range 2.56 to 10.27 mas yr ^ 1 . Our SED fitting analysis showed that HDFS 1945, 2007, and 2178 have colors more consistent with being quasars than stars.

gmw_00013098.name: HDFS 2178
gmw_00013098.overview: HDFS 2178 is a point-like quasar candidate located at a redshift of approximately z=4.0 [arXiv:astro-ph/0507530]. Spectral energy distribution fitting indicates that its colors are consistent with a quasar rather than a star, and it exhibits no proper motion within the errors [arXiv:astro-ph/0507530].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HDFS 2178 as a point-like z=4.0 quasar candidate rather than a halo white dwarf, based on SED colors and non-significant proper motion.

quotes.quote: Figure 8 shows that there are 4 point-like sources with SEDs better explained by quasars than stars. HDFS 10151 ( z=2.0 ), 1945 ( z=0.2 ), 2007 ( z=4.7 ), and 2178 ( z=4.0 ) have colors more consistent with being quasars.

These four objects have proper motions in the range 2.56 -- 4.91 8 mas yr ^ 1 (see Table 2), and are not moving (within the errors); they demonstrate that our proper motion measurements are reliable. We identified 4 quasars and 42 stars, including 36 K0 or later type stars, with our SED fitting technique.

Six of the faint blue objects (HDFS 1945, 2007, 2178, 441, 1020, 261) have proper motions in the range 2.56 to 10.27 mas yr ^ 1 . Our SED fitting analysis showed that HDFS 1945, 2007, and 2178 have colors more consistent with being quasars than stars.

gmw_00013099.name: COS 12589
gmw_00013099.overview: COS 12589 is a compact galaxy with an effective radius below 2.6 kpc [arXiv:2102.10187]. Despite having a high star-formation-rate surface density, it exhibits a near-zero outflow centroid velocity [arXiv:2102.10187].

sources.summary: The paper highlights COS 12589 as one of four compact galaxies with high star-formation-rate surface density but near-zero outflow centroid velocity, considering collimated outflows and disk orientation as possible explanations.

quotes.quote: Some of the objects in our sample do not follow the general trends between outflow and galaxy properties we have discussed. In particular, we focus on the galaxies which have high star formation rate surface densities but low outflow velocities. Our dataset has four objects (COS 12589, COS 14214, COS 19180, and EGS 29026) which have centroid velocities consistent with zero and star formation rate surface densities above the sample median of 0.4 . One possible explanation for this may be that outflows are present, but are collimated and pointed away from our line of sight; evidence for non-spherical outflow geometries has been found in other studies at similar redshifts 1 kornei_properties_2012, martin_demographics_2012, bordoloi_dependence_2014,rubin_evidence_2014 . If the absence of detectable outflows is an orientation effect, we would expect these four galaxies to be disks observed roughly edge-on.

To assess this possibility, we turn to the catalog of structural measurements of CANDELS galaxies van_der_wel_structural_2012 , from which we find that these four objects have axis ratios between 0.43 and 0.79, indistinguishable from the rest of the sample. We also find that these four galaxies are compact, all with effective radii below the sample median of 2.6 kpc; however, their SFRs are not particularly high, with all but one below the sample median, meaning that the high SFR surface densities are due more to small sizes than high SFRs. The compact nature of these objects limits our ability to derive constraints on their geometry and orientation, particularly given the fact that the WFC3 F160W images used in the van_der_wel_structural_2012 catalog are a factor of 2 lower in spatial resolution than the ACS F606W and F814W images used by kornei_properties_2012 and bordoloi_dependence_2014 .

gmw_00013100.name: COS 14214
gmw_00013100.overview: COS 14214 is a compact galaxy with an effective radius below 2.6 kpc [arXiv:2102.10187]. Despite having a high star-formation-rate surface density driven by its small size, it exhibits an outflow centroid velocity consistent with zero [arXiv:2102.10187].

sources.summary: The paper highlights COS 14214 as one of four compact galaxies with high star-formation-rate surface density but near-zero outflow centroid velocity, considering collimated outflows and disk orientation as possible explanations.

gmw_00013101.name: COS 19180
gmw_00013101.overview: COS 19180 is a compact disk galaxy with an effective radius below 2.6 kpc [arXiv:2102.10187]. It exhibits a high star-formation-rate surface density driven primarily by its small size, yet it displays an outflow centroid velocity near zero [arXiv:2102.10187].

sources.summary: The paper highlights COS 19180 as one of four compact galaxies with high star-formation-rate surface density but near-zero outflow centroid velocity, considering collimated outflows and disk orientation as possible explanations.

gmw_00013102.name: EGS 29026
gmw_00013102.overview: EGS 29026 is a compact galaxy with an effective radius below 2.6 kpc [arXiv:2102.10187]. Despite having a high star-formation-rate surface density, it exhibits a near-zero outflow centroid velocity, which may be due to its small size or disk orientation [arXiv:2102.10187].

sources.summary: The paper highlights EGS 29026 as one of four compact galaxies with high star-formation-rate surface density but near-zero outflow centroid velocity, considering collimated outflows and disk orientation as possible explanations.

gmw_00013103.name: J033228.30-274403.6
gmw_00013103.overview: J033228.30-274403.6 is a faint, extended, and highly disturbed active galactic nucleus candidate with a photometric redshift of 3.25 [arXiv:1207.4478]. It is detected in soft X-rays and exhibits erratic spectral energy distribution jumps that indicate variability [arXiv:1207.4478].

sources.summary: The paper highlights J033228.30-274403.6 as a faint, extended, disturbed variability-selected AGN candidate with photometric redshift 3.25, erratic SED variability, soft X-ray detection, and X-ray luminosity.

quotes.quote: One spectacular variability selected object with X-ray detection is J033228.30-274403.6. The object has a photometric redshift of 3.25. The SED is fit reasonably by either a Spiral or a Seyfert 1.8 SED. However, there are erratic jumps in the SED that indicate variability.

The object is detected in the soft X-rays, but not the hard X-rays. The X-ray luminosity is 2.12 10^ 43 erg/s. The SED, as well as the image in z is shown in Fig. F:Ind_AwesomeSeyfert . The objects is relatively faint, extended and highly disturbed.

gmw_00013104.name: J033239.47-275300.5
gmw_00013104.overview: J033239.47-275300.5 is a radio-loud active galactic nucleus hosted within an elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1207.4478]. This host galaxy forms a pair with a nearby symmetric spheroidal galaxy, and the two are connected by a faint stream that suggests an ongoing merger [arXiv:1207.4478].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J033239.47-275300.5 as a radio-loud AGN in an elliptical galaxy pair, with photometric redshift 0.64, no X-ray detection, a galaxy-dominated SED, and merger-like faint stream.

quotes.quote: J033239.47-275300.5 does not have a spectroscopic redshift. The photometric redshift is 0.64. It is radio-loud with log(R) 2.2 and is not detected in the X-rays.

The AGN is located in an elliptical galaxy that is located in a galaxy pair. The second galaxy is also spheroidal but more symmetric than the galaxy with the AGN. They seem to be connected by a faint stream, suggesting that they are actually undergoing a merger. The projected distance between the two galaxies is about 1.2 arcsec, which corresponds to approximately 8kpc at the redshift of the object. The SED is galaxy dominated.

gmw_00013105.name: J033241.87-274651.1
gmw_00013105.overview: J033241.87-274651.1 is a tadpole-type galaxy [arXiv:1207.4478]. It exhibits a slightly reddened spiral galaxy spectral energy distribution and is considered a likely false-positive active galactic nucleus candidate with only possible weak activity in its head [arXiv:1207.4478].

sources.summary: The paper marks J033241.87-274651.1 as a likely false-positive AGN candidate: a tadpole-type galaxy with a slightly reddened spiral-galaxy SED and only possible weak activity in the head.

quotes.quote: J033241.87-274651.1 is a 'tadpole' type galaxy . The best fit SED is a Spiral galaxy SED, but the actual SED appears slightly reddened. While weak AGN activity in the 'head' is not excluded, this object is probably a false positive.

gmw_00013106.name: J033219.86-274110.0
gmw_00013106.overview: J033219.86-274110.0 is a galaxy-dominated source with a best-fit Sd spiral classification [arXiv:1207.4478]. Rest-frame ultraviolet imaging shows no clear central point source, and the system appears to be undergoing a merger [arXiv:1207.4478].

sources.summary: The paper describes J033219.86-274110.0 as a galaxy-dominated best-fit Sd source with no clear UV central point source, apparent merger morphology, and likely false-positive AGN status.

quotes.quote: J033219.86-274110.0 is entirely galaxy dominated, with a best-fit 'Sd' SED. The rest-frame UV imaging data shows no clear central point source. The object appears to be undergoing a merger. It is very likely a false positive.

gmw_00013107.name: J033210.52-274628.9
gmw_00013107.overview: J033210.52-274628.9 is a compact but resolved lenticular galaxy [arXiv:1207.4478]. Although it was considered a candidate active galactic nucleus, its spectral energy distribution shows no clear evidence of AGN activity and is instead somewhat reddened compared to a normal S0 galaxy [arXiv:1207.4478].

sources.summary: The paper treats J033210.52-274628.9 as a likely false-positive AGN candidate, somewhat reddened relative to a normal S0 SED, compact but resolved, and without clear SED evidence for AGN activity.

quotes.quote: J033210.52-274628.9 appears to be somewhat reddened with respect to a normal S0 SED, there is no clear evidence of AGN activity in the SED. The object is very compact but resolved. It is likely a false positive.

gmw_00013108.name: J033209.57-274634.9
gmw_00013108.overview: J033209.57-274634.9 is a galaxy with a spectral energy distribution that is reasonably well fit by an Sdm spiral galaxy template [arXiv:1207.4478]. It exhibits a red excess that can be modeled with a quasar component, though it lacks a ultraviolet excess and is considered a possible false-positive active galactic nucleus candidate [arXiv:1207.4478].

sources.summary: The paper labels J033209.57-274634.9 a possible false-positive AGN candidate, with an Sdm SED, red excess fit by a QSO component, no UV excess, and possible obscuration or UV-weak activity.

quotes.quote: J033209.57-274634.9 is reasonably well fit by a Sdm SED. There is a red excess that can be fit by including a QSO component, however, there is no UV excess. This might be explained by obscuration depressing the UV excess from star formation or possibly by invoking an UV-weak AGN. This source is possibly a false positive.

gmw_00013109.name: J033225.10-274403.2
gmw_00013109.overview: J033225.10-274403.2 is a galaxy at a redshift of 0.076 with a clear disk-plus-bulge morphology [arXiv:1207.4478]. It is classified as an S0 lenticular galaxy that exhibits an extremely weak ultraviolet excess [arXiv:1207.4478].

sources.summary: The paper describes J033225.10-274403.2 as a likely false-positive AGN candidate at redshift 0.076, with S0 disk-plus-bulge morphology, clear extension, and extremely weak UV excess.

quotes.quote: J033225.10-274403.2 is well fit by an S0 SED, it shows a clear disk+bulge morphology. It is at a redshift of 0.076 and very clearly extended. The UV excess in this source is extremely weak. This is likely a false positive.

gmw_00013110.name: RID 101
gmw_00013110.overview: RID 101 is a double-lobed classical radio galaxy [arXiv:1209.4176]. It is likely a high-redshift object, as it is not detected in the K-band but has a possible counterpart at 24 microns [arXiv:1209.4176].

sources.summary: The paper treats RID 101 as an unidentified double-lobed source, rejects its faint COMBO17 core association, and argues radio contours and K-band non-detection make it a likely high-redshift classical radio galaxy.

quotes.quote: RID 101 (KID 23): double lobed source whose core was tentatively identified in M08 (KID 23) with a faint (R-band magnitude 26) galaxy at z=0.999 from the COMBO17 catalog. We think that this association is very unlikely especially because this possible counterpart is not detected at any longer wavelength. From the radio contours we believe that it is a classical radio galaxy.

Therefore, we expect for such a galaxy a correlation between the K-band magnitude and the redshift e.g., lilly84 . Since we do not detect it in the K-band we think this source may be a high redshift object. This hypothesis is also supported by the presence of a possible counterpart at 24 micron in the FIDEL catalog. Unfortunately no photometric redshift is available.

gmw_00013111.name: RID 209
gmw_00013111.overview: RID 209 is a V-shaped radio source located at a redshift of 1.3 [arXiv:1209.4176]. It features two radio lobes, one of which may experience flux density contamination from a superimposed galaxy [arXiv:1209.4176].

sources.summary: The paper describes RID 209 as a z=1.3 V-shaped radio source, rejects an intracluster-medium head-tail interpretation using X-ray and redshift evidence, and notes possible lobe contamination by a superimposed galaxy.

quotes.quote: RID 209 (KID 48): V-shape radio source at redshift 1.3. Given the radio morphology, we considered the possibility that this is a head-tail radio source due to the interaction with the intracluster medium (ICM) in a high-z galaxy cluster. Any cluster, or large group, with ICM density sufficient to bend the radio jets, would have been clearly detected in the X-ray too.

However, we do not observe it in the 4 Ms Chandra image. Also the redshift distribution of the sources in the region around RID 209 does not show any hint of clustering. Therefore, we think is unlikely that the V-shape of this radio source is due to the interaction with the ICM. There is instead a possible contamination to the flux density of one of the two radio lobes (A) from a superimposed galaxy.

gmw_00013112.name: RID 407
gmw_00013112.overview: RID 407 is a bright, extended double-lobed radio galaxy [arXiv:1209.4176]. It is associated with a bright elliptical galaxy that is offset by 2 arcseconds from the centroid of the radio image [arXiv:1209.4176].

sources.summary: The paper describes RID 407 as a bright extended double-lobed radio source, rejects a nearer K-band galaxy as spurious, and manually associates it with a bright elliptical galaxy offset from the radio centroid.

quotes.quote: RID 407 (KID 113): bright and extended double lobed source. Close to the core position there are many optical sources. In particular there is a 21 K-band magnitude galaxy 0.5 arcsec away from the expected core position that was automatically selected by our method. We consider this association spurious since it would imply that this source is far from the K-z relation for radio galaxies e.g lilly84,debreuck02 . Therefore, we manually corrected the identification by associating this radio source to a bright elliptical galaxy (K-band mag=18.4) 2 arcsec away from the centroid of the radio image.

gmw_00013113.name: 1FGLJ0648.8+1516
gmw_00013113.overview: 1FGLJ0648.8+1516 is a gamma-ray source detected by Fermi [arXiv:1203.0304]. Its infrared colors strongly support its classification as a blazar, specifically a transition BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1203.0304].

sources.summary: The paper treats 1FGLJ0648.8+1516 as a ray source associated with a VERITAS TeV BL Lac candidate and uses WISE infrared colors to support a blazar/TBL classification.

quotes.quote: As an example, we applied the WBS as a new diagnostic tool based on the \ to look for the counterpart of the recently unidentified BL Lac candidate discovered by VERITAS: \ ong10 .

We applied this to the case of the TeV unidentified source \ recently discovered by VERITAS ong10 , that has been likely associated with a blazar at low galactic latitudes stephen10 . The blazar identification has been suggested on the basis of both the X-ray counterpart stephen10 and the \ detection of the ray source 1FGLJ0648.8+1516 within the positional error circle of 0.03 deg abdo10 . We considered the positional circle of radius 108 ^ = 0.03 deg, corresponding to the error on the position of the \ source and we report the infrared colors for all the \ sources over the [ 3.4 ] [ 4.6 ] [ 12 ] m color-color diagram.

We showed the use of the WBS as diagnostic tool for the case of \ recently detected at TeV energies by VERITAS ong10 , and already present in the 1st year \ catalog abdo10 . Our analysis of the infrared colors for all the \ sources in the \ positional error circle strongly support its blazar classification stephen10 . Its position on the WBS suggest that \ is a new TBL.

gmw_00013114.name: S1
gmw_00013114.overview: S1 is a narrow-angle tail radio galaxy with an optical counterpart on the Digitized Sky Survey [arXiv:1302.0218]. The galaxy features a radio tail pointing toward the southeast, and it lacks a known redshift measurement [arXiv:1302.0218].

sources.summary: The paper describes S1 as a DSS-counterpart NAT radio galaxy without a known redshift, with a southeast-pointing tail and radio power consistent with narrow-angle-tail radio galaxies.

quotes.quote: Discrete radio sources are labelled from S1 to S7. Their flux densities at 610 MHz and positions, both measured from the image in Fig. 1, are summarized in Table tab:flux . All have optical counterparts on the DSS, except for S7. No redshift measurements are currently reported for these galaxies in the literature and optical catalogs.

Four sources (S1, S4, S5 and S6) show a narrow-angle tail (NAT) morphology. The tails of S4 and S6 are both oriented northward, while the tails of S1 and S5 point toward South-East and North-West, respectively, almost opposite to each other. Assuming that the NATs are at the redshift of the cluster, the length of their tails ranges, in projection, from 90 kpc (S5) to 180 kpc (S6), and their radio powers at 610 MHz are of the order of 10^ 24-25 W Hz ^ 1 . These values are consistent with the range of size and radio power typically measured for NAT radio galaxies e.g., 2002ASSL..272..163F .

gmw_00013115.name: S4
gmw_00013115.overview: S4 is a narrow-angle tail radio galaxy that is coincident with the X-ray center of its host cluster [arXiv:1302.0218]. The galaxy lacks a reported redshift measurement and exhibits a radio tail oriented toward the north [arXiv:1302.0218].

sources.summary: The paper describes S4 as a DSS-counterpart NAT radio galaxy without a known redshift, coincident with the cluster X-ray center and showing a northward tail with typical NAT radio power.

quotes.quote: All have optical counterparts on the DSS, except for S7. No redshift measurements are currently reported for these galaxies in the literature and optical catalogs. The cluster X-ray centre (Table tab:PLCK171 ) is approximately coincident with the peak of S4. Four sources (S1, S4, S5 and S6) show a narrow-angle tail (NAT) morphology. The tails of S4 and S6 are both oriented northward, while the tails of S1 and S5 point toward South-East and North-West, respectively, almost opposite to each other.

Assuming that the NATs are at the redshift of the cluster, the length of their tails ranges, in projection, from 90 kpc (S5) to 180 kpc (S6), and their radio powers at 610 MHz are of the order of 10^ 24-25 W Hz ^ 1 . These values are consistent with the range of size and radio power typically measured for NAT radio galaxies e.g., 2002ASSL..272..163F .

gmw_00013116.name: S5
gmw_00013116.overview: S5 is a narrow-angle tail radio galaxy that has an optical counterpart on the Digitized Sky Survey [arXiv:1302.0218]. Its radio tail points toward the northwest, with a projected length of approximately 90 kpc [arXiv:1302.0218].

sources.summary: The paper describes S5 as a DSS-counterpart NAT radio galaxy lacking a redshift; its tail points northwest, its projected tail length is 90 kpc, and its radio power matches typical NAT radio galaxies.

quotes.quote: Discrete radio sources are labelled from S1 to S7. Their flux densities at 610 MHz and positions, both measured from the image in Fig. 1, are summarized in Table tab:flux . All have optical counterparts on the DSS, except for S7. No redshift measurements are currently reported for these galaxies in the literature and optical catalogs.

Four sources (S1, S4, S5 and S6) show a narrow-angle tail (NAT) morphology. The tails of S4 and S6 are both oriented northward, while the tails of S1 and S5 point toward South-East and North-West, respectively, almost opposite to each other. Assuming that the NATs are at the redshift of the cluster, the length of their tails ranges, in projection, from 90 kpc (S5) to 180 kpc (S6), and their radio powers at 610 MHz are of the order of 10^ 24-25 W Hz ^ 1 . These values are consistent with the range of size and radio power typically measured for NAT radio galaxies e.g., 2002ASSL..272..163F .

gmw_00013117.name: S6
gmw_00013117.overview: S6 is a narrow-angle tail radio galaxy with an optical counterpart on the Digitized Sky Survey [arXiv:1302.0218]. Its radio tail is oriented northward and has a projected length of 180 kpc assuming cluster membership [arXiv:1302.0218].

sources.summary: The paper identifies S6 as a DSS-counterpart NAT radio galaxy without a redshift, with a northward tail; assuming cluster membership, its tail reaches 180 kpc and its radio power is typical for NAT radio galaxies.

quotes.quote: Discrete radio sources are labelled from S1 to S7. Their flux densities at 610 MHz and positions, both measured from the image in Fig. 1, are summarized in Table tab:flux . All have optical counterparts on the DSS, except for S7. No redshift measurements are currently reported for these galaxies in the literature and optical catalogs.

Four sources (S1, S4, S5 and S6) show a narrow-angle tail (NAT) morphology. The tails of S4 and S6 are both oriented northward, while the tails of S1 and S5 point toward South-East and North-West, respectively, almost opposite to each other. Assuming that the NATs are at the redshift of the cluster, the length of their tails ranges, in projection, from 90 kpc (S5) to 180 kpc (S6), and their radio powers at 610 MHz are of the order of 10^ 24-25 W Hz ^ 1 . These values are consistent with the range of size and radio power typically measured for NAT radio galaxies e.g., 2002ASSL..272..163F .

gmw_00013118.name: SDSS J160457.50 + 220300.5
gmw_00013118.overview: SDSS J160457.50 + 220300.5 is a background quasar with a significantly reddened spectrum [arXiv:0906.1585]. Its spectral energy distribution is well fitted by a composite quasar spectrum reddened with a Large Magellanic Cloud supershell extinction law [arXiv:0906.1585].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J160457.50 + 220300.5 as the reddened background quasar whose spectrum shows a CO absorber, a 2175 dust feature, an LMC-like SED fit, and absence from the color-selected SDSS quasar sample.

quotes.quote: The background quasar spectrum is significantly reddened (u K 4.5 mag) and presents a pronounced 2175 dust absorption feature at the redshift of the CO absorber. Using a control sample of 500 quasars we find the chance probability for this feature to be spurious is 0.3 . We show that the spectral energy distribution (SED) of the quasar is well fitted with a QSO composite spectrum reddened with a Large Magellanic Cloud supershell extinction law at the redshift of the absorber. It is noticeable that this quasar is absent from the colour-selected SDSS quasar sample.

gmw_00013119.name: SMA-1
gmw_00013119.overview: SMA-1 is a high-redshift galaxy [arXiv:1709.01238] with an estimated redshift of approximately 2.39 [arXiv:1709.01238]. It is detected in the optical and near-infrared bands, and it exhibits a flux discrepancy between SCUBA-2 and SMA observations that may be caused by unresolved faint sources [arXiv:1709.01238].

sources.summary: SMA-1 shows a SCUBA-2/SMA flux discrepancy attributed to unresolved faint sources, is detected in optical/NIR, and has an estimated redshift of about 2.39.

quotes.quote: Although there is more than a factor of 2 discrepancy between the SCUBA-2 and SMA flux densities for SMA-1 and SMA-6, the difference can be caused by multiple faint sources that are below our detection limit.
Three (SMA-1, SMA-2-1, and SMA-5) of the eight SMA sources are detected in the optical or NIR.
We obtained z = 2.39 0.17 and 2.00 0.15 for SMA-1 and SMA-5, respectively.

gmw_00013120.name: SMA-2-1
gmw_00013120.overview: SMA-2-1 is a high-redshift galaxy that is detected in the optical or near-infrared bands [arXiv:1709.01238]. Based on its submillimeter-to-radio flux ratio, the galaxy has an estimated millimetric redshift of approximately 2.9 [arXiv:1709.01238].

sources.summary: SMA-2-1 is one of the optically/NIR-detected SMA sources; the authors use its submillimeter-to-radio flux ratio, including a 3 GHz flux, to estimate a millimetric redshift of 2.9.

quotes.quote: Three (SMA-1, SMA-2-1, and SMA-5) of the eight SMA sources are detected in the optical or NIR.

For SMA-2-1, SMA-2-2 and SMA-6, we used the submillimeter-to-radio flux ratios to compute their ``millimetric redshifts'', following the method in . The relation between the redshift and the submillimeter-to-radio flux ratio is z & = (S_ 343 GHz /S_ 1.4 GHz )^ 0.26 1 & = 0.85(S_ 343 GHz /S_ 3 GHz )^ 0.26 1 With S_ 3 GHz = 5.13 1.51 Jy Hsu:2017aa , we obtained z = 2.9 0.4 for SMA-2-1.
To propagate the redshift uncertainties of SMA-2-1, SMA-2-2, SMA-3-1, SMA-3-2, SMA-4, and SMA-6, we used a uniform distribution between their lower limits and an upper limit at z = 6 .

gmw_00013121.name: SMA-2-2
gmw_00013121.overview: SMA-2-2 is a high-redshift galaxy [arXiv:1709.01238]. Because it lacks a radio detection, its submillimeter-to-radio flux ratio indicates a millimetric redshift lower limit of z > 3.7, with an estimated upper limit of z = 6 [arXiv:1709.01238].

sources.summary: SMA-2-2 lacks a radio detection, so the authors use radio limits with submillimeter flux ratios to derive a millimetric redshift lower limit of z>3.7 and propagate broad redshift uncertainty.

quotes.quote: For SMA-2-1, SMA-2-2 and SMA-6, we used the submillimeter-to-radio flux ratios to compute their ``millimetric redshifts'', following the method in .

Because SMA-2-2 and SMA-6 are not detected in the radio images, we used their 3 limits, 3.05 Jy (3 GHz) and 22.6 Jy (1.4 GHz), to compute the lower redshift limits. The results are z > 3.7 and z > 2.2 for SMA-2-2 and SMA-6, respectively.
To propagate the redshift uncertainties of SMA-2-1, SMA-2-2, SMA-3-1, SMA-3-2, SMA-4, and SMA-6, we used a uniform distribution between their lower limits and an upper limit at z = 6 .

gmw_00013122.name: SMA-4
gmw_00013122.overview: SMA-4 is a submillimeter galaxy that is subject to gravitational lensing [arXiv:1709.01238]. A nearby offset elliptical galaxy is considered an unlikely counterpart for the source, and SMA-4's magnification is highly uncertain due to its proximity to lensing critical lines [arXiv:1709.01238].

sources.summary: For SMA-4, a nearby offset elliptical is judged unlikely to be the SMG counterpart; lensing critical lines make its magnification highly uncertain, so only a lower-limit magnification is used.

quotes.quote: Note that for SMA-4, there is one elliptical galaxy that has a 0 87 offset from the SMA position. Given the positional uncertainties of the SMA and HST detections, the offset is measured above a 5 level. In addition, based on the photometric catalog from CLASH, this elliptical galaxy is a cluster member at z 0.5 . Thus, it is unlikely to be the counterpart to the SMG.

To propagate the redshift uncertainties of SMA-2-1, SMA-2-2, SMA-3-1, SMA-3-2, SMA-4, and SMA-6, we used a uniform distribution between their lower limits and an upper limit at z = 6 . The resulting magnification error for SMA-4 is very large, because the critical lines at z=1-6 are close to this source. We therefore decided to only use z > 1.0 to compute the lower limit of its magnification.

gmw_00013123.name: SMA-5
gmw_00013123.overview: SMA-5 is a high-redshift submillimeter galaxy with a photometric redshift of approximately 2.00 [arXiv:1709.01238]. It features an optical and near-infrared counterpart with a small spatial offset, which is attributed to distinct regions of unobscured and dust-obscured star formation [arXiv:1709.01238].

sources.summary: SMA-5 has an optical/NIR counterpart with a small submillimeter/optical offset attributed to separate unobscured and dust-obscured star-forming regions; its photometry gives a redshift near 2.00.

quotes.quote: Three (SMA-1, SMA-2-1, and SMA-5) of the eight SMA sources are detected in the optical or NIR.

There is also a small offset between the peaks of the submillimeter and optical emission for SMA-5. However, the offset becomes smaller in the K_ s band image. We suggest that the optical/NIR source is the correct counterpart to the SMG, and the offset is due to different regions of unobscured and dust-obscured star formation, which is very typical for SMGs.

For SMA-5, F850LP is the only available HST passband. We took the IRAC 1.9'' radius aperture photometry from the Spitzer source catalog of A2390 and ran SExtractor in single-image mode to measure the auto magnitudes at F850LP and K_ s . Before running BPZ, we corrected all the magnitudes for Galactic dust extinction from . We obtained z = 2.39 0.17 and 2.00 0.15 for SMA-1 and SMA-5, respectively.

gmw_00013124.name: SMA-6
gmw_00013124.overview: SMA-6 is a high-redshift galaxy with a millimetric redshift lower limit of z > 2.2 [arXiv:1709.01238]. It exhibits nearby emission from an optically detected source that is possibly an interacting pair of galaxies [arXiv:1709.01238].

sources.summary: SMA-6 shows a SCUBA-2/SMA flux discrepancy likely caused by unresolved faint sources, with nearby emission from an optically detected possible interacting pair and a millimetric redshift lower limit of z>2.2.

quotes.quote: Although there is more than a factor of 2 discrepancy between the SCUBA-2 and SMA flux densities for SMA-1 and SMA-6, the difference can be caused by multiple faint sources that are below our detection limit. This is clear for SMA-6, where we can see some emission with S/N > 3 coming from an optically detected galaxy (possibly a pair of interacting galaxies).
For SMA-2-1, SMA-2-2 and SMA-6, we used the submillimeter-to-radio flux ratios to compute their ``millimetric redshifts'', following the method in .

Because SMA-2-2 and SMA-6 are not detected in the radio images, we used their 3 limits, 3.05 Jy (3 GHz) and 22.6 Jy (1.4 GHz), to compute the lower redshift limits. The results are z > 3.7 and z > 2.2 for SMA-2-2 and SMA-6, respectively.

gmw_00013125.name: Q 0103-260
gmw_00013125.overview: Q 0103-260 is a bright, high-redshift quasar located in the FORS Deep Field [arXiv:astro-ph/0306223]. It is the only quasar at a redshift greater than two in this field that is bright enough for high-resolution spectroscopy [arXiv:astro-ph/0306223].

sources.summary: The paper uses Q 0103-260 as a bright high-redshift quasar in the FDF, focusing on galaxy-redshift correlations with its metal-line systems and H i absorption along its sightline.

quotes.quote: As pointed out in the papers quoted above, one of the selection criteria for the FDF was the presence of a bright high-redshift QSO Q 0103-260, cf. 1991ApJS...76...23W,1999A&A...352L...1D which was included specifically for the purpose of studying such correlations. Although in the course of the survey 7 additional QSOs were detected in the FDF, Q 0103-260 is still the only z > 2 quasar which is bright enough for high-resolution spectroscopy. The main emphasis of the present investigation is on the correlations observed between the galaxy redshift distribution and the redshifts of the metal line systems of Q 0103-260. However, some information on the H i absorption towards Q 0103-260 is also presented.

gmw_00013126.name: GB6J1711+3830
gmw_00013126.overview: GB6J1711+3830 is an active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of z = 4.0 [arXiv:2106.01953]. Based on its radio properties, the source is classified as a blazar candidate [arXiv:2106.01953].

sources.summary: The paper reports X-ray analysis of GB6J1711+3830, describing it as a z = 4.0 AGN and blazar candidate selected on the basis of its radio properties.

quotes.quote: In this section we report the X-ray analysis of the source: GB6J1711+3830. This is a z =4.0 AGN (the optical spectrum is reported in C19) classified as a blazar candidate on the basis of its radio properties. This is the only object from whose X-ray analysis was not included in because it had not been observed at the time of publication; we discuss the analysis and its X-ray properties here.

gmw_00013127.name: IGR J18257 0707
gmw_00013127.overview: IGR J18257 0707 is a nearby type 1 Seyfert galaxy [arXiv:0810.2512]. It is located at a redshift of z=0.037 and has a faint optical counterpart that exhibits a broad hydrogen emission line [arXiv:0810.2512].

sources.summary: The paper identifies IGR J18257 0707 with a faint optical counterpart and broad hydrogen emission, concluding it is a nearby type 1 Seyfert galaxy at redshift z=0.037.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: With these data the position of the source was determined with the accuracy 0.64 \ (90 confidence): , = 18:25:57.58, 07:10:22.8 (J2000).

quotes.quote: We present the results of the optical identification of hard X-ray source IGR J18257 0707 trough the spectroscopic observations of its optical counterpart with RTT150 telescope. Accurate position of the X-ray source, determined using Chandra observations, allowed us to associate this source with the faint optical object ( m_R 20.4 ), which shows broad H_ emission line in its optical spectrum. Therefore we conclude that the source IGR J18257 0707 is a type 1 Seyfert galaxy at redshift z=0.037 .

We conclude that this line should be identified with H line at redshift z=0.037 . In that case the object is identified as nearby type 1 Seyfert galaxy, which constitute the main part of extragalactic objects among the optical identifications of new INTEGRAL hard X-ray sources see, e.g. bikmaev2006a,burenin08 .

gmw_00013128.name: SDSS J235702.54-004824.0
gmw_00013128.overview: SDSS J235702.54-004824.0 is a broad-absorption line quasar located at a redshift of z=2.998 [arXiv:2209.06924]. The active galactic nucleus hosts a broad absorption line outflow with four subcomponents, as well as a high-velocity carbon IV mini-broad absorption line that exhibits time-variable absorption features [arXiv:2209.06924].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes VLT/UVES and SDSS spectra of the quasar SDSS J235702.54-004824.0, identifying BAL subcomponents, measuring S1 column densities and kinetic luminosity, and finding time variability in a high-velocity C IV mini-BAL.

quotes.quote: We found a broad absorption line (BAL) outflow in the VLT/UVES spectrum of the quasar SDSS J235702.54-004824.0, in which we identified four subcomponents. We measured the column densities of the ions in one of the subcomponents ( v=-1600 km s ^ 1 ) , which include O I and Fe II . We found the kinetic luminosity of this component to be at most 2.4 of the quasar's Eddington luminosity. This is near the amount required to contribute to AGN feedback. We also examined the time-variability of a C IV mini-BAL found at v=-8700 km s ^ 1 , which shows a shallower and narrower absorption feature attached to it in previous SDSS observations from 2000 and 2001, but not in the spectra from 2005 and onwards.

The quasar J2357-0048 (J2000: RA=23:57:02.54, DEC=-00:48:24.0; z=2.998 ) was observed with the VLT/UVES on September 5, 2005 as part of the program 075.B-0190(A), with resolution R 40,000 and wavelength coverage from 3621 to 10429 \ Murphy2019 .

gmw_00013129.name: Q0913+072; DLA at z_ abs = 2.61843
gmw_00013129.overview: The damped Lyman-alpha system toward Q0913+072 is a high-redshift, metal-poor absorber located at a redshift of z = 2.61843 [arXiv:0712.1829]. Its weak metal lines split into two closely spaced components with unsaturated carbon and oxygen transitions [arXiv:0712.1829].

sources.summary: The paper treats the DLA toward Q0913+072 as an extremely metal-poor absorber, reobserved with UVES; its weak metal lines split into two close components with unsaturated C ii and O i transitions.

quotes.quote: The metal-poor DLA in front of this bright QSO has been known since the survey by Pettini et al. (1997). It was subsequently studied at higher spectral resolution by Ledoux et al. (1998) and Erni et al. (2006) who considered it to be `the most metal-deficient DLA known' and drew attention to its role as a probe of early nucleosynthesis.

A selection of the absorption lines in this DLA is shown in Figure fig:Q0913_montage . The metal lines consist of two closely spaced components; profile decomposition with VPFIT returns b values of 3.7 and 5.4 km s ^ 1 and a velocity separation of 12.4 km s ^ 1 (see Table tab:Q0913_cloudmodel ). Table tab:Q0913_coldens lists the absorption lines analysed and the corresponding values of column density; the errors on N (X) are the formal values returned by VPFIT. Most of the metal lines in this DLA are weak; in particular, from Figure fig:Q0913_montage it can be seen that both C ii 1334 and C ii 1036 are unsaturated (these lines are nearly always saturated in DLAs), as is O i 1039 .

gmw_00013130.name: SDSS J1016+4040; DLA at z_ abs = 2.81633
aliases_local: SDSS J1016+4040; DLA at z_ abs = 2.81633
gmw_00013130.overview: SDSS J1016+4040 is a high-redshift galaxy system containing a sub-damped Lyman-alpha absorber at a redshift of 2.81633 [arXiv:0712.1829]. High-resolution spectroscopy reveals that this absorber features weak metal lines and two narrow first-ion velocity components [arXiv:0712.1829].

sources.original_name: SDSS J1016+4040; DLA at z_ abs = 2.81633
sources.summary: The paper reports HIRES spectroscopy of the absorber toward SDSS J1016+4040, finding a central sub-DLA component with damping wings, weak metal lines, and two narrow first-ion velocity components.

quotes.quote: As can be seen from Figure fig:J1016_montage , what appears as a likely damped \ line in the low resolution SDSS spectrum breaks up into three distinct components at the higher resolution of the HIRES data. However, the central one of these three absorption lines does exhibit damping wings; we deduce N (H i ) = (8 2) 10^ 19 cm ^ 2 . Such systems are sometimes referred to as sub-DLAs since their column densities are lower than the historical definition of a DLA as a system with N (H i ) > 2 10^ 20 cm ^ 2 (Wolfe et al. 1986). All the metal lines in this DLA are weak (see Figure fig:J1016_montage ). The first ions exhibit two narrow velocity components with b = 5.0 and 6.9 km s ^ 1 separated by 23 km s ^ 1 , with the latter accounting for 90 of the total column density (see Table tab:J1016_cloudmodel ).

gmw_00013131.name: SDSS J1558+4053; DLA at z_ abs = 2.55332
aliases_local: SDSS J1558+4053; DLA at z_ abs = 2.55332
gmw_00013131.overview: SDSS J1558+4053 is a high-redshift galaxy system [arXiv:0712.1829] that hosts a damped Lyman-alpha system at a redshift of z = 2.55332 [arXiv:0712.1829]. Its neutral gas is concentrated in a single, narrow component, accompanied by weaker ionized gas components [arXiv:0712.1829].

sources.original_name: SDSS J1558+4053; DLA at z_ abs = 2.55332
sources.summary: The paper confirms SDSS J1558+4053 hosts a DLA at z_abs=2.55332, with neutral gas in one narrow component and stronger absorption lines than the other systems studied.

quotes.quote: The echelle data confirm that the strong absorption feature near 4320 \ in the discovery SDSS spectrum is indeed a damped \ line with N (H i ) = 2.0 10^ 20 cm ^ 2 (see Figure fig:J1558_montage ). The neutral gas is concentrated in a single, narrow ( b = 6.1 km s ^ 1 ) component at z_ abs = 2.55332 ; other, weaker, components are seen at positive and negative velocities in C ii and Si ii and probably originate in ionised gas (Figure fig:J1558_montage and Table tab:J1558_cloudmodel ). The entries in Table tab:J1558_coldens are for the neutral component only. The absorption lines in this DLA are stronger than in the previous two cases. Nevertheless, we cover at least one unsaturated transition for every species considered except possibly for C ii : both 1334 and 1036 are just saturated.

gmw_00013132.name: Q2206 199; DLA at z_ abs = 2.07624
gmw_00013132.overview: The damped Lyman-alpha system Q2206 199 is a high-redshift galaxy located at an absorption redshift of 2.07624 [arXiv:0712.1829]. This metal-poor system appears to consist of a single, narrow component with well-determined ion column densities [arXiv:0712.1829].

sources.summary: The paper combines archival UVES and HIRES spectra of the metal-poor DLA toward Q2206 199, finding a single narrow component with well-determined ion column densities.

quotes.quote: The metal-poor DLA in this QSO has been known since the early echelle observations by Pettini et al. (1990) and by Prochaska Wolfe (1997). Here we combine spectra from the UVES and HIRES data archives to achieve S/N 100 at 5000 \ and extensive spectral coverage from 3100 \ to 1 m. Even at this high resolution and signal-to-noise ratio this DLA appears to consist of a single, narrow component: the profiles of all the absorption lines from species which are dominant in H i regions are well reproduced by a model `cloud' with b = 6.5 km s ^ 1 (Figure fig:Q2206_montage and Tables tab:Q2206_cloudmodel and tab:Q2206_coldens ). The availability of a number of transitions with differing f values for most ions ensures that the column densities are well determined. The C ii lines are just saturated but, for the reasons outlined above, this is unlikely to affect our conclusions concerning the relative abundances of C and O.

gmw_00013133.name: N08
gmw_00013133.overview: N08 is a transient gamma-ray source that experienced a flare in 2017 [arXiv:2209.12461]. Located east of the pulsar PSR J0205+6449, this source is associated with the blazar TXS 0205+643, also known as NVSS J020935+643725 [arXiv:2209.12461].

sources.summary: The paper describes N08 as a 2017 transient gamma-ray source offset east of PSR J0205+6449 and connects the corresponding 4FGL source to the blazar TXS 0205+643/NVSS J020935+643725.

quotes.quote: The other transient source at a significant distance from the associated pixel, N08, is near PSR J0205+6449 that is a 65-millisecond young rotation-powered pulsar. At the position of this pulsar the local significance is 7.8 , while at the associated pixel the local significance is 13.2 . Figure Fig2 shows the significance map obtained from the VSSTW analysis with the positions of PSR J0205+6449 and a radio source, TXS 0205+643, and illustrates this fact.

This difference in significances is suggestive that another ray source can be responsible for this transient signal. The Fermi LAT count map shown in Figure 4 of PMV2021 also indicates a similar offset from the position of PSR J0205+6449 to the east. The flare of the source N08 was in 2017 and the flux was 3.7 times higher PMV2021 than that of the nearest source, PSR J0205+6449, in the Fermi LAT 10-year source catalogue (4FGL-DR2).
In the 4FGL-DR3 catalogue, a new source, namely 4FGL J0209.7+6437, to the east of PSR J0205+6449 is added and identified with a blazar of uncertain type, TXS 0205+643 or NVSS J020935+643725.

gmw_00013134.name: host galaxy of the burst
gmw_00013134.overview: The host galaxy of the burst was detected in the B and Rc bands during deep optical observations [arXiv:astro-ph/0612545]. Its measured optical colors are consistent with a starburst galaxy template [arXiv:astro-ph/0612545].

sources.summary: The paper detects the host galaxy of the burst in B and Rc, measures its optical colours, and finds that the colours are consistent with a starburst template with E(B-V) = 0.16.

quotes.quote: Finally, 8 months after the burst, deep observations were made from the 3.5m telescope at Calar Alto. In these images we detect the host galaxy of the burst in B and Rc bands and impose a limit in Ic band.

We derive galaxy colour indices of (B-Rc) = 0.7 0.5 and (Rc-Ic) 0.73 . These values are corrected for Galactic extinction. We have compared these values to the ones derived from the templates computed by Kinney et al. ( ) for a wide variety of galaxy types. We may conclude that only starburst galaxy templates are consistent with them. The best correlation is obtained with the starbust 2 template, with an intrinsic extinction of E(B-V) = 0.16.

gmw_00013135.name: SNAD154
gmw_00013135.overview: SNAD154 is a galaxy that hosts a candidate active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2111.11555]. The host galaxy is identified in the Sloan Digital Sky Survey with a known photometric redshift, and its transient light curves are poorly fitted by supernova templates [arXiv:2111.11555].

sources.summary: The paper classifies SNAD154 as a missed AGN candidate, notes its 18.5^m-19.5^m peak and coincident SDSS galaxy with photometric redshift, and finds supernova templates fit it poorly.

quotes.quote: Perhaps more importantly, three objects from our list are missing in the official ZTF alert stream, two of which we classify as AGN candidates ( SNAD154 , SNAD158 ) and the third as a possible SN ( SNAD156 ). Missed transients SNAD154 and SNAD156 have a peak magnitude 18.5^m-19.5^m which is comparable to those of other SNAD objects listed here but are also detected by the alert system.
For SNAD154 there is a known SDSS galaxy at the source position with measured photometric redshift and corresponding errors, which we use for redshift bounds.

SNAD150 light curves are best described by Nugent's Type Ia Supernova model, while SNAD154 is not well fitted by any of them---the observed light curve width near the peak brightness in zg and zr bands is smaller than the models suggest. Thus, we conclude that SNAD154 object is likely to be an active galactic nucleus.

gmw_00013136.name: SDSS J1130+0411
gmw_00013136.overview: SDSS J1130+0411 is a quasar that hosts six observed outflow systems with radial velocities ranging from 2400 to 15,400 km/s [arXiv:2208.09990]. Its main outflow system contains iron and silicon absorption troughs, which contribute to active galactic nucleus feedback [arXiv:2208.09990].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes absorption/outflow systems in quasar SDSS J1130+0411, deriving density, photoionization, distance, mass-flow, and kinetic-luminosity properties that imply significant AGN feedback.

quotes.quote: We present the analysis of the absorption troughs of six outflows observed in quasar SDSS J1130+0411 ( z ) with radial velocities ranging from 2400 to 15,400 km s ^ 1 . These spectra were taken with the Very Large Telescope/Ultraviolet and Visual Echelle Spectrograph over the rest frame wavelength range of 1135-1890 . In the main outflow system ( v 3200 km s ^ 1 ), we identify Fe ii and several Fe ii * absorption troughs as well as Si ii and Si ii * troughs, which we use to determine the electron number density n_e = cm ^ 3 . Using the column densities of these and other ions, we determine a photoionization solution with hydrogen column density N_H = cm ^ 2 and ionization parameter U_H = . From these values we derive the distance R = kpc, the average mass flow rate M = M_ yr ^ 1 , and the kinetic luminosity E _k = erg s ^ 1 .
This E _k is of the quasar's Eddington luminosity, and therefore contributes significantly to AGN feedback.

gmw_00013137.name: VPMS J170850.95+433223.7
gmw_00013137.overview: VPMS J170850.95+433223.7 is a weak-line quasar located at a redshift of z = 2.345 [arXiv:2208.00860]. It hosts a supermassive black hole of approximately 10^9 solar masses accreting near the Eddington limit, which may drive two high-velocity outflows [arXiv:2208.00860].

sources.summary: The paper identifies VPMS J170850.95+433223.7 as a z=2.345 weak-line quasar with faint UV broad lines, a roughly 10^9 solar-mass black hole near Eddington accretion, and two possible high-velocity outflows.

quotes.quote: We demonstrate that VPMS J170850.95+433223.7 is a weak line quasar (WLQ) which is remarkable in several respects. It was already classified as a probable quasar two decades ago, but with considerable uncertainty. The non-significant proper motion and parallax from the Gaia early data release 3 have solidified this assumption. Based on previously unpublished spectra, we show that VPMS J170850.95+433223.7 is a WLQ at z = 2.345 with immeasurably faint broad emission lines in the rest-frame ultraviolet. A preliminary estimate suggests that it hosts a supermassive black hole of 10^9 M_ accreting close to the Eddington limit, perhaps at the super-Eddington level.
We identify two absorber systems with blueward velocity offsets of 0.05c and 0.1c , which could represent high-velocity outflows, which are perhaps related to the high accretion state of the quasar.

gmw_00013138.name: host galaxy of XRF 050416A
gmw_00013138.overview: The host galaxy of XRF 050416A is a faint, blue galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.6535 [arXiv:astro-ph/0603738]. Spectral analysis reveals several emission lines, indicating that the galaxy has a large amount of ongoing star formation [arXiv:astro-ph/0603738].

sources.summary: The paper reports Keck spectroscopy of the host galaxy of XRF 050416A, describing it as faint, blue, actively star-forming, and showing emission lines at redshift z=0.6535.

quotes.quote: Spectra of the host galaxy of XRF 050416A were taken with the Low Resolution Imaging Spectrometer mounted on the 10-m Keck I telescope cenko3 . The spectrum indicates that the host galaxy is faint and blue with a large amount of ongoing star formation. Spectral analysis revealed several emission lines including [ OII ] , H , H , and H , at a redshift z=0.6535 0.0002 .

gmw_00013139.name: ALFALFA 4-043
gmw_00013139.overview: ALFALFA 4-043 is a face-on spiral galaxy [arXiv:2305.11168]. It has an estimated stellar mass of approximately 1.74 × 10²´ solar masses and a current star formation rate of about 0.59 solar masses per year [arXiv:2305.11168].

sources.summary: The paper identifies ALFALFA 4-043 as the face-on spiral host of SN 2021qqp, measuring the supernova offset and fitting host photometry to infer stellar mass, metallicity, age, and star formation.

quotes.quote: The host galaxy of SN 2021qqp is a face-on spiral galaxy, https://ned.ipac.caltech.edu/byname?objname=ALFALFA+4-043&hconst=67.8&omegam=0.308&omegav=0.692&wmap=4&corr_z=1 as shown in Figure  fig:hostcircs . SN 2021qqp is offset from the center of its host by 12 2 , or 10.7 kpc with the assumed standard CDM cosmology. As can be seen in Figure  fig:hostcircs , SN 2021qqp coincides with a spiral arm and is located just within the r_ 80 13 3 light radius.

We fit photometry from the Galaxy Evolution Explore (GALEX; Martin2005ApJ...619L...1M ) in the far-UV ( 155 nm) and near-UV ( 230 nm) bands; Swift UV/Optical Telescope (UVOT) The host-galaxy photometry was extracted with an aperture size of r_ 80 . in the UVW2,UVM2,UVW1,U,B , and V bands; SDSS in the u,g,,i , and z bands; and PS1 in the g,,i , and z bands using appropriate prior distributions M_ , Z , and , a characteristic e folding timescale of the delayed- star-formation history, SFH t e^ t/ ; see and references therein for details Carnall2019ApJ...873...44C and nested sampling using dynesty v2.1.1; Speagle2020MNRAS.493.3132S . The fitting results are shown in Figure  fig:hostsed , from which we find M_ = 1.74^ +0.64 _ 0.63 10^ 10 M_ , log _ 10 (Z/Z_ )=-0.29^ +0.28 _ 0.34 , t_ age =8.98^ +3.24 _ 3.33 Gyr, =1.86^ +0.83 _ 0.76 Gyr, and a current SFR (=SFH( t_ age )) of 0.59 0.22 M_ yr ^ 1 . Moreover, using the GALEX UV photometry, we estimate an SFR of 0.62 0.05 M_ yr ^ 1 with the Salim2007ApJS..173..267S calibration, which is consistent with the value from the SED fitting.

gmw_00013140.name: SDSS 130941.36+112540.1
gmw_00013140.overview: SDSS 130941.36+112540.1 is a quasar that was initially misidentified as a high-redshift source [arXiv:0809.0547]. Although the SDSS DR5 Quasar Catalog revised its redshift to z=1.362, the spectroscopic pipeline maintained an incorrect redshift of z=4.395 in DR6 [arXiv:0809.0547].

sources.summary: The paper flags SDSS 130941.36+112540.1 as a QSO whose SDSS high-redshift pipeline classification was likely wrong, excludes it from training, and notes its DR6 pipeline redshift persisted.

quotes.quote: For two of them, SDSS 130941.36+112540.1 and SDSS 153420.23+413007.5, we found the redshifts provided by the SDSS pipelines to be likely incorrect, and subsequently the QSOs were identified in the SDSS DR5 Quasar Catalog (DR5Q; Schneider et al. 2007) with revised redshifts z=1.362 and z=1.400 respectively. These two QSOs were not considered further and are not included in Table 1 and Fig. 1. On the one hand the revised values were published after we had already trained the NNs with the objects in Table 1 and carried out most of the follow up observations of the selected candidates.

On the other, having been misidentified as high- z QSOs in DR5 (redshift confidences 0.59 and 0.71 respectively), their exclusion from the training sample, used for the learning, seems reasonable. Since our aim is to obtain a high completeness for high- z QSOs, non high- z sources whose spectra can be confused with those of high- z QSOs should be preferably removed from the training sample, since their inclusion could hinder the selection of the high- z QSOs whose spectra they resemble. We note that the redshift for SDSS 153420.23+413007.5 at DR6 has been updated to the high-confidence manual value z=1.400 , but for SDSS 130941.36+112540.1 the redshift obtained with the spectroscopic pipelines, z=4.395 (confidence 0.55), has been maintained at DR6.

gmw_00013141.name: SDSS 153420.23+413007.5
gmw_00013141.overview: SDSS 153420.23+413007.5 is a quasar [arXiv:0809.0547]. Although initially assigned an incorrect high-redshift classification by the SDSS pipeline, its redshift was later manually updated to a high-confidence value of z=1.400 [arXiv:0809.0547].

sources.summary: The paper flags SDSS 153420.23+413007.5 as a QSO with a likely incorrect SDSS pipeline high-redshift classification, excludes it from training, and notes a DR6 manual redshift update.

quotes.quote: For two of them, SDSS 130941.36+112540.1 and SDSS 153420.23+413007.5, we found the redshifts provided by the SDSS pipelines to be likely incorrect, and subsequently the QSOs were identified in the SDSS DR5 Quasar Catalog (DR5Q; Schneider et al. 2007) with revised redshifts z=1.362 and z=1.400 respectively. These two QSOs were not considered further and are not included in Table 1 and Fig. 1. On the one hand the revised values were published after we had already trained the NNs with the objects in Table 1 and carried out most of the follow up observations of the selected candidates.

On the other, having been misidentified as high- z QSOs in DR5 (redshift confidences 0.59 and 0.71 respectively), their exclusion from the training sample, used for the learning, seems reasonable. Since our aim is to obtain a high completeness for high- z QSOs, non high- z sources whose spectra can be confused with those of high- z QSOs should be preferably removed from the training sample, since their inclusion could hinder the selection of the high- z QSOs whose spectra they resemble. We note that the redshift for SDSS 153420.23+413007.5 at DR6 has been updated to the high-confidence manual value z=1.400 , but for SDSS 130941.36+112540.1 the redshift obtained with the spectroscopic pipelines, z=4.395 (confidence 0.55), has been maintained at DR6.

gmw_00013142.name: SDSS J102606.67+011459.0
gmw_00013142.overview: SDSS J102606.67+011459.0 is classified as an extreme accretion rate quasar [arXiv:2009.14177]. The object exhibits extreme blueshifts and systematically high line intensity ratios, including a strongly enhanced blue-component line ratio [arXiv:2009.14177].

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J102606.67+011459.0 as an xA quasar with systematically high intensity ratios, extreme blueshift, and enhanced BLUE-component line ratios.

quotes.quote: In particular, at least two objects ( SDSS J102606.67+011459.0 and SDSS J085856.00+015219.4 ) show systematically higher ratios, with / 10 , and / 4 . Both of them show extreme \ blueshifts and SDSS J102606.67+011459.0 shows the highest / \ ratio in the sample.
SDSS J102606.67+011459.0 shows a strong enhancement of / \ and + , confirming the trend seen in its BC.

gmw_00013143.name: SDSS J085856.00+015219.4
gmw_00013143.overview: SDSS J085856.00+015219.4 is a quasar characterized by extreme line blueshifts [arXiv:1606.02726, arXiv:2009.14177] and systematically high line ratios [arXiv:2009.14177]. It is identified as one of the most metal-rich sources in its sample, though this measurement is affected by difficult continuum placement and line blending [arXiv:2208.02387].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSSJ0858+0152 as the object with the most blueshifted and asymmetric line; a Gauss-Hermite fit modestly changes its measured line width, dispersion, and blueshift.

quotes.quote: We opted to use a GH-polynomial model primarily because it provided a significantly better fit to the most blueshifted and asymmetric line (in SDSSJ0858+0152). Figure fig:fitting_comparison shows how a model with three Gaussian components underestimates the flux in the blue wing and overestimates the flux in the red wing of the line profile. Using the Gaussian model rather than the GH polynomial changes the FWHM, line dispersion, and blueshift by -3, -3, and 10 per cent respectively. We have highlighted SDSSJ0858+0152 because, of all the objects in our sample, the choice of model leads to the largest change in line parameters. Even in this case, however, the differences are modest.

sources.summary: The paper notes continuum-placement difficulty for SDSS J085856.00+015219.4 and finds that it has systematically high line ratios together with extreme blueshift behavior.

quotes.quote: There are problems in estimating the \ line intensity: an overestimation might be possible because of difficult continuum placement (see, for example, the case of SDSSJ085856.00+015219.4 in Appendix app:spec ).

In particular, at least two objects ( SDSS J102606.67+011459.0 and SDSS J085856.00+015219.4 ) show systematically higher ratios, with / 10 , and / 4 . Both of them show extreme \ blueshifts and SDSS J102606.67+011459.0 shows the highest / \ ratio in the sample.

sources.summary: The paper describes J0858+0152 as one of the most metal-rich sources, while noting that continuum placement and blending make the metallicity result uncertain.

quotes.quote: J0858+0152 is measured as one of the most metal rich sources in the sample of sniegowskaetal21 . It visually meets all the indicators signaling high Z : ratio / 1 , extreme / , low W . The result concerning the metallicity relies, however, on the continuum placement in the region at 1350 , where severe blending with \ and \ and ultimately with the very extended \ red wing makes it difficult to identify the continuum level properly.

gmw_00013144.name: IGR J18134 1636
gmw_00013144.overview: IGR J18134 1636 is a highly absorbed X-ray source discovered by the INTEGRAL satellite [arXiv:1410.6212]. It is identified as an obscured active galactic nucleus seen through the disc of the Milky Way [arXiv:1410.6212].

sources.summary: The paper identifies IGR J18134 1636 as a highly absorbed X-ray source whose infrared counterpart and AGN-template SED fit favor an obscured AGN interpretation at likely redshift below 0.05.

quotes.quote: IGR J18134 1636 was discovered by INTEGRAL bird06,krivonos07 . Its accurate astrometric position was determined from Chandra observations tomsick09 . The energy spectrum of the source is highly absorbed, with N_H (4-17) 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 . We studied NIR data for this source in UKIDSS DR7 (see Table tab_obs ) and report that single object is visible in H and K band images inside X-ray positional uncertainty, whereas it is not present in J band exposure (see the K band image in Figure fig_field ). Based on NIR coordinates from UKIDSS data we also identify this NIR counterpart with the object denoted as G013.8877+00.5968 in the GLIMPSE catalogue where it is detected in all 4 bands.

This AGN template nevertheless matches our data better than other models we checked (stellar atmospheres, Rayleigh-Jeans, YSOs and all other SWIRE library models). Moreover, obtained value of foreground extinction is in line with the total Galactic extinction in IGR J18134 1636 direction and heavily obscured nature of the best chosen template agrees well with X-ray data showing significant intrinsic absorption of the source. Therefore we suggest IGR J18134 1636 to be an obscured AGN seen through the disc of our Galaxy.

gmw_00013145.name: G30.1038+0.3984
gmw_00013145.overview: G30.1038+0.3984 is classified as an infrared-quiet radio galaxy in the CORNISH catalogue [arXiv:2209.05437]. However, further observations are required to definitively determine whether the source is extragalactic or Galactic in nature [arXiv:2209.05437].

sources.summary: The paper reports G30.1038+0.3984 as a resolved VLBA target classified by CORNISH as an IR-Quiet radio galaxy, but says more observations are needed to decide whether it is extragalactic or Galactic.

quotes.quote: G30.1038+0.3984 (E 1) This target was observed in 3 epochs spread over a year, and was detected in all three epochs. However, the target shows a resolved structure in all three epochs, and centroid measurements for resolved sources are not always consistent with the astrophysical core of the source. We do not have a good enough uv coverage to sample the resolved structure of the target well enough to determine how the centroid of the target shifts.

We will need longer duration observations to determine if the source is extragalactic or Galactic in nature. This target is classified as an IR-Quiet radio galaxy in the CORNISH catalogue. There is a PANSTARSS source 0.76 ^ away from the VLBA position of E 1, but since the positional accuracy of PANSTARSS is 0.01 ^ it is not a likely optical counterpart of E 1.

gmw_00013146.name: CHLT 2
gmw_00013146.overview: CHLT 2 is a time-variable radio source with centimeter flux densities ranging from 0.2 mJy to 4.4 mJy [arXiv:1703.03999]. Although initially identified as a brown dwarf candidate, its persistent radio emission suggests it is a radio galaxy [arXiv:1703.03999].

sources.summary: The paper describes CHLT 2 as a brown-dwarf candidate with persistent, variable radio emission and excessive radio luminosity; prior work argues its radio properties do not support that classification and proposes it is a radio galaxy.

quotes.quote: In the field we detected the source CHLT 2 (see Fig. 6), a brown dwarf candidate (Feigelson et al. 1998).
