gmw_00013268.name: ID 45
gmw_00013268.overview: ID 45 is a faint, high-redshift radio galaxy detected by the Hubble Space Telescope but undetected in Spitzer infrared imaging [arXiv:1911.12967]. It is classified as a radio-loud active galactic nucleus and represents a potential high-redshift infrared-faint radio source [arXiv:1911.12967].

sources.summary: The paper describes ID 45 as a faint HST-detected, Spitzer-undetected radio galaxy resolved at 1.4 and 5.5 GHz, likely a high-redshift radio-loud AGN and possibly the highest-redshift IFRS known.

quotes.quote: ID 45 is not detected in Spitzer IRAC or MIPS imaging. It has a very faint F140W detection in CANDELS HST imaging skelton2014 , with an AB magnitude of 24.47. This radio galaxy is bright and resolved at 1.4 GHz , with an integrated flux density of 1.38 27 mJy (Miller et al. 2013). It was also resolved at 5.5 GHz with a flux density of 424 17.8 Jy (H15).

The MIPS 24 m limit is 30 Jy (Magnelli et al. 2009) so this source has q_ 24 obs < 1.7 , indicating it is a radio-loud AGN. Skelton et al. 2014 estimate the photometric redshift of this source to be z = 5.7755, and at that redshift the source would have an XRay (2 - 10 keV) luminosity of 7.1 10 ^ 43 erg s ^ 1 , consistent with an AGN luo2017 . The IRAC 3.6 m detection limit is approximately 0.15 Jy (3 ), so this source meets the criteria for Infrared Faint Radio Source (IFRS), and is consistent with the paradigm that these sources are high redshift radio loud AGN. If the photometric redshift is correct, then this is highest redshift IFRS currently known (see orenstein2019 ).

gmw_00013269.name: object 58
gmw_00013269.overview: Object 58 is a galaxy located in the GU Boo field [arXiv:0712.3822]. Although initially presumed to be unresolved at large pixel sizes, photometry measurements revealed that this assumption does not hold true for the galaxy [arXiv:0712.3822].

sources.summary: The paper identifies object 58 as a galaxy in the GU Boo field, notes its light-curve use, tests whether it is unresolved at the large pixel size, and discards it from relative photometry when that assumption fails.

quotes.quote: Four objects out of Table tab3_obj_stats fulfill this criterion: numbers 18, 31, 58, and 66 (see Figures 18_lc - 66_lc for their light curves). The cross-referencing in Table tab3_obj_stats shows that objects 31 and 66 are stars, and objects 18 and 58 are galaxies (as are all other objects in the field that we were able to cross-reference).

We originally presumed that, despite the fact that they are galaxies, objects 18 and 58 would be unresolved at our large pixel size ( observations ) and tested that hypothesis by comparing the flux density obtained by PRF photometry to that obtained by aperture photometry (6'' aperture; prf_apphot ). Figures stars_comp and galaxies_comp shows that our presumption does not hold true for object 58, and we discarded it from our relative photometry procedure.

gmw_00013270.name: J0545 6846
gmw_00013270.overview: J0545 6846 is a BL Lac blazar candidate [arXiv:1809.03580]. The source is characterized by a particularly large radio flux and high radio-loudness, and it is associated with repeating flaring activity detected by the Fermi Large Area Telescope [arXiv:1809.03580].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0545 6846 as a BL Lac blazar candidate whose position matches possible Fermi LAT flaring locations and whose large radio flux and radio-loudness make a high-energy association plausible but uncertain.

quotes.quote: In Figure clouds (top panel), we show both possible locations of the flaring activity (green circle denotes the 2DG model and black circle -- H II gas map) which, moreover, coincide with positions of four our blazar candidates, namely J0545 6846, J0551 6843, J0551 6916, and J0552 6850. The BL Lac candidate J0545 6846, for which the broad-band SED is shown in Figure J0545-6846 , is consistent with both estimated locations, and is characterized by a particularly large radio flux and high radio-loudness in our sample. We however note here a rather large integrated flux >1 GeV of the LAT transient, 9.6^ +2.8 _ 6.1 10^ 12 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 ; if related to the flaring activity of J0545 6846, this would imply an unusually large value of the Compton dominance parameter as for a BL Lac candidate.
Although we did not find any clear associations with high-energy ray emitters, we note a repeating flaring activity detected with Fermi LAT from the direction of the J0545 6846 BL Lac candidate.

gmw_00013271.name: AEGIS-31956
gmw_00013271.overview: AEGIS-31956 is a high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of approximately z ~ 2 [arXiv:2006.13245]. The galaxy has sparse photometric measurements in the mid- and far-infrared wavelengths, which carry large uncertainties [arXiv:2006.13245].

sources.summary: The paper uses AEGIS-31956 as a reliable-photometry test case, comparing two dust-mass treatments and finding that energy-balance and independent far-UV-to-infrared SED fits are essentially identical despite sparse mid/far-IR data.

quotes.quote: Nevertheless, we can explore the capabilities of \ by analyzing one galaxy whose photometry appears to be reliable. We used the CANDELS images and data products of barro+19 to select a galaxy whose mid- and far-IR photometry appears to be relatively unaffected by blending issues and image confusion. We then ran \ twice, only changing the treatment of dust mass.

For the galaxy AEGIS-31956, the best-fit SED using the energy balance argument is essentially identical to that generated when the long-wavelength part of the SED is fit independently of the far-UV. As the SEDs illustrate, for this z 2 galaxy, there are few photometric measurements in the mid- and far-IR, and those data that do exist have large uncertainties.

gmw_00013272.name: IGR J12134-6015
gmw_00013272.overview: IGR J12134-6015 is an extragalactic source with an infrared counterpart detected in the J band [arXiv:1209.2945]. Its hard X-ray spectrum shows no exponential cutoff up to 100 keV, suggesting the object is most likely an active galactic nucleus or a blazar [arXiv:1209.2945].

sources.summary: The paper identifies an infrared counterpart for IGR J12134-6015, reports a hard Swift/INTEGRAL power-law spectrum without cutoff, and argues that the source is extragalactic, most likely an active galactic nucleus or blazar.

quotes.quote: In the error box of the X-ray source IGR J12134-6015, only one object with coordinates R.A.=12 ^h 13 ^m 23.98 ^s , Dec=-60 15 16.9 \ (J2000) (at a distance of 0.32 \ from the localization center of the X-ray source, Fig. 3a) is present in the 2MASS infrared sky survey. Its brightness is J=16.45 0.12 ; no counterpart is detected at a statistically significant level in the H and Ks bands (only the upper limits H > 16.5 and Ks > 15.5 are given in the 2MASS catalog). The corresponding upper limits for the colors are J - H < 0.07 and J - Ks < 1.07 .

The broadband spectrum of the source obtained with INTEGRAL (17-100 keV) and SWIFT (0.5-10 keV, ObsID.00038348) data shows no influence of interstellar extinction (the 2 upper limit is 4 10^ 21 atoms cm ^-2 ) and can be described by a power law for the spectral density of photons in the form dN(E)/dE E^ 0.85 without an exponential cutoff up to energies of 100 keV (Fig. 4). Such a hard energy spectrum is quite atypical for galactic sources, but it is often encountered among extragalactic sources. Thus, the object being studied is most likely extragalactic in nature and is an active galactic nucleus or a blazar. Optical spectroscopic observations are needed for definitive conclusions to be reached.

gmw_00013273.name: IGR J17350-2045
aliases_external: IGR J17350-2045 infrared counterpart / NVSS J173459-204533
gmw_00013273.overview: IGR J17350-2045 is an active galactic nucleus with a faint infrared counterpart [arXiv:1209.2945]. Its broadband X-ray spectrum is fitted by an absorbed power law, and it is coincident with a radio source from the NVSS sky survey [arXiv:1209.2945].

sources.summary: The paper identifies a faint infrared counterpart to IGR J17350-2045, fits its broadband X-ray spectrum with an absorbed power law, finds a coincident NVSS radio source, and classifies it as an active galactic nucleus.

quotes.quote: However, using archival data from the SOFI instrument of the NTT telescope (the European Southern Observatory, ESO; the observations were performed on May 26, 2007; the exposure time was 4 and 6 s in the H and Ks bands, respectively), we were able to distinguish a faint optical object with coordinates R.A. =17 ^h 34 ^m 58.834 ^s , Dec=-20 45 32.08 \ (J2000) located at a distance of 1.8 \ from the Xray localization center. Our photometric analysis of the data yielded the following magnitudes: H=14.92 0.06 and Ks=14.37 0.06 (see. fig.3(d)).

The broadband X-ray spectrum of IGR J17350-2045 obtained from XMM-Newton and INTEGRAL data in the energy range 0.5-100 keV is shown in Fig. 7. We see from the figure that the data from both observatories are in good agreement with each other and that the spectrum in the entire energy range can be fitted by a simple power law with a slope =1.5 0.2 and absorption N_H=(18 3) 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 . Such a shape of the spectrum and its parameters are typical of active galactic nuclei.

It turned out that the source NVSS J173459-204533 with a flux of about 13 mJy is present in the NVSS sky survey at this location (the difference between the astrometric positions of the radio source and the X-ray source is 3 , which is compatible with their localization errors). Thus, IGR J17350-2045 is most likely an active galactic nucleus. Additional spectroscopic observations are needed to determine its distance.

gmw_00013274.name: Galaxy 142 30
gmw_00013274.overview: Galaxy 142 30 is a luminous, metal-rich galaxy with a relatively high star formation rate of 6.42 solar masses per year [arXiv:1201.0939]. It is located at a distance of 217 kiloparsecs from a nearby Ne VIII absorber [arXiv:1201.0939].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Galaxy 142 30 near the z=0.72478 Ne VIII absorber, describing it as luminous, metal-rich, star-forming, and surprisingly far from the sight line at 217 kpc.

quotes.quote: The other LRIS galaxy (142 30) is only v +30 from the Ne VIII absorber at =0.72478. In many regards, Galaxy 142 30 has the characteristics one might expect to find for an object embedded in a gas-rich halo: the galaxy is luminous (L L ^ ), has a high metallicity ( [ O/H ] =+0.22 0.15), and a relatively high SFR of 6.42 0.44 M _ yr ^ 1 . However, the galaxy has one surprising feature: it is fairly far from the sight line (impact parameter = 217 kpc). Given the high metallicity of the affiliated absorber, Z > Z_ , the relatively large distance to the nearest galaxy is notable, although we stress that more galaxy redshift measurements are required to thoroughly probe the origin of this absorbing gas.

gmw_00013275.name: CX3
gmw_00013275.overview: CX3 is classified as an active galactic nucleus based on its blue color and proper motion [arXiv:2111.00357]. It exhibits potential photometric variability and lacks a radio detection despite being relatively bright in X-rays [arXiv:2111.00357].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the CX3 counterpart as an AGN based on blue colour and proper motion, notes possible photometric variability, and finds its absent radio detection still consistent with AGN radio/X-ray ratios.

quotes.quote: The CX3 counterpart, which L17 classified as an active galactic nucleus (AGN), on the basis of its blue colour and proper motion consistency with the external galaxy frame, is outside of the \ and \ fields in the HUGS imaging, and thus the HUGS UV photometry does not provide any additional information. Its HUGS \ magnitude is 0.25 mag fainter than its GO-12254 \ magnitude. A magnitude difference as large as 0.25 mag might indicate some photometric variability, rather than simply measurement uncertainty, since the HUGS dataset does not include all of the GO-12254 data (see footnote to Table t:hst_obs ). The lack of a radio detection of a relatively X-ray bright AGN is mildly unusual, but the radio/X-ray flux ratio is not outside the range observed for AGN by Maccarone12 .

gmw_00013276.name: CX10
gmw_00013276.overview: CX10 is classified as an active galactic nucleus candidate based on its blue color and its proper motion consistency with the external galaxy frame [arXiv:2111.00357]. It exhibits a strong HUGS excess and shows potential photometric variability [arXiv:2111.00357].

sources.summary: The paper identifies CX10 as an AGN candidate from blue colour and extragalactic-frame proper motion, reports HUGS excess and possible variability, and says a tentative radio detection would corroborate the AGN nature.

quotes.quote: The CX10 counterpart, which L17 classified as an AGN, on the basis of its blue \ colour and its proper motion consistency with the external galaxy frame (see Fig. 5 of L17), is in the \ field of the HUGS imaging but outside of the \ field. It shows a strong \ excess. Its HUGS \ magnitude is 0.21 mag fainter than the GO-12254 \ magnitude. As for the case of CX3, this difference may either indicate some photometric variability or else photometric uncertainty. If the 3 radio detection here is genuine ( s:radio_observation ), the AGN nature could be further corroborated.

gmw_00013277.name: CX15
gmw_00013277.overview: CX15 is classified as an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2111.00357]. This classification is based on its blue color and a proper motion that is consistent with the extragalactic frame [arXiv:2111.00357].

sources.summary: The paper reports CX15 as an AGN classified from blue colour and extragalactic-frame proper motion, present across all HUGS fields and showing a large UV excess in both HUGS colour-magnitude diagrams.

quotes.quote: As in the cases of CX3 and CX10, L17 classified CX15 as an AGN, on the basis of its blue colour and proper motion consistency with the extragalactic frame. Unlike CX3 and CX10, CX15 is present in all three HUGS fields of view. It has a large UV excess in both HUGS CMDs (Figs.  f:CMD_HUGS_UV-U_CV and f:CMD_HUGS_U-B_CV ).

gmw_00013278.name: CX17
gmw_00013278.overview: CX17 is an amorphous galaxy that is likely undergoing an interaction [arXiv:2111.00357]. The galaxy is coincident with a radio source, and recent observations reveal a nearby steep-spectrum radio counterpart typical of an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2111.00357].

sources.summary: The paper describes CX17 as an amorphous, likely interacting galaxy with a coincident radio source; new ATCA data find a nearby steep-spectrum radio counterpart typical of AGNs.

quotes.quote: As L17 noted, we do not find a starlike object in the error circle for CX17 . Instead, there is an an amorphous object, suggesting an interacting galaxy as seen in Fig.  f:finding_charts_14-19 . As Pooley02 have noted, this object is coincident with a radio source, detected by an ATCA observation Frater92 . Our ATCA observation also reveals a radio source that lies 0 3 from the nominal X-ray position of CX17 (Fig. f:radio_xray_matched_finders ) with a moderately steep spectral index ( = -0.7 0.3 ; Table t:radio_matches ) typical of AGNs.

gmw_00013279.name: CX42
gmw_00013279.overview: CX42 is a likely active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2111.00357]. Its hard X-ray spectrum suggests high absorption, and it is associated with a nearby radio source [arXiv:2111.00357].

sources.summary: The paper argues CX42 is a likely AGN because its hard X-ray spectrum suggests absorption and a nearby radio source would be unlikely for a cluster CV.

quotes.quote: The X-ray spectrum of CX42 can be fit by a hard power-law ( = -0.4^ +0.9 _ 1.0 ; Table t:spectral_analyses_51 ) when N_ H is fixed to the cluster value. This is typical of sources with high N_ H , where the fixed N_ H is too low to account for the lack of soft X-rays. A high N_ H could be from a torus around an AGN, or from an accretion disc in a CV (when viewed edge-on).

We find a radio source 0 1 from the X-ray position of CX42 (Fig.  f:radio_xray_matched_finders ), with a spectral index of 0.5 0.3 (Table t:radio_matches ). We still include it in the CMDs for CVs, but consider the Chandra source CX42 to be a likely AGN, given its radio detection which would be unlikely for a CV at the distance of NGC 6752.

gmw_00013280.name: CX44
gmw_00013280.overview: CX44 is an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2111.00357]. It is identified as an external galaxy based on its proper motion, which is discordant from the cluster distribution [arXiv:2111.00357].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the fainter object in the CX44 error circle as an AGN, citing discordant proper motion similar to the external galaxy frame and no HUGS detection.

quotes.quote: There are two objects within the CX44 error circle. The fainter object is somewhat blue in \ but shows an \ deficit in both the CMD and the colour-colour diagram. We measure a proper motion for this object that is discordant from the cluster distribution, and is similar to the external galaxy frame. It appears that this object is an AGN . It is not detected by the HUGS photometry.

gmw_00013281.name: CX45
gmw_00013281.overview: CX45 is classified as an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2111.00357]. This classification is supported by a hard X-ray spectrum that may indicate absorption from an AGN torus, as well as a nearby flat-spectrum radio source that could represent a radio lobe [arXiv:2111.00357].

sources.summary: The paper classifies CX45 as an AGN, supported by an AGN-like fainter object, a flat-spectrum nearby radio source, possible radio lobe emission, hard X-rays, and inferred torus absorption.

quotes.quote: Like the fainter object in the CX44 error circle, this fainter object also appears to be an AGN . Indeed, our ATCA observation found a flat-spectrum ( =0.0 0.6 ) radio source 0 8 south of the nominal X-ray position of CX45 (Fig. f:radio_xray_matched_finders ). The radio error ellipse marginally matches the X-ray error circle, but is not consistent with the optical counterpart.

The ATCA source could then be a radio lobe produced by the AGN. Moreover, the X-ray spectrum of CX45 can be fit by a hard power-law ( = 0.1^ +1.1 _ 1.3 ; Table t:spectral_analyses_51 ). Similar to CX22 and CX42, this could indicate enhanced absorption from an AGN torus. We thus classify CX45 as an AGN.

gmw_00013282.name: 3XMM J064747.1 441950
gmw_00013282.overview: 3XMM J064747.1 441950 is an extragalactic active galactic nucleus candidate [arXiv:2001.02917]. It is proposed as a likely blazar counterpart to a Fermi gamma-ray source, supported by its infrared colors and double-peaked spectral energy distribution [arXiv:2001.02917].

sources.summary: The paper proposes 3XMM J064747.1 441950 as a likely blazar counterpart to the Fermi source, citing matched optical/WISE counterparts, extragalactic origin, infrared colors, double-peaked SED, and broad-band indices.

quotes.quote: Further inspection of POSS II plates additionally reveals a faint optical source whose position is in sub-arsecond agreement with the 'central' X-ray source 3XMM J064747.1 441950. Shining at R 19.0 , this point-like object corresponds to entry 0456-0065720 in the USNO B1 0 Catalog 2003AJ....125..984M , where its lack of proper motion also agrees with an extragalactic origin. In the absence of spectroscopic information, a reassuring information about the nature of this AGN candidate comes from the infrared domain. This is thanks to the Wide-field Infrared Survey Explorer (WISE) and its associated catalogue releases 2010AJ....140.1868W, 2013yCat.2328....0C , where we find the AllWISE J064747.12-441950.3 source. To confirm as confidently as possible that the XMM, USNO and this WISE object are actually the same, we plot their relative positions in Fig. astromet where their mutual match agrees within a fraction of an arc-second.

In addition, an alternative counterpart candidate of likely blazar nature, the X-ray source 3XMM J064747.1 441950, almost centered inside the Fermi LAT 95 confidence ellipse, is proposed as a more reliable identification. This statement is mainly supported by: i) the existence of non-thermal radio emission symmetrically located around it (SUMSS and GMRT sources); ii) the non-detection of the binary orbital period in the Fermi LAT light curve; iii) the agreement of the object's infrared colours with the well established WISE Blazar Strip; iv) the similarity of the double-peaked SED with that of LSP/ISP blazars; v) the agreement of radio/X-ray/gamma flux ratios and spectral indices also with LSP/ISP blazars. Taken together, all these facts would render \ a very ordinary blazar emitter of gamma rays instead of a new addition to the scarce family of GBs.

gmw_00013283.name: Chandra source, MSID = 165442
gmw_00013283.overview: Chandra source, MSID = 165442 is a high-redshift, X-ray-selected quasar located at a redshift of z = 5.86 [arXiv:1309.7048]. Spectroscopic observations of the source reveal broad Lyman-alpha emission, classifying the object as a broad-line active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1309.7048].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Chandra source, MSID = 165442 as a z=5.86 broad-line, X-ray-selected quasar, the most distant spectroscopically confirmed X-ray-selected quasar found in an X-ray survey at the time.

quotes.quote: We discovered the highest redshift ( z=5.86 ) quasar yet identified in an X-ray survey.

In this pilot survey of Stripe 82X, we have discovered only one object beyond a redshift of 5, but it is the most distant X-ray selected quasar from an X-ray survey to date, at z = 5.86 with L _ 0.5-10keV = 4.4 10^ 45 erg s ^ 1 ( Chandra source, MSID = 165442). The SDSS spectrum of this source is shown in Figure highz_spec , revealing broad Ly (i.e., this source is classified as a broad line AGN).
We have found the most distant, spectroscopically confirmed X-ray selected quasar in an X-ray survey to date, at z = 5.86 .

gmw_00013284.name: 3FGL J0045.2-3704
gmw_00013284.overview: 3FGL J0045.2-3704 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar located at a redshift of z = 1.0331 [arXiv:1701.06363]. Its spectrum displays magnesium II emission originating from the broad-line region and carbon II emission from the narrow-line region [arXiv:1701.06363].

sources.summary: The paper reports SALT spectra of 3FGL J0045.2-3704, measures z=1.0331, classifies it as an FSRQ from Mg II emission, and distinguishes BLR/NLR line origins.

quotes.quote: 3FGL J0045.2-3704 This source was observed twice with SALT using the C (blue) and D (red) RSS configurations, and the averaged spectrum is shown in Fig. fig:J0044 . In the blue spectrum [ C ii ] ( _0 = 2326.93 ) and Mg ii ( _0 = 2797.99 ) are detected at 4730 and 5689 . This places the source at a redshift of z = 1.0331 0.0004 . The weaker [ C ii ] feature has an equivalent width of 1.5 0.8 , while the stronger Mg ii line is 17.9 3.4 which classifies the source as a FSRQ. The [ C ii ] and Mg ii emission lines have FWHM values of 808.6 km s ^ 1 and 2777.1 km s ^ 1 , respectively, suggesting that the Mg ii originates from the BLR while the [ C ii ] line originates from the NLR.

gmw_00013285.name: 3FGL J0200.9-6635
gmw_00013285.overview: 3FGL J0200.9-6635 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar located at a redshift of z=1.28 [arXiv:1701.06363]. Optical spectra of the source reveal broad carbon III and magnesium II emission lines originating from its broad-line region [arXiv:1701.06363].

sources.summary: The paper presents blue and red SALT spectra of 3FGL J0200.9-6635, detects C III and Mg II emission, derives z=1.28, and classifies the source as an FSRQ with BLR emission.

quotes.quote: 3FGL J0200.9-6635 We obtained both blue (configuration C) and red (configuration D) RSS spectra for 3FGL J0200.9-6635 as shown in Fig. fig:J0201 . Two emission lines, C iii ] ( _ 0, vac = 1908.73 ) and Mg ii ( _0 = 2797.99 ), are detected at 4300 and 6400 , respectively. This places the source at a redshift of z=1.28 0.01 . The source is classified as an FSRQ due to the broad emission features with equivalent widths of |W_ , C III ] | = 34 13 and |W_ , Mg ii | = 42 12 , for the C iii ] and Mg ii lines, respectively.
Similarly, the broad emission lines (FWHM _ C iii =5948.3 km s ^ 1 , FWHM _ Mg ii ,blue =2825.8 km s ^ 1 and FWHM _ Mg ii ,red =2915.3 km s ^ 1 ) indicate the emission originates from the BLR.

gmw_00013286.name: 3FGL J0644.3-6713
gmw_00013286.overview: 3FGL J0644.3-6713 is an active galactic nucleus classified as a flat-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:1701.06363]. It has a redshift of z = 1.930, and its spectrum displays carbon emission lines alongside a carbon absorption feature [arXiv:1701.06363].

sources.summary: The paper reports a blue SALT spectrum of 3FGL J0644.3-6713, identifies carbon emission and absorption features, derives z=1.930, and classifies the source as an FSRQ.

quotes.quote: 3FGL J0644.3-6713 A spectrum was obtained for this source in the C (blue) configuration (Fig. fig:J0644 ). The averaged spectrum features two carbon humps, namely, C iv ( _ 0, vac = 1548.19 ) and C iii ] at 4540 and 5586 , respectively. An absorption feature, which we also associate with C iv is detected at 4540 , suggesting that some material must be lying further away from the central source to produce an absorption feature. The measurement of the C iii ] feature is, unfortunately, hampered by the strong sky line at 5577 . The broad carbon emission lines give a redshift of z = 1.930 0.004 while the equivalent width ( |W_ | = 24 13 ) and FWHM (7446.3 km s ^ 1 ) of the C iv emission line classify 3FGL J0644.3-6713 as a FSRQ.

gmw_00013287.name: 3FGL J0730.5-6606
gmw_00013287.overview: 3FGL J0730.5-6606 is an active galactic nucleus classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1701.06363]. Spectral observations reveal absorption lines that place the source at a redshift of z=0.106 [arXiv:1701.06363].

sources.summary: The paper combines SAAO and SALT spectra of 3FGL J0730.5-6606, finds absorption lines and z=0.106, notes its AGN-like weak Ca break, and classifies it as a BL Lac.

quotes.quote: 3FGL J0730.5-6606 3FGL J0730.5-6606 was observed with the SAAO 1.9-m (Fig. fig:J0730_saao ) with follow-up observations with SALT (Configuration A, Fig. fig:J0730 ). The spectrum shows Ca ii H K ( _ 0,H = 3933.66 and _ 0,K = 3968.47 ), Mg i ( _0 = 5172.68 ) and NaD ( _0 = 5895.92 ) absorption lines. The average spectral line position from both the SAAO 1.9-m and SALT observations place the source at a redshift z=0.106 0.001 . The AGN nature of the source is clear from the weak Ca break, which we measure as K _ 4000 = 0.16. Therefore, 3FGL J0730.5-6606 is classified as a BL Lac.

gmw_00013288.name: 3FGL J1218.8-4827
gmw_00013288.overview: 3FGL J1218.8-4827 is classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1701.06363]. Optical spectroscopy reveals a nearly featureless spectrum with potential absorption features that tentatively place the source at a redshift of z=0.150 [arXiv:1701.06363].

sources.summary: The paper reports a near-featureless SALT spectrum of 3FGL J1218.8-4827, tentatively identifies absorption features implying z=0.150, and classifies the source as a BL Lac.

quotes.quote: SALT spectroscopy of 3FGL J1218.8-4827 (configuration A, S/N 40 ) shows a near featureless spectrum (Fig. fig:J1218 ), with the indication of potential Ca ii H K and G-band absorption features at 4494, 4537 and 4971 , respectively. If these features are correct, it places the source at z=0.150 0.006 . Therefore, based on the mainly featureless spectra, we suggest that this is a BL Lac.

gmw_00013289.name: 3FGL J1407.7-4256
gmw_00013289.overview: 3FGL J1407.7-4256 is potentially classified as a BL Lacertae object due to its mainly featureless spectrum and lack of emission lines [arXiv:1701.06363]. Tentative G-band and NaD absorption lines suggest the source has a redshift of approximately 0.124 [arXiv:1701.06363].

sources.summary: The paper describes SAAO and SALT observations of 3FGL J1407.7-4256, finds no clear features, tentatively identifies absorption lines, estimates z=0.124, and potentially classifies it as a BL Lac.

quotes.quote: 3FGL J1407.7-4256 The second last source, 3FGL J1407.7-4256, was observed with both the SAAO 1.9-m (Fig. fig:J1407_saao ) and SALT (C and D configurations, Fig. fig:J1407 ). However, no clear features are identified. We, therefore, potentially classify this source as a BL Lac due to its mainly featureless spectrum and the lack of observed emission lines. The G-band and NaD absorption lines are tentatively suggested at 4859 and 6563 , respectively, in the RSS spectrum (Fig. fig:J1407 ) though follow-up studies should be undertaken to obtain a higher S/N. If these are correct the source has a redshift of z=0.124 0.009 .

gmw_00013290.name: objID=587736479206342888
gmw_00013290.overview: The galaxy objID=587736479206342888 was initially reported to have a suspiciously low velocity width of 27 km/s in the ALFALFA.40 catalog [arXiv:1501.03114]. Upon closer inspection, its noisy spectrum revealed a possible second emission component that led to the original underestimate [arXiv:1501.03114].

sources.summary: The paper says objID=587736479206342888 had a suspiciously low ALFALFA velocity width because its noisy spectrum included a second emission component; the authors smoothed the spectrum and recomputed the galaxy's parameters.

quotes.quote: For one object, objID=587736479206342888, the ALFALFA.40 catalog reports a suspiciously low velocity width of 27 . Upon inspection of the spectrum, the data were found to be very noisy with a possible second component in the emission. The original spectrum only measured one narrow peak resulting in a under estimate of the velocity width. We applied further smoothing to the spectrum and re-measured the flux adding the second peak. From this spectrum we then re-computed the parameters for this galaxy with our own data reduction tools, and report the final adopted value in Table alf_tab .

gmw_00013291.name: QSO J2228 0753
gmw_00013291.overview: QSO J2228 0753 is a quasar [arXiv:2007.06824] that behaves as a point source at high radio frequencies [arXiv:2007.06824]. High-resolution ALMA observations at 289 GHz and 405 GHz show that its measured position is consistent with both ALMA and VLBA calibrator catalogs [arXiv:2007.06824].

sources.summary: The paper uses QSO J2228 0753 as the high-frequency ALMA target, images it at 289 and 405 GHz, measures its position, and finds the astrometry consistent with ALMA and VLBA calibrator catalogs.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we report on HF long-baseline (14--16 km) image capability tests for a point source QSO J2228 0753 in Band 7 (289 GHz) and Band 8 (405 GHz) observed together with a closely located phase calibrator, J2229 0832, in Band 3 (96 GHz) and Band 4 (135 GHz), respectively, to demonstrate a quasi end-to-end ALMA science observation.

The left panels of Figures fig:07 and fig:08 show synthesized images of the target J2228 0753 at 289 GHz in Band 7-3 and at 405 GHz in Band 8-4, respectively, after B2B phase referencing. The highest angular resolution of 14 11 mas was achieved at Band 8-4(1) with a maximum projected baseline length of 13 km.

The averaged position of J2228 0753 for the two epochs and two Bands is ( _ J2000 , _ J2000 )=( 22^ h 28^ m 52^ s .607590 , 7^ 53'46''.64238 ) with a 1 error of (0.24, 0.40) mas. The ALMA calibrator source catalogue provides the position of the target as ( _ J2000 , _ J2000 )=( 22^ h 28^ m 52^ s .60764 , 7^ 53'46''.6414 ) with a 1 error of (0.6, 1.2) mas, so that the measured position of J2228 0753 is consistent with the position in the ALMA calibrator catalogue within the quoted uncertainties. In centimeter-wave observations, J2228 0753's position measured with VLBI is ( _ J2000 , _ J2000 )=( 22^ h 28^ m 52^ s .607568 , 7^ 53'46''.64215 ) with a 1 error of (0.10, 0.21) mas from the Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA) calibrator list http://www.vlba.nrao.edu/astro/calib/ , thus, the obtained position is also consistent within 0.4 mas, less than a 20th of the Band 8-4 synthesized beam.

gmw_00013292.name: MGC 2214+3550 A,B
gmw_00013292.overview: MGC 2214+3550 A,B is a binary quasar pair located at a redshift of z=0.88 [arXiv:astro-ph/9710014]. The two components have a small projected separation of 3 arcseconds, with only the brighter optical component A exhibiting radio emission [arXiv:astro-ph/9710014].

sources.summary: The paper identifies MGC 2214+3550 A,B as a z=0.88 binary quasar pair, separated by 3 arcsec, with radio emission only from component A and lensing ruled out.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The VLA radio coordinates for the peak of the compact core are =22:14:56.98, =35:51:25.8 (J2000.0)

quotes.quote: We report the discovery of a binary quasar, MGC 2214+3550 A,B, whose components have similar optical spectra at a redshift z=0.88 . The quasars are separated on the sky by 3 0 , and have a magnitude difference of m_I=0.5 mag. The VLA radio map at 3.6 cm shows a single 47 mJy radio source with a core-jet morphology that is coincident with the brighter optical quasar A. Gravitational lensing is ruled out by the lack of radio emission from quasar B, and the lack of any visible galaxies to act as the lens.
We conclude that MGC 2214+3550 A and B are physically associated. With a projected separation of 12.7 h^ 1 kpc ( _ 0 =1 ), MGC 2214+3550 A,B is one of the smallest z>0.5 binary quasars.

gmw_00013293.name: 3FGL J0921.0-2258
gmw_00013293.overview: The active galactic nucleus 3FGL J0921.0-2258 is classified as a blazar [arXiv:1909.10834]. Its Fermi LAT spectrum is best fit by a log-parabola, showing strong intrinsic curvature that makes it an exception for detection with the Cherenkov Telescope Array [arXiv:1909.10834].

sources.summary: The paper reports that 3FGL J0921.0-2258 has a Fermi LAT spectrum best fit by a log-parabola, differs from its preliminary 4FGL power-law counterpart, and is a CTA exception because of strong intrinsic curvature.

quotes.quote: Only for three sources (3FGL J0921.0-2258, 3FGL J0153.4+7114, and 3FGL J0506.9-5435) did we find that a log-parabola is preferred at the 4 level, i.e. TS _ curv > 16 , where TS _ curv is twice the difference of the log-likelihood values of the best fit with a log-parabola and a power law, respectively.

The 4FGL sources corresponding to 3FGL J0921.0 2258 and 3FGL J0153.4+7114 have power-law spectra instead of the curved spectra we found, although the evidence for curvature is of low significance in our analysis.
Any of the VHC sources that lie at low redshift should be detectable with CTA, with the exception of 3FGL J0921.0 2258, which shows strong intrinsic curvature.

gmw_00013294.name: J123624.6+620728
gmw_00013294.overview: J123624.6+620728 is a variable radio source that is reported in the e-MERLIN catalogue [arXiv:2402.01569]. It is a low-signal-to-noise ratio VLBA source that remains undetected in the Owen2018 VLA catalogue [arXiv:2402.01569].

sources.summary: The paper reports J123624.6+620728 as an e-MERLIN-only catalogue source, a low-SNR VLBA source with e-MERLIN flux ratio above unity, and a variable source undetected in the Owen2018 VLA catalogue.

quotes.quote: One source, J123701.1+622109 was only reported in the VLA catalogue Owen2018 and missing in the catalogue, whilst the source J123624.6+620728 is only reported in the e MERLIN catalogue Muxlow2020 and not the VLA catalogue.

The comparison between VLBA and (see Figure  figure:Fluxratios , middle panel b), reveals a median flux ratio of R_ eMERLIN = 0.63 , with two low-SNR ( 5.5 ) sources (J123627.2+620605 and J123624.6+620728) showing R_ eMERLIN >1 . The source J123624.6+620728 is reported as variable in Radcliffe2019 and remains undetected in the Owen2018 VLA source catalogue, while the source J123627.2+620605 shows a similar trend in VLA with R_ VLA >1 .

gmw_00013295.name: J123627.2+620605
gmw_00013295.overview: J123627.2+620605 is a low signal-to-noise ratio radio source with no available redshift [arXiv:2402.01569]. It exhibits VLBA/e-MERLIN and VLBA/VLA flux ratios above unity, including the largest VLA flux-density ratio in its sample [arXiv:2402.01569].

sources.summary: The paper notes J123627.2+620605 lacks a redshift, is a low-SNR source with VLBA/e-MERLIN and VLBA/VLA flux ratios above unity, and has the sample's largest VLA flux-density ratio.

quotes.quote: The source J123627.2+620605 has no available redshift.

The comparison between VLBA and (see Figure  figure:Fluxratios , middle panel b), reveals a median flux ratio of R_ eMERLIN = 0.63 , with two low-SNR ( 5.5 ) sources (J123627.2+620605 and J123624.6+620728) showing R_ eMERLIN >1 . The source J123624.6+620728 is reported as variable in Radcliffe2019 and remains undetected in the Owen2018 VLA source catalogue, while the source J123627.2+620605 shows a similar trend in VLA with R_ VLA >1 .

The majority of the VLBA-VLA flux density ratios (see Figure  figure:Fluxratios , right panel ) show R_ VLA <1 , with only one source (J123627.2+620605) showing R_ VLA >1 . This is a 5.5 source and shows the largest R_ VLA =2.48 .

gmw_00013296.name: J123622.5+620653
gmw_00013296.overview: J123622.5+620653 is a galaxy classified as a non-radio-excess source [arXiv:2402.01569]. It is a variable source that exhibits a high VLBA radio luminosity and a higher flux density in VLBA compared to EVN [arXiv:2402.01569].

sources.summary: The paper reports J123622.5+620653 as a remaining galaxy with higher VLBA than EVN flux density, identifies it as variable, and flags its high VLBA luminosity among non-radio-excess sources needing follow-up.

quotes.quote: Only 2/4 of the remaining galaxies, J123640.5+621833 and J123622.5+620653, show significantly greater flux density in VLBA at R_ EVN = 1.35 and 1.33, respectively. The source J123622.5+620653 is also reported as a variable in Radcliffe2019 .

The remaining 4/7 sources considered non-radio excess (J123716.7+621733, J123646.3+621404, J123634.5+621241 and J123622.5+620653), have high luminosities L_ VLBA >10^ 24.5 W Hz ^ 1 and future work should be done to investigate these non-radio excess sources with high radio luminosity.

gmw_00013297.name: SXDS1 12778
gmw_00013297.overview: SXDS1 12778 is a star-forming galaxy located at a redshift of approximately z ~ 1.4 [arXiv:1407.0392]. Observations of the galaxy indicate a gas metallicity close to the solar value, though its CO(2-1) emission line is only weakly detected [arXiv:1407.0392].

sources.summary: The paper includes SXDS1 12778 among three z~1.4 star-forming galaxies, finds a weak offset CO(2-1) feature with SN=1.8, and sets an upper CO flux limit.

quotes.quote: We carried out ^ 12 CO( J=2-1 ) observations toward three star-forming galaxies on the main sequence at z 1.4 with the Nobeyama 45m radio telescope. These galaxies are detected with Spitzer /MIPS in 24 m , Herschel /SPIRE in 250 m and 350 m , and their gas metallicity, derived from optical emission line ratios based on near infrared spectroscopic observations, is close to the solar metallicity.

In the spectra of SXDS3 80799 and COSMOS 9, a weak signal-like feature is seen close to the expected zero point with a velocity width of 200-250 \ km \ s^ 1 . The spectrum of SXDS1 12778 also shows a weak signal-like feature with a 200 \ km \ s^ 1 offset from the arrow. The noise level at 250 \ km \ s^ 1 resolution on T_ mb scale, _ 250 , is 1.0 mK, 0.7 mK, and 1.0 mK for SXDS1 12778, SXDS3 80799, and COSMOS 9, respectively. The signal-to-noise ratio (SN) for SXDS1 12778, SXDS3 80799, and COSMOS 9 with 250 \ km \ s^ 1 resolution is 1.8, 2.4, and 2.6, respectively.

The signal-like features may be real, but the SN is not good enough to be significant. Hence, we don't regard these features as significant signal and we put upper limits on the CO(2-1) fluxes of the targets.

gmw_00013298.name: SXDS3 80799
gmw_00013298.overview: SXDS3 80799 is a star-forming galaxy located at a redshift of approximately z ~ 1.4 [arXiv:1407.0392]. It lies on the main sequence of star-forming galaxies and has a gas metallicity close to the solar value [arXiv:1407.0392].

sources.summary: The paper includes SXDS3 80799 among three z~1.4 star-forming galaxies, finds a weak near-zero CO(2-1) feature with SN=2.4, and sets an upper CO flux limit.

quotes.quote: We carried out ^ 12 CO( J=2-1 ) observations toward three star-forming galaxies on the main sequence at z 1.4 with the Nobeyama 45m radio telescope. These galaxies are detected with Spitzer /MIPS in 24 m , Herschel /SPIRE in 250 m and 350 m , and their gas metallicity, derived from optical emission line ratios based on near infrared spectroscopic observations, is close to the solar metallicity.

In the spectra of SXDS3 80799 and COSMOS 9, a weak signal-like feature is seen close to the expected zero point with a velocity width of 200-250 \ km \ s^ 1 . The spectrum of SXDS1 12778 also shows a weak signal-like feature with a 200 \ km \ s^ 1 offset from the arrow. The noise level at 250 \ km \ s^ 1 resolution on T_ mb scale, _ 250 , is 1.0 mK, 0.7 mK, and 1.0 mK for SXDS1 12778, SXDS3 80799, and COSMOS 9, respectively. The signal-to-noise ratio (SN) for SXDS1 12778, SXDS3 80799, and COSMOS 9 with 250 \ km \ s^ 1 resolution is 1.8, 2.4, and 2.6, respectively.

The signal-like features may be real, but the SN is not good enough to be significant. Hence, we don't regard these features as significant signal and we put upper limits on the CO(2-1) fluxes of the targets.

gmw_00013299.name: COSMOS 9
gmw_00013299.overview: COSMOS 9 is a star-forming galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 1.4 [arXiv:1407.0392]. Observations of the galaxy revealed a weak CO(2-1) emission feature near the expected zero point, though its signal-to-noise ratio of 2.6 is insufficient to confirm a detection [arXiv:1407.0392].

sources.summary: The paper includes COSMOS 9 among three z~1.4 star-forming galaxies, finds a weak near-zero CO(2-1) feature with SN=2.6, and sets an upper CO flux limit.

quotes.quote: We carried out ^ 12 CO( J=2-1 ) observations toward three star-forming galaxies on the main sequence at z 1.4 with the Nobeyama 45m radio telescope. These galaxies are detected with Spitzer /MIPS in 24 m , Herschel /SPIRE in 250 m and 350 m , and their gas metallicity, derived from optical emission line ratios based on near infrared spectroscopic observations, is close to the solar metallicity.

In the spectra of SXDS3 80799 and COSMOS 9, a weak signal-like feature is seen close to the expected zero point with a velocity width of 200-250 \ km \ s^ 1 . The spectrum of SXDS1 12778 also shows a weak signal-like feature with a 200 \ km \ s^ 1 offset from the arrow. The noise level at 250 \ km \ s^ 1 resolution on T_ mb scale, _ 250 , is 1.0 mK, 0.7 mK, and 1.0 mK for SXDS1 12778, SXDS3 80799, and COSMOS 9, respectively. The signal-to-noise ratio (SN) for SXDS1 12778, SXDS3 80799, and COSMOS 9 with 250 \ km \ s^ 1 resolution is 1.8, 2.4, and 2.6, respectively.

The signal-like features may be real, but the SN is not good enough to be significant. Hence, we don't regard these features as significant signal and we put upper limits on the CO(2-1) fluxes of the targets.

gmw_00013300.name: Object A
gmw_00013300.overview: Object A is a background source located near the pulsar PSR J2144-3933 [arXiv:1101.5100]. It is likely a galaxy, as indicated by its somewhat patchy morphology and optical colors [arXiv:1101.5100].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Object A near PSR J2144-3933 as a likely background galaxy, giving its coordinates, optical magnitudes, patchy morphology, and noting that the angular separation could cause a detectable gravitational-lensing displacement.

quotes.quote: Its coordinates, =21^ h 44^ m 11 97 , = -39^ 33 56 42 , imply an offset of 1.2 arcsec from the computed pulsar position at the epoch of our \ observations, i.e. 7 times larger than the overall uncertainty of our astrometric solution. Thus, we can safely rule out that this object is associated to the pulsar. Its magnitudes, measured through customised aperture photometry with sextractor , are U=23.39 0.05 , B=23.82 0.03 , V=23.76 0.05 , after correcting for the air mass. Its colours suggest that the object (Object A) might be a background star, or, more likely, a galaxy, as suggested by its somewhat patchy morphology.

Considering that \ is a nearby neutron star and that Object A is a relatively bright ( B =23.8) background source (probably a galaxy), their small angular separation might cause a detectable gravitational lensing effect (see, e.g., paczynski96hst ). The displacement (or deformation, being the object slightly extended) of the background source due to the gravitational field of a point-like mass is expected to be paczynski96 = _E^2 , where _E is the Einstein ring radius and is its angular distance from the lens. In the approximation of a very distant lensed object, the Einstein radius for a lens with mass M at a distance D is _E = 4GM ^2 D ^ 1/2 . Although the proximity of \ ( D=170 pc) and its relatively small angular separation from Object A ( =1.2 arcsec) make it the most favorable case for any known neutron star, for a mass of 1.4 a displacement of only =0.06 mas is expected.

gmw_00013301.name: ID 215424
gmw_00013301.overview: ID 215424 is a galaxy analyzed using full-spectrum fitting techniques [arXiv:2205.05701]. Incorporating photometric data alongside spectroscopic data improves the modeling of its stellar population, accurately reproducing the depth of its CaII H and K absorption lines [arXiv:2205.05701].

sources.summary: The paper uses ID 215424 as an example galaxy to show that adding UltraVISTA photometry improves full-spectrum fitting: the spectroscopic-only posterior misfits CaII H/K, while photometry reproduces the feature.

quotes.quote: In Figure  fig:spectrum&SFH2 we show the full spectrum fitting results from an example galaxy (ID 215424) obtained with spectroscopic data alone and adding photometry. We observe that in the posterior the CaII K line is less deep than the H line for the spectroscopic (only) fitting, contradicting the observational data. On the contrary, this feature is well reproduced after including the photometric data in the fit.

gmw_00013302.name: 2MASX J09325962+0405062
gmw_00013302.overview: 2MASX J09325962+0405062 is a type-I active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2106.14522]. Although initial eROSITA observations showed an extremely flat X-ray power-law slope, follow-up Chandra and optical spectroscopy confirmed it is a typical type-I active galactic nucleus without Compton-thick or warm absorption [arXiv:2106.14522].

sources.summary: The paper flags 2MASX J09325962+0405062 as a type-I AGN with an unusually flat eROSITA X-ray slope; Chandra and optical follow-up rule out Compton-thick absorption or warm absorption and favor a typical type-I AGN.

quotes.quote: We noticed that a type-I AGN 2MASX J09325962+0405062 (ID = 352, z= 0.0592) has an extremely flat power-law slope of 1.25 ( 1.07 1.42 ). Its flat spectral shape might be due to Compton-thick absorption or due to warm absorber. To test the type-I Compton-thick scenario, we performed simultaneous optical and X-ray spectroscopic observations. We observed this source with Chandra on Jan. 26 and 28, with a total exposure time of 50 ks. We performed optical photometry on Jan. 10, 11, 13, 16, 18, 26, 27, 28, and 29 with the GROND instrument on the 2.2m ESO/MPG telescope and optical spectroscopy on Jan. 24, 26, 27, and 29 at Asiago.

However, the Chandra spectrum can be well described by a powerlaw with photon index of 1.63 0.09 , and does not require a Compton-thick absorption model or a warm absorber. Therefore, this source is more of a typical type-I AGN. The flat slope measured by eROSITA might be caused by spectral variability or by additional uncertainties in the extraction of source and background spectra for this particular source.

gmw_00013303.name: host galaxy of XRF 030723
aliases_external: host galaxy of XRF 030723 at the optical afterglow position
gmw_00013303.overview: The host galaxy of XRF 030723 is associated with a faint, extended source detected near the transient's afterglow position [arXiv:astro-ph/0402240]. This host galaxy has an estimated upper limit magnitude of R ~ 27 [arXiv:astro-ph/0402240].

sources.summary: The paper reports only a faint source near the XRF 030723 afterglow, treats a nearby galaxy as probably unrelated, and sets R 27 as an upper limit for the host.

quotes.quote: The Host Galaxy In our latest R-band image from Sep. 25.1 there is a faint, extended source with magnitude R =26.8 0.4 at the position of the afterglow (Fig. fig:host and Fynbo et al. \ 2003). However, a deeper and later image taken with the SUBARU telescope shows the presence of a R =27.6 0.4 point source at the afterglow position (Kawai et al. \ 2003). The nearest other galaxy is located about 2 arcsec north of the position of the afterglow. Whereas we cannot exclude this object as the host galaxy it is most likely unrelated to XRF 030723. We therefore consider R 27 to be an upper limit on the magnitude of the host galaxy of XRF 030723.

gmw_00013304.name: Q 1218 + 0832
gmw_00013304.overview: Q 1218 + 0832 is a quasar located at a redshift of z=2.60 [arXiv:2308.15781]. The object is reddened and dimmed by dust in a foreground damped Lyman-alpha absorber at z=2.226, which causes a strong narrow-band flux deficit [arXiv:2308.15781].

sources.summary: The paper treats Q 1218 + 0832 as a z=2.60 quasar reddened by a dusty foreground DLA, revisits its field, and notes its strong narrow-band flux deficit.

quotes.quote: We report on further observations of the field of the quasar Q 1218 + 0832. Geier2019 presented the discovery of the quasar resulting from a search for quasars reddened and dimmed by dust in foreground Damped Lyman- Absorbers (DLAs).

In this paper, we will present new data for one such a system, namely the striking example of the z=2.226 DLA towards the z=2.60 Q 1218 + 0832 for other examples see Krogager2016B, Fynbo2017, Heintz2018 .
Q 1218 + 0832 stands out as the object with the strongest narrow-band flux deficit.

gmw_00013305.name: S
gmw_00013305.overview: S is a bright high-redshift emission-line galaxy located south of the quasar Q 1218 + 0832 [arXiv:2308.15781]. It exhibits a compact continuum size of approximately 300 pc alongside a larger, resolved Lyman-alpha emission structure spanning about 4 kpc [arXiv:2308.15781].

sources.summary: The paper identifies S as a bright emission-line/Lyman-alpha source south of Q 1218 + 0832, giving its coordinates, extended narrow-band emission, compact continuum size, and line flux.

quotes.quote: One object at RA(J2000) = 12:18:28.41, Dec(J2000)= + 08:31:22.65 has a strong excess of emission in the narrow-band image as seen in Fig. fig:Emitter . The object is marked as 'S' 59 arcsec south of Q 1218 + 0832 in Fig. fig:field . At z=2.23 59 arcsec correspond to 493 proper kpc. The object is very bright and can be seen in all the individual 3000-sec narrow-band exposures.

In the HST image, the source is only slightly larger than the PSF with a measured fwhm of 0.119 arcsec whereas a PSF has a fwhm of 0.113 arcsec. The intrinsic size of the object must be only about 0.037 arcsec or 300 pc at z=2.2261 . In the narrow band, the source is also resolved leading to an intrinsic size of about 4 kpc.

The flux of the line is 2.5 10 ^ 16 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 , which is in good agreement with the estimated flux from the narrow-band imaging given the uncertainty in the narrow-band calibration and the effect of slit loss.

gmw_00013306.name: SDSS J121822.83 + 083157.0
gmw_00013306.overview: SDSS J121822.83 + 083157.0 is a quasar located at a redshift of z=2.637 [arXiv:2308.15781]. Its sightline is separated from the nearby quasar Q 1218+0832 by 109 arcseconds and shows no metal absorption at z=2.2261 [arXiv:2308.15781].

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J121822.83 + 083157.0 as a nearby z=2.637 quasar sightline, separated from Q 1218+0832, with no metal or strong H I absorption at the DLA redshift.

quotes.quote: There is another quasar in the field, namely SDSS J121822.83 + 083157.0 (marked 'QB' in Fig. fig:field ). This quasar is at nearly the same redshift as Q 1218+0832 ( z=2.637 vs. z=2.60 for Q 1218+0832) and has a separation of 109 arcsec from Q 1218+0832 and 90 arcsec from the emission line source. Converted to physical distances at z=2.23 the distance between the two quasar sightlines is 899 proper kpc. In the spectrum of SDSS J121822.83 + 083157.0 (shown in Fig. fig:SDSSquasar ), there is no metal absorption at z=2.2261 down to a 3- observed equivalent width limit of 0.6 . There is also no strong absorption in the Lyman- forest at this redshift.

gmw_00013307.name: SDSS J121824.76 + 083443.5
gmw_00013307.overview: SDSS J121824.76 + 083443.5 is a photometric quasar candidate [arXiv:2308.15781]. It has an estimated photometric redshift of 2.245, with a lower bound of 2.040 and an upper bound of 2.630 [arXiv:2308.15781].

sources.summary: The paper cites SDSS J121824.76 + 083443.5 as a photometric quasar candidate near the DLA field, with uncertain redshift and potential use as an additional absorption sightline.

quotes.quote: Furthermore, we note that Richards2009 classify the object SDSS J121824.76 + 083443.5 as a quasar with a photometric redshift of z=2.245 (marked 'QC' in Fig. fig:field ). This object is located 168 arcsec North of Q 1218 + 0832. The upper and lower values for the photometric redshift estimate are by Richards2009 determined to be z_ L = 2.040 and z_ U = 2.630 , respectively.

Hence, the object may well be unrelated to the DLA, but it would be interesting to measure the spectroscopic redshift for this source to establish if it is part of the same structure as the DLA or possibly is at the same redshift as the two other quasars. If it is at a larger redshift of the DLA the spectrum could still be used to look for intervening absorption at z=2.2261 - the redshift of the DLA towards Q 1218 + 0832. We note that the object is not detected by Gaia nor by the WISE satellite Wise2015,Gaia2023 .

gmw_00013308.name: J 1113-1533, =3.2655
gmw_00013308.overview: J 1113-1533 is a high-redshift galaxy [arXiv:1409.4577] at a redshift of z=3.2655 [arXiv:1409.4577]. It contains a damped Lyman-alpha system with a four-component absorption structure [arXiv:1409.4577].

sources.summary: The paper reports a new argon abundance for the z=3.2655 DLA J 1113-1533, deriving a four-component structure and using an unsaturated, non-blended 1066 line.

quotes.quote: J 1113-1533, =3.2655: this DLA has an \ column density of log N( ) =21.23 0.05 . The four-component structure of this system is derived from the fit to the \ 1250, 1259 absorption lines. The argon abundance is derived from unsaturated, non-blended \ 1066 line (see Fig. fig:1113 ). The redshift of the components, Doppler parameters and column densities are listed in Table tab:1113 . The \ lines are either saturated or blended.

gmw_00013309.name: J 1356-1101, =2.9669
gmw_00013309.overview: J 1356-1101 is a high-redshift galaxy [arXiv:1409.4577] located at a redshift of z=2.9669 [arXiv:1409.4577]. This damped Lyman-alpha system features a four-component structure derived from its absorption lines [arXiv:1409.4577].

sources.summary: The paper reports a new argon abundance for the z=2.9669 DLA J 1356-1101, fitting a four-component structure from the 1808 line and using the 1066 line.

quotes.quote: J 1356-1101, =2.9669: this DLA has an \ column density of log N( ) =20.80 0.10 . The four-component structure of the system is derived from the fit to the \ 1808 absorption line. The argon abundance is derived from unsaturated, non-blended \ 1066 line (see Fig. fig:1356 ). The fit parameters are described in Table tab:1356 . Note that noterdaeme08 derived log N( ) =14.96 0.06 , consistent with our results within the errors.

gmw_00013310.name: B 2332-094, =3.0572
gmw_00013310.overview: B 2332-094 is a high-redshift galaxy [arXiv:1409.4577] classified as a sub-damped Lyman-alpha system at a redshift of z=3.0572 [arXiv:1409.4577]. Its metal profile is spread over several components, with unsaturated, non-blended argon absorption lines detected in three of its strongest components [arXiv:1409.4577].

sources.summary: The paper treats B 2332-094 as a z=3.0572 sub-DLA with several metal-profile components, deriving argon from the unblended 1048 line in its strongest components.

quotes.quote: B 2332-094, =3.0572: this sub-DLA has an \ column density of log N( ) =20.27 0.07 . The metal profile is spread over several components. However, unsaturated, non-blended \ 1048 line is seen in only three of the strongest components (see Fig. fig:2332 ). This three-component structure is used to fit the \ 1259, \ 1608, and \ 1808 absorption lines. The parameters for the fit are summarised in Table tab:2332 .

gmw_00013311.name: XID 30
gmw_00013311.overview: XID 30 is an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1510.06737]. Its light curve is best fitted by a CARMA(2,0) process and exhibits weak periodic behavior, though its power spectral density shows no signature of a quasi-periodic oscillation [arXiv:1510.06737].

sources.summary: The paper describes XID 30 as an AGN whose CARMA(2,0) light-curve model leaves narrow residuals and weak apparent periodicity, though no PSD QPO signature is found.

quotes.quote: In contrast the AGN with XID 30 (lower panel of Fig. fig:residuals ), which is best fitted by a CARMA(2,0) process, shows some level of deviation from the expected distribution. Since the histogram of the residuals is significantly narrower than the standard normal, a Gaussian process may not be the best description for this light curve. In fact the light curve indicates weak periodic behaviour which may cause the difference from the normal distribution. However the observed periodicity is likely a coincidence due to the irregular sampling pattern and in fact the PSD of this source does not show any signature of a quasi-periodic oscillation (QPO). Nonetheless the data suggest that the autocorrelation structure is correctly described by the CARMA model for this object.

gmw_00013312.name: SDSSJ0839+0454
gmw_00013312.overview: SDSSJ0839+0454 is a low-ionization broad-absorption line quasar detected by SPIRE [arXiv:2007.04334]. The system exhibits prolific star formation, with an estimated star formation rate of 1610 solar masses per year [arXiv:2007.04334].

sources.summary: The paper classifies SDSSJ0839+0454 as a SPIRE-detected LoBAL quasar, argues its slightly offset far-infrared flux is associated with the quasar, and derives a high star-formation rate.

quotes.quote: These three targets - SDSSJ0810+4806, SDSSJ0839+0454 and SDSSJ0943-0100 - are therefore classed as detections and form a core subsample of this work (Fig. fig:ImgDetect ). We do however note that the flux of SDSSJ0839+0454 appears to be dominated by light from the source North West of the catalogued quasar position.

To confirm that this flux is associated with the quasar, we search for SDSSJ0839+0454 observations from the Wide-field Infrared Survey Explorer (WISE). Indeed, we find our quasar target is detected in three of the four WISE bands (W1; 3.4 m, W2; 4.6 m, W3; 12.0 m) and we detect positive flux at the position of our target in the remaining band (W4; 22.0 m). In contrast, no source is detected in multiple WISE bands north-west of the quasar target. We therefore conclude that the although the flux in Fig fig:ImgDetect appears slightly offset from the catalogue position of SDSSJ0839+0454, it is still likely associated with the quasar and not a spurious source in the image.

Based on this conversion (Eqn. eqn:lSFR ), we derive SFRs of 740 _ 170 ^ +220 , 1610 _ 260 ^ +280 and 2380 _ 210 ^ +220 M _ yr ^ 1 for SDSSJ0810+4806, SDSSJ0839+0454 and SDSSJ0943-0100 respectively (Table tab:SFRs ), finding evidence for prolific star formation in our detected LoBAL sub-sample.

gmw_00013313.name: SDSSJ0943-0100
gmw_00013313.overview: SDSSJ0943-0100 is classified as a low-ionization broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:2007.04334]. The system exhibits prolific star formation, with an estimated far-infrared star-formation rate of 2380 solar masses per year [arXiv:2007.04334].

sources.summary: The paper classifies SDSSJ0943-0100 as one of three SPIRE-detected LoBAL quasars and derives a far-infrared star-formation rate of 2380 solar masses per year.

quotes.quote: These three targets - SDSSJ0810+4806, SDSSJ0839+0454 and SDSSJ0943-0100 - are therefore classed as detections and form a core subsample of this work (Fig. fig:ImgDetect ).
To this end, we estimate the FIR SFRs of our detected targets (SDSSJ0818+4806, SDSSJ0839+0454 and SDSSJ0943-0100) based on the aperture photometry derived in Sec. sec:AperPhot (Tab. tab:DataTab ).

Based on this conversion (Eqn. eqn:lSFR ), we derive SFRs of 740 _ 170 ^ +220 , 1610 _ 260 ^ +280 and 2380 _ 210 ^ +220 M _ yr ^ 1 for SDSSJ0810+4806, SDSSJ0839+0454 and SDSSJ0943-0100 respectively (Table tab:SFRs ), finding evidence for prolific star formation in our detected LoBAL sub-sample.

gmw_00013314.name: GRB 141220A host galaxy
gmw_00013314.overview: The GRB 141220A host galaxy is a dusty, absorbing environment located at a redshift of z = 1.3195 [arXiv:2106.10295]. It exhibits high obscuration, with host galaxy dust that can induce optical polarization of up to 1.8 percent [arXiv:2106.10295].

sources.summary: The paper models the GRB 141220A host as a dusty, absorbing environment, deriving host-frame reddening and hydrogen absorption and estimating that host dust can explain much of the observed optical polarization.

quotes.quote: The models we employ are either power laws (powerlaw in XSPEC) or connected power laws (bknpower) that account for the rest-framed host galaxy and MW total hydrogen column absorption and dust extinction (phabs*zphabs*zdust*zdust). For both SEDs, we fix the MW dust extinction to E_ B-V, MW = 0.0128 0.0005 1998ApJ...500..525S , the total hydrogen column absorption to N_ H, MW = 1.33 10^ 20 cm ^ 2 (derived using the NHtot Swift tool https://www.swift.ac.uk/analysis/nhtot/ ; 2013MNRAS.431..394W ) and the GRB redshift to z =1.3195 2014GCN.17198....1D . Given that the number of data bins is low compared to the number of parameters of the model, we further reduce the model complexity. We assume no temporal evolution for the host galaxy dust and total hydrogen absorption, and we also pair the optical and X-ray spectral indexes of both SEDs. Additionally, we assume an Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) profile for the host galaxy dust absorption 2007MNRAS.377..273S .

In the host galaxy rest frame, the reddening is E_ B-V, HG = 0.24 0.06 (i.e. absorption of A_ V, HG = 0.71 0.15 mag) and the total hydrogen absorption is N_ H, HG = (3 2) 10^ 21 cm ^ 2 . MW and Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) dust profiles yield compatible results with A_ V, HG = 0.74 0.18 mag and A_ V, HG = 0.76 0.16 mag, respectively.
The obscuration in the GRB host galaxy is high; we estimate that host galaxy dust could induce up to P 1.8 at most ---consistent with our observations.

gmw_00013315.name: MQS J050155.46 700210.1
gmw_00013315.overview: MQS J050155.46 700210.1 is an active galactic nucleus candidate that exhibits two distinct redshift systems at z=0.228 and z=0.341 [arXiv:1106.3110]. This system may represent a chance alignment or a gravitational lens involving a faint source and a brighter extended galaxy [arXiv:1106.3110].

sources.summary: The paper describes MQS J050155.46 700210.1 as an AGN-candidate target with two redshift systems, possibly a chance alignment or gravitational lens involving a brighter extended galaxy.

quotes.quote: One target, MQS J050155.46 700210.1, shows emission lines corresponding to redshifts of z=0.228 and 0.341 , as shown in Figure fig:doubleAGN . This could be a chance coincidence or a gravitational lens. In the OGLE-III reference image (Figure fig:doubleAGNchart ), we find that the AGN candidate was a faint source ( V=21.3 , I=20.5 ) in the wings of a brighter extended source.

The standard OGLE catalogs may not include all extended sources, so it is likely that the faint source was mistargeted and the brighter galaxy is the AGN candidate. If we assume the bright galaxy is acting as a gravitational lens, then we estimate that it has an Einstein radius of R_ E <0.4 arcsec, which is very small because of the small redshift difference. Higher resolution images and spatially resolved spectra are required to clarify the nature of this source.

gmw_00013316.overview: The quasar 0842+5804 exhibits the bluest mid-infrared-to-optical color in its sample, despite having a redder ultraviolet spectral shape [arXiv:2106.02646]. It also displays weak magnesium II and iron II emission, and its visually compact radio core is significantly extended [arXiv:2106.02646].

sources.summary: 0842+5804 has the sample's bluest MIR-to-optical normalization but a redder UV shape and weak Mg II/Fe II emission; e-MERLIN fits show its visually compact radio core is significantly extended.

quotes.quote: The spectrum of 0842+5804 is plotted in Figure spectra_comparisons with a black line to contrast it with the mean composite spectra of the cQSOs and rQSOs. Its normalisation is higher than both the composites because it has the bluest MIR-to-optical colour of our entire sample. Despite this, it shows a UV spectral shape that is a bit redder than the median cQSOs. It also shows the weakest Mg II 2800 equivalent width among our targets, as well as weaker than average Fe II band emission.

0842+5804, shown in black in Figure beamcomps , is the only visually compact object which has a significantly larger core size than the corresponding restoring beam. While its radio core is fit well by a single Gaussian, the size of this component is 30 larger than the beam, showing that it is definitely extended.

gmw_00013317.overview: The object 1003+2727 is classified as a core-dominated, single-component core quasar [arXiv:2106.02646]. Although observations show an apparent offset between its unresolved radio core and its optical position, this astrometric solution may be unreliable due to calibration complications [arXiv:2106.02646].

sources.summary: The paper flags 1003+2727 as a cQSO with an apparent radio-optical offset, but calibration problems make the astrometric solution unreliable, so it is treated as a core-dominated single-component source.

quotes.quote: Two objects show offsets in the long tail of the distribution: 0842+5804 and the cQSO 1003+2727.

1003+2727, on the other hand, has an image that is completely consistent with the restoring beam, yet the respective panel in Figure images clearly shows a visible offset between the centre of its apparently unresolved core and the optical position. However, the gain calibration for this particular source was complicated by heavy flagging of the calibrator scans by the pipeline, and the final astrometric solution may be unreliable. In light of this, we do not place much weight on the apparent offset, and henceforth treat 1003+2727 as a single component core-dominated source. Possible higher frequency e-MERLIN observations in the future may help to verify its nature.

gmw_00013318.overview: The quasar 0946+2548 is a radio source composed of two bright resolved knots separated by 0.5 arcseconds along a position angle of -17 degrees [arXiv:2106.02646]. The Gaia position of the quasar lies between these two main knots, coinciding with a faint, marginal radio knot [arXiv:2106.02646].

sources.summary: e-MERLIN images of 0946+2548 reveal two resolved radio knots, a faint knot near the GAIA QSO position, perpendicular extension in the northwest knot, and possible diffuse emission along the source axis.

quotes.quote: 0946+2548 The presence of a nearby bright radio source affected the imaging of this object, leading to a high level of noise. The final self-calibration was performed on this nearby source, and then applied to the target. The QSO radio source is composed of two bright resolved knots separated by 0 5 , along PA =-17^ which we take to define an axis for the system.

The GAIA position of the QSO lies between the two main knots, coinciding with another faint knot which has a marginal S/N (Figure images ). The brighter north-western knot shows an extension perpendicular to the axis. There is a hint of diffuse patchy emission oriented along the axis that extends beyond the two knots, which may indicate partially-recovered extended emission.

gmw_00013319.overview: The object 1007+2853 is classified as a radio-quiet quasar [arXiv:2106.02646]. It features two compact radio components separated by 0.34 arcseconds, which may represent the opposite lobes of a bipolar jet or outflow [arXiv:2106.02646].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1007+2853 as an rQSO with two compact radio components bracketing the QSO position, interpreted as possible opposite lobes of a bipolar jet or outflow.

quotes.quote: Five of our targets exhibit visually extended emission, both from an examination of the central images (Figure images ) and based on their single-component Gaussian fits: 0946+2548, 1007+2853, 1122+3124, 1153+5651, and 1535+2434. All five are rQSOs.

1007+2853 The source consists of two compact components separated by 0 34 along PA =0^ , which we modelled using two gaussian components. The QSO position lies between the two components, suggesting that they are opposite lobes of a bipolar jet or outflow.

gmw_00013320.overview: The radio-quiet quasar 1153+5651 exhibits a complex radio morphology dominated by a central core [arXiv:2106.02646]. This core is accompanied by a bipolar extension, a mildly resolved northern knot with a faint tendril of emission, and a poorly-defined southern lobe [arXiv:2106.02646].

sources.summary: 1153+5651 shows complex radio morphology: a dominant core, bipolar extension, a northern knot with a faint tendril, and southern low-S/N lobe-like emission.

quotes.quote: Five of our targets exhibit visually extended emission, both from an examination of the central images (Figure images ) and based on their single-component Gaussian fits: 0946+2548, 1007+2853, 1122+3124, 1153+5651, and 1535+2434. All five are rQSOs.

1153+5651 This source exhibits a complex morphology with a dominant central core and a bipolar extension along PA =171^ . In addition to the bright unresolved core, there is a mildly resolved knot 0 42 away, from which a faint tendril of emission extends even further north. Towards the south, faint emission appears to outline the edge of a poorly-defined lobe. We model the source using three components, one each for the core, northern knot and extended jet-like emission. The low S/N of the apparent lobe-like southern feature prevents it from being modelled.

gmw_00013321.overview: The object 1535+2434 is a quasar [arXiv:2106.02646]. Its radio source is composed of two bright knots separated by 0.27 arcseconds, with the Gaia position of the quasar located close to the center of the extended western knot [arXiv:2106.02646].

sources.summary: The paper describes 1535+2434 as a QSO whose calibrated e-MERLIN image shows two bright radio knots, with the GAIA QSO position near an extended western knot.

quotes.quote: 1535+2434 The pipeline-calibrated images of this source were strongly affected by two bright sources a few arcminutes away. A first round of wide-field self-calibration was used to model the target as well as these bright nearby contaminants. After strong data averaging to smear out the effects of the other sources, a final round of self-calibration was performed on the target to obtain the final image. The QSO radio source is composed of two bright knots separated by 0 27 along PA =115^ . The GAIA position of the QSO is close to the centre of the western knot, which is also extended beyond the size of the beam.

gmw_00013322.overview: The source 1410+2217 is a compact quasar dominated by a bright, unresolved radio core [arXiv:2106.02646]. It exhibits detectable extended emission, including a faint arc-like lobe feature modeled as two knots south of the core [arXiv:2106.02646].

sources.summary: 1410+2217 is a cQSO with detectable extended emission: a bright unresolved core plus a faint arc-like lobe feature modeled with two knots.

quotes.quote: 1410+2217 This is one of two cQSOs with detectable extended emission. The source structure is dominated by a bright unresolved core. South of the core, 2 25 away along PA =173^ , a faint arc-like feature marks the outer edge of a barely-defined lobe. We modelled the source as a core component and two faint knots on the edge of this tentative lobe, which we manually adjusted and tied during the fit.

gmw_00013323.overview: The quasar 1511+3428 is resolved into two compact radio sources separated by 5.06 arcseconds [arXiv:2106.02646]. The fainter source corresponds to the optical quasar, while the brighter northeastern knot may represent a hotspot of an asymmetric radio galaxy [arXiv:2106.02646].

sources.summary: e-MERLIN resolves 1511+3428 into two compact sources; the fainter matches the optical QSO, while the brighter northeastern knot may be an asymmetric FRII-like radio-galaxy hotspot.

quotes.quote: 1511+3428 The QSO was selected for visual compactness in its FIRST imaging, but our e-MERLIN image clearly resolves it into two compact sources, of which the fainter source corresponds to the optical location of the QSO (Figure multi_image ). The two knots are separated by 5 06 along PA =53^ . The core is unresolved, but the north-eastern knot has a FWHM that is 25 larger than the restoring beam, indicating a moderately resolved structure. The brighter north-eastern knot could be one hotspot of an asymmetric FRII-like radio galaxy. Neither FIRST or VLASS images, or a natural-weighted large-scale image of the e-MERLIN data, show any evidence of a south-eastern counterpart to the north-eastern knot.

gmw_00013324.name: PMN J1106-3647
gmw_00013324.overview: PMN J1106-3647 is a quasar that exhibits large, short-term variations in both the amplitude and shape of its radio spectrum [arXiv:1705.06051]. These rapid spectral changes, which include sharp kinks, broad peaks, and wiggles, are interpreted as interstellar scintillation of a compact, non-pointlike radio source [arXiv:1705.06051].

sources.summary: The paper reports ATCA radio observations of quasar PMN J1106-3647, finding large, rapidly changing spectral variations with sharp kinks, broad peaks, and wiggles, interpreted as interstellar scintillation of a compact but non-pointlike radio source.

quotes.quote: We report radio observations of the quasar PMN J1106-3647. Our data, taken with the Australia Telescope Compact Array, show large variations in the amplitude and shape of its spectrum, on a short time-scale. A great variety of spectral features is evident, including: sharp kinks; broad spectral peaks; and wiggles.

No two spectra are alike. We interpret the variations as interstellar scintillation of a radio source that is compact, but not point-like. Under this interpretation, complex spectral structure can arise purely refractively, under high magnification conditions, or from interference between waves that have been scattered by small-scale density fluctuations (diffractive scintillation).

gmw_00013325.name: Q0105+061
gmw_00013325.overview: Q0105+061 is an active galactic nucleus classified as an associated absorption line quasar [arXiv:1809.05433]. Its absorbers exhibit blueshifted outflows with velocities ranging from 1900 to 3000 kilometers per second, which may originate from quasar-driven winds [arXiv:1809.05433].

sources.summary: Q0105+061 is treated with two other AAL quasars; their absorbers show 1900-3000 km/s blueshifted outflows, no partial covering, large inferred distances, and uncertain feedback relevance.

quotes.quote: The AALs in the other three quasars in our sample, Q0105+061, Q0334 204, and Q2044 168, have velocity blueshifts consistent with outflows at speeds of 1900 to 3000 . These systems do not exhibit partial covering of the background light source, and the upper limits we derive on their densities, 150 to 15 , place the absorbers at large distances from the quasars, namely, 40 to 330 kpc. Thus the relationship of the AAL systems to the quasars is not known. It is possible that they form in intervening gas that is not physically related to the quasars, such as galactic halos in the same galaxy cluster or group as the quasar host galaxies.

We conclude that the AAL systems in these three quasars are likely to form in quasar-driven outflows, but their energies and potential for feedback to the host galaxies cannot be determined from existing data.

gmw_00013326.name: Q2044 168
gmw_00013326.overview: Q2044 168 is a quasar that hosts associated absorption line systems with velocity blueshifts consistent with outflows of 1900 to 3000 km/s [arXiv:1809.05433]. These absorbers are located at large distances of 40 to 330 kpc from the quasar, and they may form in quasar-driven outflows or intervening galactic halos [arXiv:1809.05433].

sources.summary: Q2044 168 is treated with two other AAL quasars; their absorbers show 1900-3000 km/s blueshifted outflows, no partial covering, large inferred distances, and uncertain feedback relevance.

quotes.quote: The AALs in the other three quasars in our sample, Q0105+061, Q0334 204, and Q2044 168, have velocity blueshifts consistent with outflows at speeds of 1900 to 3000 . These systems do not exhibit partial covering of the background light source, and the upper limits we derive on their densities, 150 to 15 , place the absorbers at large distances from the quasars, namely, 40 to 330 kpc. Thus the relationship of the AAL systems to the quasars is not known. It is possible that they form in intervening gas that is not physically related to the quasars, such as galactic halos in the same galaxy cluster or group as the quasar host galaxies.

We conclude that the AAL systems in these three quasars are likely to form in quasar-driven outflows, but their energies and potential for feedback to the host galaxies cannot be determined from existing data.

gmw_00013327.name: AT20G J091058-504807
gmw_00013327.overview: AT20G J091058-504807 is a radio source associated with an X-ray source [arXiv:1801.05502]. Its strong radio and X-ray emission, combined with a lack of optical detection, suggest it is an active galactic nucleus located behind the Galactic disk [arXiv:1801.05502].

sources.summary: The paper links AT20G J091058-504807 to the likely X-ray counterpart of Fermi J0910-5041 and says its X-ray/radio strength and optical non-detection suggest an AGN behind the Galactic disk.

quotes.quote: The X-ray source appears to be associated with the radio source AT20G J091058-504807 . No optical counterpart is found at the position of the X-ray or radio source. If Fermi J0910-5041 is associated with AT20G J091058-504807, the strong X-ray and radio emission and the lack of optical emission suggest that the gamma-ray source is an AGN serendipitously located behind the Galactic disk.

gmw_00013328.name: IGR J14043 6148
aliases_external: IGR J14043-6148 / XRT source near G311.45-0.13
gmw_00013328.overview: IGR J14043 6148 is a hard X-ray source that is classified as an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1203.0557]. Observations showing a decrease in its X-ray flux suggest that the source is variable, which rules out a supernova remnant origin [arXiv:1203.0557].
