quotes.quote: The soft X-ray counterpart to 1FHL J0110.0--4023 is associated with RBS0158 (also ATESP J010956--402051) a radio source that shows 20 and 36 cm flux densities of 57 and 36 mJy, respectively [ 8 ] . The source, which was optically classified as a BL Lac object by [ 9 ] , has a redshift of 0.313. It is also listed in the WISE catalogue [ 10 ] with colours W2-W3=1.84 and W1-W2=0.59 , which are well inside the blazar strip.

gmw_00013391.name: 6dF J1440378--384655
gmw_00013391.overview: 6dF J1440378--384655 is a galaxy classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1506.07006]. It is detected in the radio and X-ray bands, and its WISE colors locate the source outside the blazar strip [arXiv:1506.07006].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 6dF J1440378--384655 as the galaxy counterpart of 1FHL J1440.6--3847, reporting radio and X-ray fluxes, WISE colors, and a BL Lac classification.

quotes.quote: The X-ray counterpart to 1FHL J1440.6--3847 is unambiguously identified with the galaxy 6dF J1440378--384655, which is detected at 20 and 36 cm with flux densities of 22.8 and 23.2 mJy, respectively; the 0.2--12 keV flux is 7.9 10^ 12 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 . The WISE colours ( W2-W3=1.38 and W1-W2= 0.62 ) locate the source outside the blazar strip. 6dF J1440378--384655 is classified as a BL Lac in NED (see also [ 15 ] ), but on the basis of a poor quality optical spectrum.

gmw_00013392.name: 1FHL J2004.7 + 7003
aliases_external: soft X-ray/BL Lac counterpart of 1FHL J2004.7+7003
gmw_00013392.overview: 1FHL J2004.7 + 7003 is a variable source identified as a probable BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1506.07006]. It exhibits variability in both WISE and X-ray bands, with its infrared colors aligning with the blazar strip [arXiv:1506.07006].

sources.summary: The paper describes the 1FHL J2004.7+7003 counterpart as a radio, WISE, and X-ray variable source whose colors and prior studies support a probable BL Lac identification.

quotes.quote: The soft X-ray counterpart to 1FHL J2004.7 + 7003 is radio detected at 20 cm with a flux density of 6.5 mJy and listed in the WISE catalogue with colours W2-W3=2.21 and W1-W2=0.77 , i.e. fully compatible with the blazar strip. It is variable in both WISE ( W1 and W2 wavebands) and XMM-Newton Slew catalogues: the X-ray 0.2--12 keV flux ranges from 2.8 to 8.2 10^ 12 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 . This source was studied and discussed by various authors: all suggested that it is probably a BL Lac (see [ 16 ] ; [ 17 ] ; [ 18 ] ), as confirmed in the 3FGL catalogue.

gmw_00013393.name: NVSS J011904--145858
gmw_00013393.overview: NVSS J011904--145858 is a BL Lacertae object located at a redshift of 0.1147 [arXiv:1506.07006]. It is associated with a bright ROSAT X-ray source and has been detected in both radio and infrared wavelengths [arXiv:1506.07006].

sources.summary: The paper treats NVSS J011904--145858 as the radio association of the ROSAT source near 1FHL J0118.5--1502, with WISE detection and BL Lac classification at z=0.1147.

quotes.quote: The only X-ray source we found in the case of 1FHL J0118.5--1502 is a bright ROSAT source, which is located just outside the border of the Fermi error ellipse (left upper panel of Figure 1). Despite this, the source is within the positional uncertainty quoted for the 3FGL counterpart. The ROSAT source, which has the greatest error radius reported in Table 1, has a radio association in the NVSS (NVSS J011904--145858) with a 20 cm flux density of 5 mJy and is WISE-detected with colours W1-W2=0.521 and W2-W3=1.713 . The source was optically studied by [ 15 ] and found to display a spectrum with only absorption lines: it was therefore classified as a BL Lac at redshift 0.1147.

gmw_00013394.name: NVSS J082930+085821
gmw_00013394.overview: NVSS J082930+085821 is a radio-bright quasar located at a redshift of z=0.866 [arXiv:1506.07006]. It is classified as a flat-spectrum radio object, though its WISE colors place it outside the typical blazar strip [arXiv:1506.07006].

sources.summary: The paper selects NVSS J082930+085821 as the promising counterpart for 1FHL J0828.9+0902, identifying it with a radio-bright QSO and flat-spectrum radio object despite non-blazar WISE colors.

quotes.quote: The remaining object (source 2) coincides with the radio source NVSS J082930+085821 (also TXS 0826+091), which displays a 20 cm flux density of 333.9 mJy. This X-ray detection is identified with a QSO at z=0.866 in NED. It was also classified as a flat spectrum radio object by [ 20 ] , but its WISE colours ( W2-W3=3.08 and W1-W2=0.66 ) are outside the blazar strip. Source 2 is also the association reported in the 3FGL catalogue and it looks like the most promising one at the moment.

gmw_00013395.name: 1FHL J0841.4--3558
aliases_external: BL Lac XRT counterpart of 1FHL J0841.2-3556 / J0841.4-3558
gmw_00013395.overview: 1FHL J0841.4--3558 is a gamma-ray blazar located at a redshift of z > 0.15 [arXiv:1506.07006]. Optical spectroscopy of the source reveals a featureless spectrum, leading to its classification as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1506.07006].

sources.summary: The paper favors a non-stellar X-ray/radio source in the 1FHL J0841.4--3558 field as a blazar counterpart, citing WISE colors, a featureless BL Lac spectrum, and z>0.15.

quotes.quote: The error box of 1FHL J0841.4--3558 contains two X-ray sources, as evident in the left lower panel of Figure 1: one is a bright ROSAT /XRT object (R.A.(J2000) = 08^ h 41^ m 21^ .40 and Dec.(J2000) = 35^ 57^ 04^ .50 , 9 arcsec error radius) associated with the star 2MASS J08412132--3557154 (also HIP 42640) of spectral type F2V, which is unlikely to emit gamma-rays. The other, reported in Table 1, is located only 1.3 arcmin north of the star; it is listed as a radio source in various catalogues and has WISE colours W2-W3=2.328 and W1-W2=0.863 , which locate the source in the locus of gamma-ray blazars. The optical spectrum obtained recently by [ 14 ] is featureless and the source was classified as a BL Lac at z>0.15 . Note that this source is still listed as an unclassified blazar in the third Fermi /LAT catalogue.

gmw_00013396.name: RBS 1350
gmw_00013396.overview: RBS 1350 is classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1506.07006]. It has been suggested to be an extreme high-energy-peaked blazar or a TeV candidate, with WISE colors that are compatible with the blazar strip [arXiv:1506.07006].

sources.summary: The paper associates RBS 1350 with the 1FHL J1406.4+164 field, describing it as a BL Lac, possible extreme high-energy-peaked blazar or TeV candidate, with compatible WISE colors.

quotes.quote: In the case of 1FHL J1406.4+164, the only X-ray detection is just 3 arcmin outside the Fermi 1FHL error ellipse, but it is located at the border of the positional uncertainty of 3FGL J1406.6+1644 (right lower panel of Figure 1). This X-ray object is associated with RBS 1350, which is classified as a BL Lac object and suggested to be an extreme high-energy peaked blazar or a TeV candidate (see [ 21 ] ; [ 22 ] ). The source redshift has a lower limit of 0.623 and a photometric value of 1.985. In radio, the source has a 20 cm flux density around 78 mJy, while its WISE colours ( W2-W3=2.362 and W1-W2=0.574 ) are compatible with the blazar strip. Given the overall properties RBS 1350 and the overlap in positional uncertainties between the 1FHL and the 3FGL sources, we regard the association proposed here as likely, although not certain.

gmw_00013397.name: DLA at z_ abs = 2.05 towards the quasar FBQS J2340-0053
aliases_local: DLA at z_ abs = 2.05 towards the quasar FBQS J2340-0053
aliases_external: DLA at z_abs=2.05 toward FBQS J2340-0053 sightline
gmw_00013397.overview: The damped Lyman-alpha absorber at z_abs = 2.05 towards the quasar FBQS J2340-0053 is a high-redshift galaxy system [arXiv:1808.07906]. It has a metallicity of 0.3 solar metallicity, a dust-to-gas ratio of 0.34, and contains atomic, molecular, and HD hydrogen species [arXiv:1808.07906].

sources.original_name: DLA at z_ abs = 2.05 towards the quasar FBQS J2340-0053
sources.summary: The paper models the z_abs=2.05 DLA toward FBQS J2340-0053, measuring H I, H2, HD, metallicity, dust content, radiation field, and the physical structure of its molecular components.

quotes.quote: We perform detailed spectroscopic analysis and numerical modelling of an H _ 2 bearing damped Lyman- absorber (DLA) at z_ abs = 2.05 towards the quasar FBQS J2340-0053. Metal absorption features arise from fourteen components spread over v_ 90 = 114 km s ^ 1 , seven of which harbour H _ 2 . Column densities of atomic and molecular species are derived through Voigt profile analysis of their absorption lines. We measure total N ( H i ), N (H _ 2 ) and N (HD) to be 20.35 0.05, 17.99 0.05 and 14.28 0.08 (log cm ^ 2 ) respectively.

The DLA has metallicity, Z = 0.3 Z_ ( [ S/H ] = -0.52 0.06) and dust-to-gas ratio, = 0.34 0.07. Numerical models of the H _ 2 components are constrained individually to understand the physical structure of the DLA. We conclude that the DLA is subjected to the metagalactic background radiation and cosmic ray ionization rate of 10 ^ 15.37 s ^ 1 . Dust grains in this DLA are smaller than grains in the Galactic interstellar medium.

gmw_00013398.name: 2MASXJ12224762+0536247
gmw_00013398.overview: 2MASXJ12224762+0536247 is a spiral galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 0.018 [arXiv:1712.03370]. The galaxy features narrow hydrogen and doubly ionized oxygen emission lines, and it hosted the supernova SN 2012ab near its nucleus [arXiv:1712.03370].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 2MASXJ12224762+0536247 as the spiral host of SN 2012ab, gives its redshift, and uses late-time spectroscopy to assess host-galaxy light contamination in the SN spectra.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: the location of the host galaxy is measured to be (J2000) = 12^ h 22^ m 47^ s .64 , (J2000) = +05 36^ 24^ .41 0.02^

quotes.quote: SN 2012ab was discovered coincident with the nucleus of the spiral galaxy 2MASXJ12224762+0536247 (redshift z=0.018 ) as a part of the Robotic Optical Transient Search Experiment (ROTSE) at an unfiltered apparent magnitude of 15.8 ( M = -19.0 mag) on 2012 January 31.35 (UT dates are used throughout this paper) with the 0.45-m ROTSE-IIIb telescope at the McDonald Observatory 2012CBET.3022....1V .

A spectrum of SN 2012ab acquired with the 9.2-m Hobberly Eberly Telescope/Marcario Low-Resolution Spectrograph on February 7.34 revealed a hot, blue continuum with narrow H and [ O iii ] emission lines from the host galaxy 2012CBET.3022....1V . The redshift calculated from the narrow features in the SN agrees with the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) galaxy redshift of 0.018.

Since SN 2012ab was found near its host galaxy's nucleus, we took a very late-time spectrum on day 1206 in order to sample the likely contamination of host-galaxy light in all of the spectra. We would have directly subtracted the host-galaxy light seen on day 1206 from all previous epochs of spectra, but the absolute flux calibration of the spectra is uncertain owing to slit losses, variable seeing and slit sizes, and in some cases cloud cover. Consequently, we use the day 1206 spectrum as a qualitative template for the host-galaxy light.

gmw_00013399.name: GRB970228
gmw_00013399.overview: The host galaxy of GRB970228 is one of the first two identified gamma-ray burst host galaxies [arXiv:astro-ph/9912454]. It is also characterized as one of the least luminous host galaxies in its sample [arXiv:astro-ph/9912454].

sources.summary: The paper describes the host associated with GRB970228 as one of the first two identified GRB hosts and among the least luminous hosts in the sample, making earlier subluminous-host arguments coincidental.

quotes.quote: The first argument is that GRB970228 and GRB970508 (the first two identified GRB hosts) are in the bottom 1 of the luminosity weighted Schechter luminosity function, with this result being unlikely unless the GRB hosts are systematically subluminous.

The first two discovered GRB hosts (for GRB970228 and GRB970508) are galaxies at the bottom of the luminosity function. But now we have data for hosts on sixteen GRBs with optical transients or radio transients (OT/RT) to provide arc-second positions and ten of these have measured red shifts (see Table 1). This much larger sample can answer the question of ``What is the luminosity function for the host galaxies of faint bursts?'' An approximate answer to this question can be obtained by merely examining the derived absolute magnitudes of the hosts as taken from Table 1. We see that the first two GRB hosts are fortuitously the least luminous hosts by about one magnitude.
This means that the early argument for subluminous hosts based on GRB970228 and GRB970508 is wrong due to a rather unlikely coincidence.

gmw_00013400.name: GRB970508
gmw_00013400.overview: The galaxy associated with GRB970508 was one of the first two identified gamma-ray burst host galaxies [arXiv:astro-ph/9912454]. It is characterized as a subluminous galaxy that lies at the bottom of the luminosity function [arXiv:astro-ph/9912454].

sources.summary: The paper describes the host associated with GRB970508 as one of the first two identified GRB hosts and among the least luminous hosts in the sample, making earlier subluminous-host arguments coincidental.

gmw_00013401.name: host of GRB 070707
gmw_00013401.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 070707 is an intrinsically faint galaxy with an optical magnitude of R ~ 27.3 [arXiv:0807.1348]. It has a low luminosity of less than one-sixth of the Schechter luminosity at any redshift, and near-infrared observations rule out a bright, red host [arXiv:0807.1348].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the GRB 070707 host as extremely faint, with R 27.3, low luminosity at allowed redshifts, and no evidence for a bright red host.

quotes.quote: Whichever its redshift, the host of GRB 070707 ( R 27.3 ) is the faintest ever detected for a short GRB, with a magnitude comparable to that of long GRB hosts at high redshift (e.g. Wain07,Fruchter06 ).

The sparse data on the host galaxy of GRB 070707 do not allow for a detailed analysis. At the maximum allowed redshift z = 3.6 , the host would have an absolute magnitude M_ AB = -18.6 (at rest-frame wavelength 1500   ). This is more that 2 magnitudes fainter than the Schechter luminosity at this redshift ( M^*_ AB = -20.7 ; Gabasch04 ). We can thus firmly set L < (1/6)L_* at any redshift, implying that the host was intrinsically faint. Our late-time NIR upper limit ( K > 22.7 ) implies R - K < 4.3 and hence can rule out a bright, red host, such as an extremely red object (ERO) or a moderate-redshift elliptical.

gmw_00013402.name: FBQS1641+3058
gmw_00013402.overview: FBQS1641+3058 is a broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:1007.0028]. It exhibits high continuum and white-light polarization, with elevated polarization levels observed in its Si IV and Mg II absorption troughs [arXiv:1007.0028].

sources.summary: The paper identifies FBQS1641+3058 as the sample's most polarized BALQSO, with high continuum and white-light polarization and stronger polarization in Si IV and Mg II absorption troughs.

quotes.quote: FBQS1641+3058 This BALQSO is the most polarized object in our sample with P=7.77 from 6200-6500 \ (observed frame) and 7.79 in white light. While the flux in the C 4 trough bottoms is so low it is hard to analyze the polarization level well there, binning across the whole absorption feature does not indicate any significant rise in polarization. The polarization in the Al 3 absorption line also seems consistent with the continuum.

However there does appear to be a large rise in the Si 4 troughs, with a value averaged over all of the features of almost 9 . Although close to the red end of the spectrum where contamination from second-order light may be an issue, the polarization in the Mg 2 BAL seems to rise over 9 . The discrepancy between polarization levels in the C 4 absorption troughs and others could be an indication of a higher C 4 column density along the polarized line of sight, something not typically seen in these objects.

gmw_00013403.name: FBQS0946+2744
gmw_00013403.overview: FBQS0946+2744 is a broad-absorption line quasar that is likely unpolarized or only very weakly polarized [arXiv:1007.0028]. Its spectrum is characterized by the absence of any strong emission features despite the presence of obvious absorption [arXiv:1007.0028].

sources.summary: The paper describes FBQS0946+2744 as likely unpolarized or weakly polarized, with possible small intrinsic polarization near N V and Lyman alpha absorption and an unusual spectrum.

quotes.quote: FBQS0946+2744 FBQS0946+2744 is likely unpolarized, or only very weakly polarized. In the wavelength range 5770-6070 \ (observed frame) we measure P=0.18 ; however, the insterstellar polarization along this line of sight can reach as high as 0.22 . Therefore, the polarization is not likely to be intrinsic.

The polarization however may rise to around 0.4 in the regions around the N 5 and Lyman alpha absorption, and there may be some indication of a rotation in the position angle in that region. This could indicate that there is a small level of intrinsic polarization in this BALQSO, but these observations are not extremely convincing. The spectrum is also slightly odd, with the absense of any strong emission features even though there is obvious absorption present.

gmw_00013404.name: PKS 2355-106
gmw_00013404.overview: PKS 2355-106 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar classified as a blazar [arXiv:2207.01807]. It exhibits a compact radio core and displays substantial variability in both the optical and 15 GHz radio bands [arXiv:2207.01807].

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 2355-106 as a blazar/flat-spectrum radio quasar with variable radio and optical emission, a relativistic jet, compact VLBI structure, and nearly co-spatial optical and radio sightlines.

quotes.quote: As part of MALS science verification, we observed the quasar PKS 2355-106 (hereafter, 23).

The quasar 23 \ is classified as a blazar of flat spectrum radio quasar (FSRQ) type with detectable ray emission Ackermann2015 . The observed synchrotron peak frequency ( _p = 12.651 ) is consistent with the object being a low frequency synchrotron peaker. While the quasar is classified as non-variable in rays, it shows substantial variability at 15 GHz Richards2014 and optical bands Hovatta2014 .

The VLBA observations at 1.4 GHz Gupta12 and 605 MHz, i.e., the redshifted frequency of the \ 21-cm absorption Ellison2012 revealed 23 \ to be compact with almost all the emission being associated with an unresolved component. VLBI space observatory program (VSOP) data of 23 \ obtained at 5 GHz suggests a compact core component of 0.2 Jy within 0.2 mas Dodson2008 . Recently, Petrov2017 have reported an off-set of -0.89 mas along RA and -0.40 mas along DEC between VLBA and Gaia coordinates with a probability of 0.29 for this offset to occur by chance. This suggests that the optical and radio sightlines towards 23 \ are most likely co-spatial.

gmw_00013405.name: SDSS J220233.85 073225.0
gmw_00013405.overview: SDSS J220233.85 073225.0 is a galaxy that exhibits a double-lobed extended morphology at 1.4 GHz [arXiv:1601.00054]. Its southeast radio lobe is associated with the optical position, suggesting a possible core-jet structure [arXiv:1601.00054].

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J220233.85 073225.0 as having double-lobed 1.4 GHz radio morphology, with the southeast lobe associated with the optical position and a possible core-jet structure.

quotes.quote: SDSS J220233.85 073225.0 -- This source has a double-lobed extended morphology at 1.4 GHz. It is the southeast radio lobe, which is associated with the optical position. Hence, it is possible that radio core is slightly resolved showing a core--jet morphology.
